4.6.3 Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Category
Now that we’ve covered the basics of the process instruction category, let’s create a self-
defined process instruction category of type 0 as an example. In Chapter 7, we’ll show
you how to use this self-defined process instruction category, PP20.
To create a new process instruction category, use menu path SAP IMG  Production
Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Instructions  Define
Process Instruction Categories (General) or use Transaction O12C.
On the initial screen that appears, enter “1710” in the Plant field and choose Continue to
open the screen shown in Figure 4.25.
The screen that appears shows the list of available process instruction categories for
Plant 1710. Notice the ProcInstr Type field; if you place your cursor on this field and
press (F4), the system will show a dropdown list of process instruction types. These are
the same process instructions that we covered in Section 4.6.1. Choose New Entries and
the screen shown in Figure 4.25 will appear.
On the initial screen that appears, define process instruction category PP20 as type 0
(universal) and give a short Description. Next, select Process instruction categories and
double-click the Characteristics/Process instruction category field on the left-hand side
of the screen to enter specific process instruction characteristics. For this example,
enter the characteristics as shown in Table 4.6.
158© 

159
4.6 Process Instruction Categories
Figure 4.25 Overview Screen of Process Instruction Category PP20
Characteristics Characteristic Values
PPPI_DATA_REQUEST_TYPE Simple Data Request
PPPI_MESSAGE_CATEGORY PP20
ZPI_CREATION_DATE
PPPI_OUTPUT_TEXT P.O. Basic Start Date
PPPI_OUTPUT_CHARACTERISTIC ZPI_CREATION_DATE
PPPI_PROCESS_ORDER
PPPI_CONTROL_RECIPE
Table 4.6 Self-Defined Process Instruction Category PP20
Table 4.6 consolidates the configuration made so far. It incorporates process mes-
sage category PP20 as well as the self-defined process instruction characteristic ZPI_
CREATION_DATE. See Section 4.6.4 to create a self-defined process instruction characteris-
tic. Also note that the system automatically fills in the characteristic values that are
blank in the preceding table.
4.6.4 Creating a Self-Defined Process Instruction Characteristic
Although the SAP system offers many standard process instruction (and process mes-
sage) characteristics, unique business processes or business needs often require custom-
defined or self-defined characteristics.

For our example, you create a simple self-defined process instruction characteristic
that automatically fetches the process order basic start date when the user creates the
process order. Use this characteristic to display this information (process order end
date) on the PI sheet. Although this example uses automatic value assignment—that is,
the system automatically fills in the date field—you can also use it to ask the process
operator to send the requisite information in the form of a process message. You can
also use process instruction characteristics to define the RFC destination to which a
production supervisor wants the process operator to send process instructions for an
external control system.
To create a new process instruction characteristic, follow menu path SAP IMG  Produc-
tion Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Process Instructions 
Define Characteristics for Process Instructions  Create Characteristics for Process
Instructions or use Transaction O25C.
Figure 4.26 displays the initial screen you use to create the process instruction charac-
teristic. Notice the similarities between creating a standard characteristic (in Transac-
tion CT04) and creating a process instruction characteristic.
For this example, you create ZPI_CREATION_DATE and click the Basic data tab. In this
tab, it’s important to ensure that the Char. Group (characteristics group) is defined as a
process instruction characteristic (PPPI_02). This is the same characteristic group that
we covered in Section 4.4.2.
Then, define the Data Type as DATE Date Format and click the Proc. mgmt icon. The sys-
tem will then display a popup that allows you to incorporate additional process
instructions and process message details. If you place the cursor on the Table field and
press (F4), or if you click the dropdown option, then the dialog box shown in Figure
4.27 will appear. This screen provides you with the option to select any of the five tables
available: Process Order (Header), Operation, Phase, Resource, and Material (Header).
The system will display the fields that are associated with the table you select.
160
4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
Example
The process operator wants to refer to specific information for a process order or its
operations in the PI sheet, and this isn’t available in the standard offering. Similarly,
there are some standard fields for which a production supervisor wants the process
operator to send back the requisite information through process messages. The option
to self-define the process instruction characteristic offers the flexibility to choose from
a large number of fields in the production process and use them to bring together dif-
ferent information on the PI sheet.
© 

161
4.6 Process Instruction Categories
Figure 4.26 Creating Process Instruction Characteristic
Figure 4.27 Automatic Value Assignment for Process Order Tables

4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
162
For this example, select 01 Process Order (Header) and the system will bring back the
previous screen. This time, place the cursor in the Field field and again press (F4) to see
a limited selection of fields that are available in the process order table. Select GSTRP,
which is the technical name of the process order start date (see Figure 4.28).
Figure 4.28 Automatic Value Assignment of Basic Start Date
Apart from entering the Table and the Field, you should check the Only automatic box
to enable the system to automatically fill in the requisite information in the process
instruction characteristic and save it. Then, when the user creates the process order and
the system assigns the basic start and finish dates of the process order, the system will
fetch the basic start date of the process order and make it an integral part of the PI sheet.
Tips and Tricks
If a business process involves the creation of a simple process instruction characteris-
tic, then use Transaction CT04 (which doesn’t have a process management option) to
create characteristics and ensure selection of the correct characteristic group.
4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets
There are four types of control recipe destinations. Two of them are used when transfer-
ring information within the SAP S/4HANA system, and the other two facilitate the trans-
fer of information to external systems. The system creates a control recipe when a pro-
cess order is released, and this can be automatic (controlled in order type−dependent
parameters; Section 4.2) or generated manually. Creating the control recipe and then
sending the PI sheet to its destination enables the process operator to begin recording
important plant parameters.
Table 4.7 provides the necessary details for the different types of control recipes.
Note
Although they’re still available in SAP S/4HANA 2023, ABAP list−based PI sheets are
already obsolete and may not be available in future releases of SAP S/4HANA.
© 

163
4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets
If the control recipe destination is of either type 1 or type 4, then the system sends the
recipes to different destinations within the SAP system. The system creates a control
recipe, either driven by the user (manually) or automatically, and consolidates the pro-
cess instructions for a given order and destination in the form of the PI sheet. The PI
sheet contains user-defined details—for example, process notes, process steps, input
fields, calculations, options to sign the phases of the PI sheet, details to enter GI (con-
sumption), and navigation to the confirmation screen or to record in-process quality
results. When the process operator sets the status of the PI sheet to Complete, the sys-
tem sends the data back to the SAP system in the form of process messages. The system
then uploads the information from the process messages into the SAP S/4HANA system
tables using function modules and BAPIs. If the control recipe destination is of either
type 2 or type 3, then the system consolidates the process instructions in the control rec-
ipe of the process order and sends them to an external process control system.
An internal SAP S/4HANA system destination can be any logical or virtual destination
where the process operator receives the PI sheet and fills in the data. For example, inter-
nal destinations could include a mixing unit, packing unit, blending section, ammonia
section, urea section, or quality section. For external destinations—that is, external pro-
cess control systems—these are the RFC destinations of the external system.
It’s also possible to integrate a digital signature using an SAP Universal ID and pass-
word. The following options are available for use with digital signatures:
 Activating or deactivating the PI sheet
 Locking or unlocking the PI sheet
 Changing the status of the PI sheet to Complete
 Entering each parameter value into the PI sheet
Digital signature options are also available in case a parameter value deviates from pre-
defined limits, as well as for completion of each phase in the PI sheet. If there’s a busi-
ness need to automatically generate process instructions to minimize maintaining
them in the master recipe, then you can achieve this when creating the control recipe
destination. See Section 4.7.2 for information on the scope of generation. In the follow-
ing sections, we’ll explain how to set up a control recipe destination and how to make
the necessary settings for the scope of generation for process instructions.
Control Recipe Destination Types Destination Locations Output Types
1 SAP S/4HANA system ABAP list−based PI sheet
2 External control system External control system
3 External control system External system
4 SAP system Browser-based PI sheet
Table 4.7 Control Recipe Destination Types

4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
164
4.7.1 Creating a Control Recipe Destination
To create a new control recipe destination, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production
Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Control Recipe/PI Sheets  Con-
trol Recipe Destinations  Define and Set Up Control Recipe Destinations.
On the screen shown in Figure 4.29, create a new control recipe destination PP of Type
4 (a browser-based PI sheet). Give a short description and set the Destination address to
Chemical Mixing. Check the SortMat box to enable the system to display materials in
the same order in which they appear in the master recipe or in the process order. If you
place the cursor on the Type field and press (F4), or if you use the dropdown option,
then the system will display the four different types of control recipe destinations that
are available to choose from. (This is the same as in Table 4.7.)
Figure 4.29 Control Recipe Destination: Overview
Figure 4.30 Maintaining Control Recipe Destination
© 

165
4.7 Control Recipe/Process Instruction Sheets
Figure 4.30 shows the details screen where you create the control recipe destination,
and it also shows the options for incorporating digital signatures in some or all of the
processes.
4.7.2 Scope of Generation
In some situations, the process operator enters the same data, but you can use scope
data to automate the entry of this data so that it’s the same every time. This is helpful
when you want to do the following:
 Confirm the quantity produced or activities consumed at the end of each phase.
 Enter shift details at the end of every phase or operation.
 Ensure that each master recipe created for a specific destination has standard
instructions and notes, which then become visible on the PI sheet.
Figure 4.31 shows the New Entries: Overview of Added Entries screen that appears when
you’re creating control recipe destination 10 for Plant 1710 and when you choose Pro-
cess instructions to be generated on the left-hand side.
Figure 4.31 Process Instructions to Be Generated during Control Recipe Creation
Place the cursor on the Position field and press (F4) and the dialog box will appear.
Here, you can define where in the control recipe you want to place the process instruc-
tion category. It can be at the start or end of a control recipe, an operation, or a phase.
Once you’ve defined a position, the next step is to assign a process instruction cate-
gory, place the cursor on the Gen.scope field, and press (F4). This opens the screen
shown in Figure 4.31, where you can use the Scope of generation to enable the system
to generate process instructions based on the details you provide. For example, you

4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
166
can select the 01 option for the GI process instruction category. When the system cre-
ates a process order and incorporates the materials list based on the BOM, it simultane-
ously creates a reservation with item numbers. To record consumption (GI) against the
process order using the PI sheet, select the relevant process instruction category (CH_
CONS or CH_CONS2) and define the scope of generation as 01 (For all reservations).
For this example, assign the instruction category SIGN, which the system will generate
once at the end of the PI sheet (refer to Figure 4.31).
Tips and Tricks
When creating the process instruction category SIGN, make sure to assign a character-
istic value (e.g., PI Completion Signoff) to the process instruction characteristic PPPI_
INPUT_VALUE.
4.8 Background Jobs
Background jobs help to automate processes that otherwise require manual interven-
tion. For example, when a user creates a process order and generates its control recipe,
the user will have to manually send it to the control recipe destination. The system can
be configured to automatically send the control recipe as soon as the system generates
it or even periodically. Similarly, sending back process messages to the SAP S/4HANA
system can be a manual effort that users can manage as a background (automatic) job.
Apart from sending control recipes and process messages, it’s also possible to delete
them periodically to save precious system resources. For example, there may not be
any need to preserve process messages older than six months, in which case, those can
be deleted.
In this section, we show you how to set up a background job for sending a control rec-
ipe. The same steps apply to sending process messages and deleting process messages
as background jobs.
4.8.1 Sending Control Recipes
To set up the background job for sending a control recipe as soon as the system creates
it, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production Planning for Process Industries  Process
Management  Control Recipe/PI Sheet  Define Background Job for Sending or use
Transaction SM36. Figure 4.32 shows the initial Define Background Job screen to define
the background job. Enter “ZPP_PI_SEND” in the Job Name field and press (Enter).
On the next screen, enter “SAP_NEW_CONTROL_RECIPES” in the Event field, which
causes the system to send the control recipe as soon as the system creates it. If you click
the Start Condition button, you can define the schedule for sending control recipes to
© 

167
4.8 Background Jobs
various destinations. You can also assign the program name RCOCB006 to set up the
background job.
Figure 4.32 Defining Background Job to Send Control Recipe
Tips and Tricks
Use the Job Wizard button to enable the system to provide guidance in a step-by-step
process to set up a background job. This works not just for sending a control recipe to a
destination but for any background job. It’s important to know the program name for
which to define the background job.
4.8.2 Sending Process Messages
In Transaction SM36, you can use program RCOCB004 to send newly created process
messages as a background job. The system also processes the process messages that
have statuses of To Be Submitted and To Be Submitted with Warning. This program is
for sending process messages within your plant. If you need to send process messages
to different plants, then use program RCOCB002. The corresponding event is SAP_NEW_
PROCESS_MESSAGES.
Tips and Tricks
Whenever you define a background job by using Transaction SM36, you need to assign
a variant to it. Use Transaction SE38 to create a new variant for any background job
(and its associated program). On the initial screen, enter the program name, click the
Variants radio button, and then click the Change button. On the next screen, give the
variant name and click Create.
4.8.3 Deleting Process Messages
The background program also lets you delete process messages as a background job.
Use Transaction SM36 to set up the background job.

4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
168
4.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit
The process manufacturing cockpit finds diverse applications in areas where the activ-
ities performed, actions taken, or results recorded don’t reference any specific process
order. In the cockpit, it’s possible to use function calls—for example, to call up a trans-
action or display a document. You can also create a cockpit for a predefined set of activ-
ities that a process operator performs on a specific resource or in a specific work center.
The cockpit also facilitates input or output data entry as well as calculations. The sys-
tem creates a PI sheet with reference to a process order, and the system reflects any
changes or updates made to it. In a cockpit, there’s no need to give reference to a pro-
cess order. Here are some examples in which creating process manufacturing cockpits
meets business needs:
 There’s a need to record the values of five specific process parameters of the plant
and report them back to the main control room. The reporting for the parameters
doesn’t reference a specific process order but is used to ensure that there is regular
recording and consistent transmission of this information.
 The production process generates an ancillary product (a by-product of low value),
but the production supervisor doesn’t want to record it with reference to any spe-
cific process order or book the cost of generating the by-product to a process order.
There’s also a need to record the quantity of the by-product every three days, along
with other details, such as the date of recording, the name of the person who
recorded the information, and that person’s signature.
 A company has installed a new production line for which it wants to ensure that the
latest technical drawings and manuals are available for process operators to refer to.
These drawings are for each work center or work station. In the process manufactur-
ing cockpit, the process operator only has to click the relevant drawing’s icon, and all
details will become visible.
You can create process manufacturing cockpits for as many business applications or
activities as needed. Because the cockpit uses a process instruction category, it’s also
possible to incorporate a process message category into it. When you make entries are
into the cockpit, the system generates process messages that you can send to pre-
defined destinations.
To define a process manufacturing cockpit, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production
Planning for Process Industries  Process Management  Define Process Manufacturing
Cockpits (see Figure 4.33).
Here, you define Cockpit PP10 for Plant 1710. Notice that you can check a newly created
cockpit for correctness by choosing the Check icon, and it you can simulate it by choos-
ing Simulate at the bottom of the screen. Finally, you need to activate the process man-
ufacturing cockpit so that it becomes available and changes you make to the cockpit
become effective. To activate the cockpit, choose Activate.
© 

169
4.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit
Figure 4.33 Process Manufacturing Cockpit
For this example, check the box for Cockpit PP10 and double-click Assign Process
Instructions to Cockpits. This brings up the screen in Figure 4.34, where two different
process instruction categories are maintained. The system only allows process instruc-
tion categories of type 0 (universal). The Line no. column defines the sequence in which
the process instruction categories will appear in the process manufacturing cockpit.
You should then create a new process instruction category with reference to the exist-
ing ones and assign it type 0.
Checking the Ref (reference) box ensures that the system will automatically reflect any
changes made to the specific process instruction category.
Figure 4.34 Assigning Process Instruction Categories to Cockpits

4 Process Manufacturing Configuration
170
Tips and Tricks
A quick and easy way to use the desired process instruction category in the process
manufacturing cockpit is to copy the original process instruction category as a refer-
ence and define it as type 0 (universal) during the copy function. In this example, the
process instruction category CONS_PI is used as a reference to create a new process
instruction category, CONSPP, which is defined as type 0 (universal).
In Chapter 7, we’ll show the outcome and the output of the PP10 process manufactur-
ing cockpit created in this chapter.
4.10 Summary
Most of the configuration settings that are applicable in discrete manufacturing also
apply to process manufacturing. However, the process management area in PP for pro-
cess industries requires detailed coverage. The concepts and fundamentals covered in
this chapter apply equally whether a company plans to implement XSteps in discrete
manufacturing or use process manufacturing cockpits.
The next chapter covers the configuration basics of the last main manufacturing type
we’ll discuss in this book: repetitive manufacturing.
©

chapters.title: Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
chapters.text:
171
Chapter 5
Repetitive Manufacturing
Configuration
Configuration steps in repetitive manufacturing include creating a
repetitive manufacturing profile, setting scheduling parameters for a
run schedule quantity (planned orders), and selecting backflush settings
for use online or in the background.
The configuration steps in repetitive manufacturing (REM) are relatively straightfor-
ward, without many complexities when compared to discrete manufacturing or pro-
cess manufacturing types. In fact, the very purpose of REM is to enable lean manufac-
turing in actual business scenarios with correspondingly fewer entries in the SAP
S/4HANA system. REM may not be able to manage complex manufacturing processes,
but you can use it to bring about significant process optimization with a decreased data
entry workload and improved system performance.
This chapter begins by explaining how to set up the REM profile using the REM assis-
tant. The assistant guides the user with a step-by-step approach to ensure that the cor-
rect settings are made during the REM profile creation stage. The assistant also pro-
vides the detailed function of each configuration step and gives recommendations
where necessary. The user can choose the desired production method, such as MTS or
MTO. We deliberately took the long route to REM profile creation by using the assistant
because we wanted to focus on explaining every available option in detail and with
business examples.
After we finish creating the REM profile by using the assistant, we explain the “normal”
or prevalent method of creating an REM profile with a single screen (and multiple tabs).
We also explain the options that aren’t available in the REM profile assistant. If you’re
configuring an REM profile for the first time, we recommend that you to use the REM
profile assistant. But if you’re already familiar with REM profile creation and the func-
tionalities it offers, then proceed directly to using the single-screen REM profile cre-
ation option.
This chapter also covers scheduling REM planned orders and running the schedule
quantity, and then, it discusses the layout and display settings available on various
screens in REM processing. The effects of the REM configuration will become clear in
Chapter 8, which covers the production planning for REM in detail. Finally, this chapter

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
172
also covers the settings available to optimize day-to-day business transactions, such as
backflushing.
Note
Refer to Chapter 1 for feature comparison among discrete manufacturing, process
manufacturing, and REM, as well as to see how to decide which manufacturing type is
most relevant for a given industry.
Let’s get started.
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
The REM profile offers several controls for important business functions that form the
basis of regular or periodic data recording in the system. This includes options to post
activities online or at a later date, automatic goods movements of all or some of the
materials, and stock and/or automatic batch determination, among other things. To
create an REM profile using the REM profile assistant, follow SAP menu path SAP IMG 
Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Control  Create Repetitive Manufac-
turing Profile Using Assistant or use Transaction OSPT.
The following sections will explain the steps involved in setting up an REM profile.
5.1.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Production Type
When creating a RE M profile, you need to decide whether to use the profile for the MTO
or MTS production method. Alternately, you can cater separate REM profiles to meet
MTO or MTS business scenarios.
Note
Chapter 12 covers MTO and MTS production methods.
Depending on the option you select, the system will display the relevant screens and
options to choose from. If you choose MTO production, then the system will create a
profile suited to REM that references the sales order. If you choose MTS production, the
system will create a profile suited to REM that doesn’t reference the sales order.
It’s also possible to use an REM profile template to copy previously created and avail-
able REM profiles to create a new REM profile. This step copies all the settings of the
REM template profile into the new REM profile, and then, you can make the desired
changes as needed.
© 

173
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
Tips and Tricks
Click the Meaning icon on each REM profile creation screen to get information on the
underlying concept for that functionality or area.
Click the Recommendation icon on each REM profile creation screen to get system
advice on recommended settings.
For this example, you create an REM profile for an MTS production method. Figure 5.1
shows the initial screen where you create an REM profile using the assistant. For this
example, click the Make-to-stock - REM radio button and then click Next.
Figure 5.1 Initial Screen of Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Assistant
5.1.2 Reporting Points
A reporting point enables the system to record consumption or other details, such as
identifying the work in process (WIP) and better stock visibility in inventory manage-
ment (IM), which are specific to actual information in time and equally helpful in report-
ing. As shown in Figure 5.2, you need to select whether the system should include no
reporting points or make the reporting points mandatory or optional. A reporting point
serves the same purpose as a milestone does in routing for discrete manufacturing. If

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
174
you select the Mandatory Reporting Points option, then you need to define a reporting
point at the time of assembly or component backflush.
Figure 5.2 Reporting Points
You can use reporting points in one of the following situations:
 If you select the mandatory reporting points option, then a backflush must take
place at every reporting point. It makes sense to backflush at reporting points if the
GI is to be posted as close as possible to the actual issuance of the materials or if WIP
is to be calculated.
 If you select the optional reporting points option, then it entails carrying out the
normal final backflush and using a reporting point backflush only in certain situa-
tions. With optional reporting points, you use the standard GR posting procedures
in REM for backflushing and then only backflush at reporting points for special
purposes—for example, when backflushing scrap or calculating WIP. The optional
reporting point makes sense when, for example, there’s usually no business need for
a reporting point at all, but due to technical issues in the production line, the pro-
duction supervisor wants to evaluate extra material issuance or extra scrap genera-
tion.
For this example, choose Mandatory reporting points and then click Next.
5.1.3 Automatic Goods Movements
The automatic goods movement option applies to reporting point backflush only.
Without reporting points, you can post the GR on final confirmation. On the screen
shown in Figure 5.3, you choose whether the system should perform automatic GR at
the time of assembly backflush. When assembly backflush is recorded and the yield is
confirmed, the system automatically performs the GR at the last reporting point.
When you’re using optional reporting points, choose the Automatic GR setting because
usually, the components are backflushed along with the GR posting.
When you’re using mandatory reporting points, the production supervisor can only
backflush at reporting points. The normal GR posting isn’t available here, so for man-
datory reporting points, we recommend that you define the last workplace on the
© 

175
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
production line as a reporting point with the Automatic GR setting. If you choose the
Automatic GR option, the production supervisor has to post activities and GIs manu-
ally for operations that occur after the last reporting point. This is an exception to the
preceding recommendation.
Figure 5.3 Automatic GR
For this example, choose Automatic GR and then choose Next.
Note
While you’re configuring the REM profile using the assistant, the system displays either
the screen shown in Figure 5.3 or the screen shown in Figure 5.4. Which one it shows
depends on the option you chose while creating an REM profile.
5.1.4 Reporting Points: Confirmations and Kanban
Depending on the system release, you select whether to use reporting points with or
without Kanban, as shown in Figure 5.4. Using reporting points with Kanban enables
you to monitor the production process at the intermediate level by using Kanban to
process products in individual operations. The reporting points record the confirma-
tion that an operation has been completed. For this example, choose RP Confirmation
w/o Kanban and then click Next.
Figure 5.4 RP Confirmation for Kanban

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
176
5.1.5 Activity Posting
On the next screen, you choose whether or not the system will also post the activities
to a product cost collector at the time of assembly backflush. You do this by choosing
either the Post activities radio button or the or No activity posting radio button (see
Figure 5.5). Activities can be machine hours, labor hours, or processing time, and they
are defined in rate routing. If a company follows a lean manufacturing approach, in
which even the data entry effort is minimal, activity posting involves additional use of
the system’s resources, such as material calculation and price updates. Activity posting
should only be considered if it’s significant when compared with cost. We suggest that
you engage a product cost controlling resource to help with the decision-making pro-
cess about activity posting.
If you instruct the system to post production activities, it will calculate these activities
either on the basis of details from the standard cost estimate or from preliminary cost-
ing in the cost collector for material. The system also posts the activities during the
confirmation process. For this example, choose Post activities and then click Next.
Figure 5.5 Activities Backflush
5.1.6 Separated Backflush
In this step, you choose whether or not the system should perform separated back-
flushes by selecting the Separated Backflush radio button or the No Separated Back-
flush radio button. This option is primarily used to improve a system’s performance,
especially if it becomes slow due to a large number of confirmations. With separated
backflush, you can uncouple certain aspects of backflushing during the confirmation
process by scheduling them as separate background jobs offline.
You should use separated backflushing in the following cases:
 To improve system performance
 If you’re dealing with large BOMs
 For sales orders with many schedule lines for small quantities
For this example, choose Separated Backflush and then click Next.
© 

177
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
If you select the No Separated Backflush option, then Figure 5.6 will appear, with an
option to choose whether the system should simultaneously post GR and GI in the
same assembly confirmation transaction or post them separately. In REM, the final
confirmation also encompasses GR. You should choose whether to also post GI during
the confirmation process. If you select the Post GR Only option, then you can post the
GIs for the components collectively—for example, by using Transaction MIGO in REM.
For this example, choose Post GR and GI in the assembly confirmation.
Figure 5.6 Automatic GR and GI
5.1.7 Process Control
On the screen shown in Figure 5.7, you choose when GI and the calculation of costs are
performed and whether GI and the calculation of costs are aggregated during confirma-
tion. While the option to let the system perform several steps and functions together is
available, you can use the Process Control option to customize the process according to
your business needs. Click Customizing on this screen to make the necessary changes.
(We’ll cover the customization of process controls later in Section 5.5.).
Figure 5.7 Process Control in Backflushing
It’s important to note that this option is only available if you choose Separated Back-
flush in the previous step. If you do, then you need to define the process control for this
separated backflush, and there are four options available to maintain. For this example,
enter “SAP1” for the Process Control and click Next.

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
178
5.1.8 Firming Planned Orders
It makes sense to firm planned orders when creating them on the REM planning table
because a firmed planned order won’t change during the MRP run. There are also
options to not firm the planned order at the time of its creation and to firm planned
orders in the planning time fence only. For this example, you choose Firm planned
orders in plng time fence and then click Next (see Figure 5.8).
Figure 5.8 Firming Planned Orders in REM
5.1.9 Automatic Stock Determination
You can take advantage of the system’s ability to perform automatic stock determina-
tion at the time of component or assembly backflush. You do this by selecting the Use
stock determination radio button (see Figure 5.9). The prerequisite is that you must
have already set up the automatic stock determination procedure in the system. Stock
determination enables the system to suggest available stock for consumption based on
the defined criteria. For this example, you choose Do not use stock determination and
then click Next.
Figure 5.9 Stock Determination
5.1.10 Batch Determination Procedure
Similar to the previous automatic stock determination procedure, you must now
choose whether or not the system should perform the batch determination procedure
in REM. You do this by selecting Separate batch search procedure or No separate batch
© 

179
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
search proc. The material in this case must be batch managed to use this functionality.
Batch determination enables the system to suggest available batches for consumption
based on the defined criteria. For this example, choose No separate batch search proc.
and then click Next.
Note
Batch determination is covered in Chapter 10.
5.1.11 Reduction in Planned Order Quantities
On the screen shown in Figure 5.10, you choose whether the system should reduce the
production quantities in the planned order or in the run schedule quantity while per-
forming assembly confirmation of a material. You also choose whether the system
should reduce the planned order quantity only if it pertains to a specific production
version. As each production version reflects a production line, this option enables the
reduction of planned order quantities as soon as the goods on the production line (in
direct relationship to the production version) are produced.
For this example, you choose All, which causes the system to reduce planned order
quantities in all cases. Then click Next.
Figure 5.10 Reduction in Planned Order Quantities
5.1.12 Reduction Period
On the screen shown in Figure 5.11, choose the period in days during which the system
should reduce the planned order quantities. The system considers the reduction period
from today and expands it to consider the defined dates in the future. For example, if a
company is consistently faced with overproduction and wants to avoid additional pro-
duction for the defined days in the future, then you maintain the reduction period
here. In addition, note that the system reduces the planned order quantities dates from
the past in any case and accords them higher priority than the current date or future
dates. For this example, you enter “3” in the Reduction Period field and then click Next.

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
180
Figure 5.11 Reduction Period for Planned Orders
5.1.13 Creating New Planned Orders on Goods Receipts Reversals
On the screen shown in Figure 5.12, you choose whether the system should automati-
cally create planned orders for reversed quantities. If you do not choose this option,
then the system automatically creates planned orders during the MRP run if necessary.
For this example, choose No planned order creation and then click Next.
Figure 5.12 Planned Order Creation on GR Reversals
5.1.14 Online Error Correction
In this step, you choose how the system processes errors encountered during transac-
tion postings, such as a failed component backflush due to a mismatch in IM. Other
types of errors may include an insufficient quantity of a component’s stock in a com-
ponent backflush, a missing storage location for GI or GR, and so on. If you select the No
online correction or Optional online correction option, then you’ll use a separate trans-
action to address errors separately, provided that you create the reprocessing records
(see the next step). You can also choose Online correction mandatory. For this example,
choose Optional online correction and then click Next.
© 

181
5.1 Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
5.1.15 Error Log Maintenance for Reprocessing
During automatic goods movement processing, such as backflushing, sometimes the
processing isn’t successful due to insufficient stock, an incorrect storage location, or
missing or nonupdated rates for activity types. All such unsuccessful records end up in
error log maintenance for reprocessing, and you need to note that they haven’t been
resolved yet.
On the screen shown in Figure 5.13, you choose how the system should manage all
reprocessing records of all items that have caused errors. The available options are that
the system won’t maintain any reprocessing records, will maintain collective repro-
cessing records, or will maintain individual and collective reprocessing records of
errors. For this example, choose Online correction mandatory and then click Next.
Figure 5.13 Reprocessing Incorrect Records
5.1.16 Movement Types for Stock Postings
On the screen shown in Figure 5.14, the system provides standard movement types that
it uses to perform various IM transactions. For example, for GI against a REM planned
order, the movement type is 261, and its reversal is reflected in movement type 262.
Figure 5.14 Movement Types for MTS Production
If there’s any customized movement type that you need to record an inventory trans-
action, then you enter it on this screen. The prerequisite for using a customized move-
ment type is that you’ve previously maintained it in the system. You can then assign it
on this screen and click Next.

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
182
5.1.17 Naming the Repetitive Manufacturing Profile
Finally, the last screen of the REM profile creation process using the assistant appears.
On this screen, you enter “PP10” for the REM Profile and give it a short description. You
can also go back and make changes to any of the settings you’ve made so far.
5.1.18 Summary of Repetitive Manufacturing Profile Settings
Figure 5.15 shows the overview/summary screen of REM profile PP10. Here, you choose
Continue and then Save. Scroll down to review the remaining options you configured
for the REM profile on the previous screens.
After you create an REM profile, you can’t use the same transaction to make changes to
it; that transaction is only for the creation of the REM profile. Once you’re familiar with
the REM profile, you can create it directly without using the REM profile assistant. That
saves you time and effort because all of the selection options are available on one
screen and in different tabs.
Figure 5.15 Overview of REM Profile after All Settings Are Made
© 

183
5.2 Scheduling Planned Orders
To make changes or update any field in the already created REM profile, follow menu
path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Control  Define Repetitive Man-
ufacturing Profiles or use Transaction OSP2. Here, you make the desired changes to
REM profile PP10. For this example, scroll down and choose Backflush Using Standard
Cost Estimate for Material (not shown) and then save the REM profile (see Figure 5.16).
Figure 5.16 Overall Maintenance of REM Profile
5.2 Scheduling Planned Orders
The next basic configuration topic we’ll discuss is scheduling planned orders. As with
the discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing types, a production planner
can take advantage of the available scheduling options. You set the scheduling param-
eters for planned orders in REM by following menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repet-
itive Manufacturing  Planning  Define Scheduling Parameters for Planned Orders. The
initial screen then appears where you maintain the scheduling parameters for a
planned order. Because the order type for the REM planned order is PE, you enter “1710”
for Plant and “PE” for Order type, and then, you double-click the Plant field (1710). It’s
important to note here that the PE order type is identical to the run schedule quantity.
After making this selection, you choose the Detail icon. This brings up the detailed
screen where you set scheduling parameters for the REM planned order for order type
PE and plant 1710 (see Figure 5.17).

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
184
Because REM uses rate-based scheduling, you check the Takt Time/Rate-Based Sched.
box. If your company decides to use detailed scheduling for all non-REM applications,
you check the relevant scheduling and capacity requirements boxes (both the Schedul-
ing and Generate Capacity Reqs. boxes) to meet your business needs.
You also select the Scheduling Type, which for this example is Backwards. Choose from
the Backward Scheduling and Current Date scheduling types. Refer to Chapter 3 for
more details about scheduling. Although the details covered in Chapter 3 are applicable
to scheduling production orders, they apply equally to planned orders.
Figure 5.17 Detailed Screen of Scheduling Parameters for Planned Orders
5.3 Display
You can select different options to control how different settings will appear in the SAP
S/4HANA system. The following sections cover two display options.
5.3.1 Entry Parameters for a Planning Table
You can choose how the display entry parameters of the REM planning table should
look and how to present the requisite information. The settings you make in this sec-
tion will eventually be available on the initial parameters selection screen of the REM
planning table (Transaction MF50). To maintain or change the entry parameters of the
© 

185
5.3 Display
planning table, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing 
Planning Table  Maintain Entry Parameters (see Figure 5.18).
Figure 5.18 Entry Parameters of REM Planning Table
We will discuss the impact of the preceding configuration on the parameters selection
screen of the planning table (Transaction MF50) in Chapter 8.
5.3.2 Maintaining Row Selection
You can control what information is displayed in the planning table, including receipts,
requirements, and stock situations. To configure the selection of rows in the planning
table, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Planning
Table  Maintain Rows Selection. For this example, check all the boxes (see Figure 5.19);
in a real-world situation, you’d check only the boxes that are relevant to your business
needs to keep things lean when dealing with your company’s unique situation.

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
186
Figure 5.19 Determining Visible Rows in REM Planning Table
5.4 Material Staging
A pull list is basically a movement of stock (stock transfer) from storage location to stor-
age location that uses inventory movement type 311. It’s also known as material stag-
ing. You can assign the previously configured stock determination and batch search
procedures on the screen shown in Figure 5.20. To make the necessary settings, follow
menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Material Staging  Define
Control Data for Pull List.
Figure 5.20 Pull List Control
© 

187
5.5 Global Settings for Confirmation and the Logistics Information System
5.5 Global Settings for Confirmation and the Logistics Information
System
Some settings are maintained at the global level. This includes the period the system is
to consider when automatically updating the statistics (the logistics information sys-
tem [LIS]) of the planning figures. It also includes an option allowing a production plan-
ner to require the system to display the planned order number of the run schedule
quantity number during backflushing.
To configure the necessary settings, you follow menu path SAP IMG  Production 
Repetitive Manufacturing  Backflushing  Maintain Global Settings for Confirmation
and LIS. In the Start: current date+ and Finish: current date+ fields (see Figure 5.21), you
can enter “1” to start from the current date (the earliest) and enter any number of days
for the finish date.
Figure 5.21 Field Selection Table in REM
When the process control for confirmation is maintained, it can control when the sys-
tem should update the transactions, such as during backflush or activity costs calcula-
tion. The system can update the information immediately and online by using an
update program or later in a background program. It’s therefore important to select the
process controls while creating the REM profile.
On the screen shown in Figure 5.22, you place the cursor in the Backflush field and press
(F4); the dropdown list on the right side of the screen then appears. From the drop-
down list, choose one of the three backflushing options available. To set the relevant
parameters, use menu path SAP IMG  Production  Repetitive Manufacturing  Back-
flushing  Separate Backflushing Processes  Define Confirmation Processes.

5 Repetitive Manufacturing Configuration
188
Figure 5.22 Process Control Confirmation Parameters
5.6 Operational Methods Sheet
The operational methods sheet supports business tasks throughout the entire REM
cycle. An operational methods sheet shows the production details that a plant operator
needs to produce goods, and you can make several layout and configuration settings to
customize the display layout of an operational methods sheet. For example, you can
specifically select the desired fields from the work center, operation, component, pro-
duction resources/tools (PRT), or SAP Document Management service that must
appear in the operational methods sheet. You can also separately customize the header
and table (item) details.
To create an operational methods sheet, follow menu path SAP IMG  Production  Basic
Data  Line Design  Operational Method Sheet or use Transaction OLDPS. In the Table
Assignments area shown in Figure 5.23, select COMP (component), MAT (material), OPR
(operation), or PRT from the Table ID column. Next, use the Assign fields folder on the
left-hand side of the screen to choose the fields that you want to appear on the opera-
tional methods sheet.
In Chapter 8, we’ll show you how the settings you’ve made here let you display (and
print) the operational methods sheet in REM. Integration with Microsoft Word must be
in place as well, and the prerequisite is to have Microsoft Word and macros enabled.
The template file is placed within the SAP S/4HANA system, which the system refers to
while creating the OMS.
© 

189
5.7 Summary
Figure 5.23 Operational Methods Sheet
5.7 Summary
The REM profile is the main configuration activity, and it controls the various func-
tions that you can perform in REM. You assign the configured REM profile in the mate-
rial master. You can configure the scheduling parameters of run schedule quantities,
and you can alter the display layout of the planning table. You can maintain controls
for when the system will perform automated functions, such as backflushing, either
online or in the background.
The next chapter will cover the business processes associated with discrete manufac-
turing in the SAP S/4HANA system. It will build upon the configuration concepts and
understandings that were developed in Chapter 3.

© 

PART III
Production Planning Workflows

©

chapters.title: Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
chapters.text:
193
Chapter 6
Production Planning for Discrete
Manufacturing
This chapter covers the important business processes and functions that
are used in discrete manufacturing. We’ll discuss vital and logical links
to explain how the configurations that you made in Chapter 3 match
with business processes when you’re dealing with products or materials
that are subject to high changeability.
Discrete manufacturing, which is also referred to as shop floor control (SFC) in the SAP sys-
tem, is characterized by frequent changes to products in production lines. The demand
for the product is generally irregular, and often, the entire production process is com-
plex, including the routing (the sequence of work steps). Assemblies are often placed in
interim storage, and components are staged with reference to a production order.
Discrete manufacturing finds extensive implementation in the high-tech, steel sheet
recoiling, pumping, textile, steel rerolling, and automobile industries. Production plan-
ning (PP) for discrete manufacturing helps with creating production orders, material/
capacity availability checks, calculating variances, and much more. It also helps to man-
age and smooth out some of related business processes that are involved with this type
of manufacturing, including scheduling, costing, and GI/GR. To help manage the busi-
ness processes seamlessly, PP for discrete manufacturing works with most other func-
tionalities in SAP S/4HANA.
Note
The master data and processes in discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing
have significant similarities. While the specific master data details of process manufac-
turing are covered in Chapter 7, this chapter covers the general master data and pro-
cess specifics in detail.
This chapter begins with an overview of discrete manufacturing and explains how it fits
into the planning and production perspectives. Then, it covers important discrete man-
ufacturing master data, with extensive focus on creating a material, a BOM, a work cen-
ter, routing, and a production version. The concepts and fundamentals covered in this
chapter apply equally to process manufacturing and REM. After the section covering the
creation of master data, the chapter uses the same master data to cover the end-to-end

6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
194
business processes of discrete manufacturing—from production order creation to com-
pletion and settlement.
Finally, this chapter covers some additional functions available in discrete manufactur-
ing to optimize business processes. Let’s get started with an overview of discrete man-
ufacturing.
6.1 Process Overview
Figure 6.1 illustrates the end-to-end process flow in discrete manufacturing. The pro-
cess begins when requirements from manually created planned orders, MRP, or SAP
S/4HANA Sales flow in, based on which production the process must initiate.
Figure 6.1 Process Flow in Discrete Manufacturing
To initiate the production process, you need a production order 1. The system sched-
ules the order 2 and checks for material and PRT requirements and their availability 3
to see if it can produce the required quantities or not. It also checks for the capacity 4
needed to produce the material. If the system is unable to find the requisite capacity, it
reflects an overload.
The next step is to release the production order 5 to enable the system to perform
additional functions. Several functions can’t be performed until the order is released,
including printing shop floor papers 6. The production execution step is the actual and
Completion
Scheduling
Availability
Checks
Capacity
Requirements
Planning
Order Release
Shop Paper
Printing
Material
Withdrawal
Confirmation
Goods Receipt
Order
Settlement
Order
Creation
© 

195
6.2 Master Data
physical production of material, and the first step toward production is material with-
drawal from the warehouse 7.
The plant operator then enters the operation or order confirmation to record the actual
processing of material or production, including scrap produced or rework required 8.
The production department delivers the produced material to the warehouse, which
performs the GR against the production order 9. The last two steps reflect the integra-
tion of SAP S/4HANA Finance’s CO functionality with PP, in which order settlement j
takes place and the production order is closed k.
6.2 Master Data
Before you run processes and transactions in the SAP S/4HANA system, it’s imperative
to set up complete and comprehensive master data. In discrete manufacturing (and in
other manufacturing types), if the master data is set up in the right sequence, then it’s
much easier to connect with other types of master data because the predecessor-suc-
cessor relationship is already taken care of.
The following steps outline the procedure that you need to follow to set up master data
correctly and that we discuss in more detail in the following sections:
1. Create the material master of the product (a finished good or an assembly) that
needs to be produced. Also, create the material master for the raw material that will
eventually become part of the production process.
2. Create the BOM of the product that needs to be produced and assign components,
together with the quantities needed to produce the product. If applicable, also define
a scrap percentage at the operation and component levels. Also, if required, main-
tain details of coproducts or by-products in the BOM.
3. Create the work center.
4. Create a routing for the material and assign the previously created work center.
5. Create the production version for the material and assign the material’s BOM and
routing to it.
Due to the highly diverse nature of the material master creation process, we cover it
briefly in this chapter while offering much more detailed coverage of the PP-specific
master data (specifically, the BOM, work center, routing, and production version).
Note
Refer to Chapter 3 for a more in-depth understanding of the configuration specifics for
discrete manufacturing.

6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
196
6.2.1 Material Master
A material master is whatever the company wants to maintain as an inventory item.
Examples are far ranging and include the following: a finished product that the com-
pany first produces in house and then sells, a raw material it procures and consumes in
the production process, consumables (e.g., greases, lubricants, gloves), trade goods that
it buys and then sells, and spare parts for maintenance of machines.
Creating and maintaining information on a material master is the collective and com-
bined responsibility of a company’s various departments and divisions. A material
master is a central element, so everyone contributes their share of maintaining differ-
ent information. In the SAP S/4HANA system, maintaining logically structured infor-
mation absolutely helps the relevant departments efficiently perform their day-to-day
business functions.
Example
A company produces a finished good (a product). For this product, the company needs
to maintain information for producing the product, selling it, and maintaining plan-
ning and design data, quality control and life span information, costing, and more.
More than likely, one person or department won’t have all of the information related
to many business functions. For example, sales personnel may not know what quality
tests a product goes through. Similarly, the production department won’t necessarily
care about the selling price of the product because it’s primarily concerned with or
focused on producing it. However, salespeople need instant access to the selling price
and the applicable taxes or discounts upon creation of a sales order. Similarly, the pro-
duction supervisor wants better control over the underdelivery and overdelivery toler-
ances allowed for a material, whereas the warehouse in charge needs instant informa-
tion about storing the product.
To add greater visibility to the entire material master creation process, the SAP
S/4HANA system provides views in which the relevant departments of the company
(depending on which SAP S/4HANA functionality they’re working with) can fill in their
share of information. The Basic Data view contains basic information that is applicable
to the entire material master, such as base units of measure, gross and net weights, vol-
ume, product hierarchy, and design/drawing information. We cover the details of these
views later in this section.
When you’re creating a material master, it’s also important to define the material
type—that is, whether it’s a raw material, a semifinished product, a trading good, a
spare part, or a consumable item. The material type controls the views that the system
makes available for the user to maintain information for later use. The views contain
either organization-independent or organization-dependent details. The organization-
independent information includes, for example, the material description or its weight
and volume. The organization-dependent information—that is, information specific
© 

197
6.2 Master Data
to a plant or storage location—may include the planning or forecasting data that is
unique to a given plant. For a different plant (organization dependent), the planning or
forecasting data may be different.
Table 6.1 provides an overview of plant-independent and plant-dependent views of the
material master. A checkmark in a column denotes the data that needs to be main-
tained for a given function.
The following list provides broad explanations of the data in some of the views of the
material master:
Material View Organization-
Independent
Plant Storage
Location
Storage
Number
SAP S/4HANA
Sales
Basic Data 1 and 2 
Classification 
Sales: Sales Org.  
Sales: General/Plant  
Foreign Trade/Export
Data
 
Sales Text  
Purchasing 
Foreign Trade/Import
Data

Purchase Order Text 
MRP 1–4 
Advanced Planning 
Forecast  
Work Scheduling 
Plant Data/Storage 1
and 2
  
Warehouse Manage-
ment 1 and 2
 
Quality Management 
Accounting 1 and 2 
Costing 1 and 2 
Table 6.1 Material Master Views

6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
198
 Basic data
These views consist of information such as base unit of measure, which is the small-
est unit of measure in which a company maintains the product’s inventory. It con-
sists of gross weight, net weight, volume, product hierarchy, size/dimension, and
design/drawing details if you’re using the SAP Document Management service.
 Classification
This view enables classifying specific materials with identical or similar characteris-
tics into groups and categorizing those groups based on specific characteristics.
Due to this view’s diverse and practical nature, it’s available in several areas of the SAP
S/4HANA system. You can classify materials as well as customers and equipment.
When there isn’t any other option to do so, you can use classification to maintain
material-specific information.
 Sales
These views contain details such as the delivering plant, selling unit of measure,
taxes, conditions (e.g., gross price, taxes), sales order text, transportation group,
loading group, and item category. These views are maintained for materials that a
company wants to sell.
 Purchasing
This view contains the purchase unit of measure, purchase group, material group that
groups together materials with similar attributes, purchasing value key that incorpo-
rates underdelivery and overdelivery tolerances, GR processing time, and batch man-
agement. For externally procured materials, maintaining this view is mandatory.
 MRP
These views enable a materials planner to define how to procure a material and how
to plan and control the material within the production process. The definition of the
procurement type specifies whether a company procures the material externally,
produces it in house, or can do both. It enables specifying whether the material fol-
lows reorder point planning or is demand driven. Using a strategy group, you can
maintain controls for whether a material uses make-to-stock (MTS), make-to-order
(MTO), or assemble-to-order (ATO) production. Material planning for a procured or
produced material requires you to maintain some or all of the MRP views.
Note
Chapter 13 covers MRP.
 Advanced planning
Production planning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) with SAP S/4HANA introduces
the advanced capacity planning and heuristics for materials planning that were pre-
viously available in SAP Advanced Planning and Optimization (SAP APO). PP/DS
transactions are executed directly in SAP S/4HANA, thereby eliminating the need
for a separate installation to run PP/DS transactions. The material master and work
center creation are also synchronous, which means that a material or a work center
that has just been created can be immediately available for planning in PP/DS.
© 

199
6.2 Master Data
 Forecast
This view enables a materials planner to define forecasting parameters and models,
such as constant, seasonal, moving average, and trend. If you already know the fore-
casting data properties, then you can select from one of the available forecasting
models. If you don’t know which model to select, the system provides an option for
automatic model selection. It requires you to define the number of historical and
forecast periods, based on which the system runs forecasting.
Note
Chapter 11, Section 11.4 covers forecasting.
 Work scheduling
This view contains details for underdelivery and overdelivery tolerances for in-
house production, the production scheduler, the production scheduling profile that
controls several business functions, the production unit of measure, and the pro-
duction time in days. For material produced in house, maintaining this view is man-
datory. It’s possible to have a different unit of measure, which in this case is the pro-
duction unit of measure and may be different from the base unit of measure.
 Plant data
This view contains information such as the storage bin, temperature conditions, and
shelf life data for batch-managed materials, including maximum storage period,
minimum remaining shelf life, and total shelf life.
 Quality management
This view provides the option to select different types of inspections that a material
may go through—for example, finished goods inspection, raw material inspection,
and in-process (during production) inspection. The QM procurement key denotes
whether raw material must be quality managed and also denotes the relevant certif-
icate type.
 Accounting
These views contain the price control indicator—that is, whether the material will be
valued at a standard price or a moving average price—and the valuation class.
 Costing
These views contain details such as overhead group, origin group, and variance key.
Note
The creation of material types and also the material master is largely driven by MM
processes, with other functional areas supporting and facilitating it.

6.2.2 Bill of Materials
A material’s BOM consists of components grouped together with the relevant quanti-
ties that are needed to produce the material in the production process. For example, if
vanilla ice cream is the main product, then its BOM will consist of all the ingredients
(components) needed to produce it. Similarly, to produce an automobile, there’s a need
for an engine, a chassis, a body, and a large number of other components.
To create the BOM of a material, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data 
Bills of Material  Bill of Material  Material BOM  Create or use Transaction CS01. On
the initial screen, enter “FG129” for Material and “1710” for Plant, as shown in Figure 6.5.
This is the material number for which you want to create the BOM. In the BOM Usage
field, enter “1” to specify the purpose for which you’re creating and using a BOM. BOM

6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
202
Usage 1 denotes that it’s for production purposes and will be available when creating a
production order for this material. Other BOM usages are SD BOM, PM BOM, and Cost-
ing BOM.
In the Valid From field, enter “01/01/2026” to denote the date from which the BOM
becomes available in the production process. In this example, the BOM’s validity starts
on January 01, 2026 (01/01/2026), when a user creates a production order. Therefore,
the BOM won’t be available if the user creates a production order in the month of
December 2025. If no date is given, the system automatically assigns the BOM’s cre-
ation date as the Valid From date. The purpose of BOM validity is to ensure that if the
company has a new BOM starting at a later date, then the validity of the new BOM can
be set accordingly and will become available during the production process.
In the Alternative BOM field, you can specify up to 99 different alternative BOMs for a
material. This means that the same material can be produced in up to 99 different ways
by having an alternative BOM option available. For example, when one component of
a material’s BOM is unavailable in stock, a production planner can select another alter-
native BOM to continue the production process. When a material’s BOM is created for
the first time, the system automatically assigns the alternative BOM as 1. When a new
BOM for the same material is created, then the system issues a warning message and
internally assigns the next alternative BOM number to it.
You choose Header to open the Create Material BOM: Header Overview screen. The
base quantity of a material denotes the quantity based on which the material’s BOM
components are defined. The system then automatically divides the component’s
quantity by the base quantity during production order creation. This is especially use-
ful when component quantities are very small and defined in, for example, milligrams,
as with vanilla flavor.
For this example, you assign a base quantity of “10 PC” in the BOM header as shown in
Figure 6.5. The components and their quantities that you define in the BOM now
denote the requirements to produce ten pieces of material FG129.
The BOM Status field controls whether the BOM is active and is available for usage, and
the value 1 denotes that it’s active. If there’s a business need to deactivate a BOM, then
you can simply change the status of the BOM to Inactive at any time.
Next, click the Item button to go to the screen shown in Figure 6.6, which shows the
item overview of the BOM.
Enter the material numbers for the individual components, together with the quantity
for each. The system will divide the component’s quantity by the base quantity (if any)
during production order creation to calculate the quantity of the component needed to
produce the main material, which in this case is FG129.

 Fixed Quantity
If you check this box, you’re denoting that regardless of production order quantity,
the component quantity remains fixed. For example, to produce 50 kg of a main

6 Production Planning for Discrete Manufacturing
204
material, the component quantity required is 50 kg. To produce 35 kg of the main
material, the system calculates the component quantity of 35 kg, as defined in the
material’s BOM. However, if you check the Fixed Quantity box and select a compo-
nent Quantity of, say, 35 kg, then the system will calculate the component quantity
required as 35 kg, even if the main material’s production quantity is 50 kg.
 Operation scrap in %
Operation scrap is defined as a percentage increases the issuance quantity of a com-
ponent to account for scrap during the production process. For example, if you
define Operation scrap as 5% and the component quantity as 100 kg, then the sys-
tem automatically calculates the required component quantity as 105 kg to account
for the operation (during production) scrap. It’s mandatory to select Net ID (not
shown) when defining operation scrap.
 Component scrap (%)
Component scrap is defined as a percentage increase of the issuance quantity of a
component to account for scrap during the production process. For example, if you
define Component Scrap as 10% and the component Quantity as 100 kg, then the
system automatically calculates the required component quantity as 110 kg to
account for the operation scrap.

system will consider both scrap types during component quantity calculation. During
the MRP run, the system will take all of these scrap definitions into account to suggest
planning proposals.

material as soon as the first shift starts on a given morning, then they will need to
approach the warehouse supervisor on the day before to issue the requisite compo-
nents. To handle this scenario, you enter “–1” in the Lead-time offset field so that the
component’s required availability date is one day earlier than the planned start date of
the production. The negative number of the workday reflects that the production
supervisor needs components earlier than the start date of production, and a positive
number of days is used to show that there’s no need to have the components until the
defined number of days after the start of production. The system ignores this option if
the production process follows lead-time scheduling (and not just basic date schedul-
ing). To enable this behavior for lead-time scheduling, you assign the components to
the corresponding operations in the routing. Note that such a positive or negative lead
time is always independent of lot size.
The Special Procurement field enables a materials planner to define a component-

plant. Maintaining a special procurement type at the component level gives it a higher
precedence than at the material level. During an MRP run, the system explodes the
material BOM and evaluates parameters maintained at the component level for plan-
ning purposes before it looks for the information at the material level.

Tips and Tricks
You can make mass changes to BOMs to attend to any new business requirements. For
example, a materials planner might want to replace an old and obsolete component
number in all of the material BOMs in which it’s currently in use or change the compo-
nent’s quantity in all of the material BOMs in which it’s in use. For such business sce-
narios, you use Transaction CS20 to make mass changes to the BOM. In the upper half
of the screen, you define the selection criteria; in the lower half, you define the changes
that need to be made. When making the desired changes, be sure to select one of the
many radio buttons that are available.
6.2.3 Work Center
A work center can be a production facility, a processing unit, a machine or group of
machines, or even a laborer or group of laborers directly involved in the production
process. It’s also possible to group together machines and laborers in a single work cen-
ter. To create a work center, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data 
Work Centers  Work Center  Create or use Transaction CR01.
Note
For process industries, create a resource by following menu path Logistics  Production –
Process  Master Data  Resources  Resource  Resource  Create or using Transaction
CRC1.

On the initial screen, enter Plant “1710” for the relevant new work center. It’s manda-

the Work Center Category field; this is a control function used to ensure that only the
relevant screens are eventually available during work center creation. Work center cat-
egory 0001 denotes a machine, whereas 0007 denotes a production line.
Other important functions on the screen are Copy from Plant and Copy from Work Cen-
ter. Check the boxes of the reference work center views that need to be copied to the
new work center. These functions help in reducing data entry efforts during the new
work center creation by automatically making available all of the necessary informa-

needed.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the various tabs that are available in a work center
and the data to be maintained, as well as the data’s impact on business processes.

denotes using this work center in all task list types.
Two important fields on this screen are as follows (but not shown):
 Shift Note Type
A shift note is an option to record the important shift-related details of a work center.
These can be general details of the shift, as well as any specific problems encoun-
tered at this specific work center during the production process.
 Shift Report Type
A shift report compiles and consolidates all of the information from a single shift
note or multiple shift notes. It is available either in PDF format or other formats, as
well as in the layout defined for a company. The user can also digitally sign a shift
report before a printout is possible and send a shift report by email.
Recall from Chapter 3 that we discussed the configuration of the Standard Value Key
field, in which you entered “SAP1.” The standard value key consists of three activities to
note and record during the production process when the ASSEMBLY work center was
used. A standard value key denotes activities performed at the work center, as well as
the option to record them or not. Some of the activities are production time, setup
time, processing time, and labor time. If you enter “SAP1,” the system automatically
brings up three activities (Setup, Machine, and Labor) for which the actual details in the
confirmation process are recorded (see Figure 6.10).
