You can also change the description of how each control instruction in the PI sheet
appears. This option helps combine the relevant process instructions in one visual tab
so that it’s easier for the line operator to either enter the details of the process parame-
ters or simply use the available functions. To ensure that the system displays control
instructions as a tab, you first enter the desired description of the control instruction
and then select the Control Instruction characteristic from the dropdown menu.
Tables in Execution Steps
To generate a table in the PI sheet using XSteps, you first create a process instruction
and then follow menu path Create  Control Data  Table. The screen shown in Figure
7.49 provides the option to enter the minimum and maximum number of lines (rows)
that must appear in the table.
When you create a table, you maintain the input value of the parameters by following
menu path Create  Entry  Numeric Value (or Parameter Value). Figure 7.50 shows an
example of a table created in XSteps with calculation and a signature option.
© 

327
7.7 Execution Steps
Figure 7.49 Tables Settings in XSteps
Figure 7.50 Tables in XSteps
Scope of Generation in Execution Steps
Whenever an XStep is created, the system provides the option to define the scope of
generation, just as it does for process instructions. In this step, define the scope of gen-
eration for materials, resources/work centers, documents, or orders. XSteps offer far
greater generation options as compared to process instructions. Figure 7.51 lists the
available options for scope of generation.

7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing
328
Figure 7.51 Scope of Generation in XSteps
Signature in Execution Steps
At the process instruction level in XSteps, you can incorporate a signature at various
steps of data entry (or other input) to validate the information entered (see Figure 7.50
again). You can even enter the details of a digital signature; the digital signature func-
tionality is already configured in the system, and you can choose it by following menu
path Create  Control Data  Signature.
Process Messages in Execution Steps
At the same level on which you enter a signature in the process instruction, you can
select the associated process message. To do so, follow menu path Create  Control Data
 Process Message. Use the same process message example here that you created in
Chapter 4. Enter Process Message Category “PP20” and the relevant message character-
istic will automatically appear.
Execution Steps Overview
Place the cursor on any level and press (F8) to simulate the PI sheet, and it will appear
on the right-hand side of the figure.
Tips and Tricks
Use the drag-and-drop option on any element in XSteps to move it to the desired loca-
tion. For example, if Table Entries should appear earlier than you want in the XStep
tree, simply select it and drag it to your desired new location.
© 

329
7.8 Process Message Evaluation
7.8 Process Message Evaluation
The system offers process message evaluation as a report that you can use to access
information on process messages. Process message evaluation integrates with Micro-
soft Excel and offers the option to save the evaluated information either as a pivot table
or simply as an Excel worksheet.
You can use all the standard and self-defined process message characteristics in the
evaluation. You can also self-define the layout for evaluation—that is, decide how the
system should display the information during evaluation. But it’s important to first
create an evaluation version before using it to evaluate process messages.
For process message evaluation, use menu path Logistics  Production—Process  Pro-
cess Management  Evaluation  Process Messages or use Transaction CO52.
On the initial screen, enter the Plant as “1710” and the Evaluation version as “SAPS4”
and then choose the Create icon. But first, create an evaluation version before using it
in process message evaluation. You can also create a new evaluation version with refer-
ence to an existing version. To create an evaluation version, simply enter the Plant and
choose the SAP XXL icon. The system will then bring up the screen shown in Figure 7.52,
wherein a user can check the relevant boxes and save. The system will then ask the user
to enter the Evaluation version and its Description.
Figure 7.52 Selection Screen of Process Message Evaluation
For this example, shown in Figure 7.52, you use the previously created Evaluation ver-
sion SAPS4 and check the boxes in the line characteristics (Line char.) and column char-
acteristics (Col. char.) columns for the characteristics (process messages) that need to
be evaluated. For the Line char. option, check the requisite boxes so that the data

7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing
330
appears in horizontal lines (in rows). For the Col. char. option, check the requisite boxes
so that the data appears in columns. In this example, the control recipe status appears
in a column, whereas the control recipe destination appears in a row.
If you select the Evaluate option, the system downloads the information either in an
Excel spreadsheet or in a pivot table format. Alternatively, you can use the Preview
option. The first column and the related rows will show the control message ID. In the
second column, the first row pertains to the control recipe status, whereas the second
row reflects the control recipe number.
7.9 Process Manufacturing Cockpit
Building on the configuration made in Chapter 4 for the process manufacturing cockpit,
PP10, we now show how it appears when a user wants to use it for data entry and report-
ing purposes. To access the process manufacturing cockpit, use menu path Logistics 
Production—Process  Process Management  Manufacturing Cockpit  Start or use
Transaction COPOC. On the initial screen, enter the Plant as “1710” and the cockpit Name
as “PP10.” Then, choose Execute.
Figure 7.53 shows manufacturing cockpit PP10, in which the required details are main-
tained. All details are optional, except for the Material quantity consumed field, which
is mandatory, as denoted by the red asterisk. When you exit the process manufacturing
cockpit, the process messages automatically become available in the message monitor
(Transaction CO54XT). From there, you can send them to the predefined destinations.
Figure 7.53 Process Manufacturing Cockpit, PP10
© 

331
7.10 Resources Network
7.10 Resources Network
A resource network is a group of individual resources used in the production process. The
ability for a user to pick and choose from the available or even required resources during
process order creation ensures smoother production. Using a resource network also
eliminates the need to create a production version for every variant of the production.
We’ll show you a real-time example in which several resources are available and usable
for similar production processes, but the business process entails a user deciding which
resource to use just before production begins. For this purpose, a planning resource is
defined that is only used for planning and that represents several resources that can be
used in the same way. There’s a case of three equivalent resources (mixes), and the
business requirement is that the user wants to dynamically (and in real time) select the
actual resource while creating a process order level (before release).
To map this business requirement in SAP S/4HANA, you use the resource network
functionality. You begin by creating a specific task list usage type and checking the rel-
evant box. Then, you create the relevant master data (including the resource network),
create a resource hierarchy, and assign them to a master recipe. Then, when a process
order is created and ready to be released, the system will prevent the user from releas-
ing it until the user selects one resource from a resource network.
Let’s go through this step by step.
First, you access the screen shown in Figure 7.54 via Transaction OP45 and create a new
task list usage, Z08, by copying standard task list usage 008. In the detailed view, check
the until rel. box to denote that this task list usage will only allow for release of a pro-
cess order after one of the resources from the resource network is selected.
Figure 7.54 Configuration Settings for Task List Type 2
Then, using Transaction CRC1, assign the previously created task list usage Z08 to
Resource MIX_PLAN, as shown in Figure 7.55. Simultaneously, create a few additional
resources (Mix1, Mix2, and Mix3) and assign standard task list usage 008 to them.

7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing
332
Figure 7.55 Assigning Task List Usage in Planning Resource
Next, access Figure 7.56 via Transaction CR24 to create Resource network MIX_RN. Here,
assign all the previously created resources, including planning resource MIX_PLAN, by
first choosing New Assignment and then entering individual resources in the popup
that appears. Be sure to also check the Assignment Indicator box for each of the
resources you’re adding to the resource network. To create relationships among these
assigned resources in the resource network, choose Graphic in Figure 7.56, which brings
up Figure 7.57.
Figure 7.56 Resource Network and Assignment of Resources
© 

333
7.10 Resources Network
The three resources in the resource network MIX_PLAN shown in Figure 7.57 appear
connected to the planning resource MIX_PLAN. To connect a resource, first select the
relevant resource and then choose Connect (shown on the right-hand side of Figure
7.57). Choosing a resource will also enable currently disabled options (such as Duplicate,
Modify, and even Delete) to delete a resource from a resource network.
Figure 7.57 Creating Resource Network
Next, access the screen shown in Figure 7.58 via Transaction C201 or C202 (create or
change a master recipe). Then, in the Recipe Header tab, enter “MIX_RN” for Resource
netwrk to assign it.
Figure 7.58 Assigning Resource Network in Master Recipe

7 Production Planning for Process Manufacturing
334
Next, choose the Operations tab, which opens the screen shown in Figure 7.59. Then,
assign only planning resource MIX_PLAN at the operation and phase levels and main-
tain any additional details if required.
Figure 7.59 Assigning Planning Resource in Operation and Phase
Figure 7.60 shows the process order creation screen (Transaction COR1). While you’re
trying to release the process order, the system sends an error message (BS013—System
Status WCRE Is Active) to indicate that the process order release has failed. You will now
need to select a resource from the resource network before the process order can be
released. To select a resource, choose Operations on the screen shown in Figure 7.59,
which takes you to the screen in Figure 7.61.
Figure 7.60 Error Message when Releasing Process Order
Figure 7.61 shows the operations view of a process order and consists of the same plan-
ning resource, MIX_PLAN. To choose an actual resource from the resource network,
first select Operation 0010 and then choose Execute resource selection, which will
cause the screen shown in Figure 7.62 to appear.
Figure 7.62 shows the popup that enables you to select one of the resources from the
resource network. Place the cursor on the relevant resource and click Choose, and plan-
ning resource MIX_PLAN shown in Figure 7.61 will be replaced by the resource you’ve
chosen. To choose a different resource from the resource network, choose Execute
Resource Selection on the screen in Figure 7.61 again.
© 

335
7.11 Summary
Figure 7.61 Resource Selection
Figure 7.62 Choosing Resource from Resource Network
7.11 Summary
In general, the production processes in process industries are no different from those
in discrete manufacturing, except that PP-PI offers options to record greater details of
the processes. However, PP-PI also offers functionalities such as process management,
which is unique to PP-PI only. With the introduction of XSteps, the process manage-
ment functionality not only becomes easier to implement in PP-PI but also finds usage
in discrete manufacturing industries.
The next chapter covers production planning in repetitive manufacturing.

©

chapters.title: Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
chapters.text:
337
Chapter 8
Production Planning for Repetitive
Manufacturing
This chapter covers the important business processes and functions in
repetitive manufacturing, along with the vital and logical links to
required configuration.
Repetitive manufacturing (REM) is mainly used for production scenarios with high rep-
etition rates, high product stability, and low product complexity. REM is characterized
by quantity-based and period-based production (uninterrupted production over a long
period of time). The REM type is often mixed up with takt-based flow manufacturing,
which is usually used whenever the production process is based on takt time (e.g., in
the automobile industry). The two production types (REM and takt-based flow manu-
facturing) have different business backgrounds and properties, and they use different
SAP technologies, different master data, and even different planning tools.
Note
Refer to Chapter 1 for comparisons of discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing,
and REM, as well as how to decide which manufacturing type is most relevant for a given
industry.
This chapter will discuss what REM is and how it fits into the planning and production
perspectives. A walk through the REM process flow will provide you with a comprehen-
sive, step-by-step understanding of each process involved. We’ll discuss the important
REM master data and walk through how to run the end-to-end MRP process and how to
use planning to optimize the production and procurement processes. We’ll also discuss
performing the material availability check for missing components, as well as the
capacity available check and overload, if any.
Then, we’ll cover the actual and daily business processes in REM: planning tables and
material staging, based on the current material situation; the creation of a production
list; REM confirmation at the assembly, component, and activity levels; and capacity
requirements planning. Next, we move into handling scrap at various levels, cancella-
tions, and reversals. A short discussion of cost object controlling is next, followed by
discussing heijunka and its role in REM. Finally, we conclude with a few important eval-
uation reports available in REM.

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
338
As often as possible, we link this chapter’s discussions with the configurations you
made in Chapter 5. If necessary, we also give pointers to necessary configurations.
8.1 Overview
Companies that work with high-volume and mass-produced materials (e.g., pens, pen-
cils, keyboards, batteries) find that REM (rather than discrete manufacturing or process
manufacturing) best reflects their production processes. If your actual business pro-
cess is simple and straightforward, you should consider implementing the lean REM
production type, as long as it can fulfill your business needs from a functional perspec-
tive.
The SAP S/4HANA system supports and offers make-to-order (MTO) REM, which
enables handling of configurable materials. REM also supports handling unit manage-
ment, in which goods and packaging are mapped as a logistic unit. Serial number
assignment for unique identification is also possible in REM, and make-to-stock (MTS)
REM is equally possible.
The following sections will cover how REM plays a central role in production planning
and production execution, and they will also discuss the REM process flow.
8.1.1 Roles of Repetitive Manufacturing in Planning and Production
Figure 8.1 provides an overview of the role of REM in the planning and production areas
of the SAP S/4HANA system. The planning process starts with requirements coming
from SAP S/4HANA Sales or from the material forecast, and it then becomes a part of
demand management. The system runs the MRP based on the figures given in the
demand program, and the outcome of the MRP run (the planning run) is the creation of
procurement proposals. These procurement proposals can be planned orders (order
proposals), purchase requisitions for external procurement, or delivery schedules for
scheduling agreements with vendors. In REM, the planned orders can’t be converted
because these are sufficient to trigger the production process. In fact, the REM planned
orders take on the new order type of the run schedule quantity when the system assigns
a production version to them.
After actual production execution, the system records the relevant information, such
as components consumption, activities consumption, and production yield. Finally,
because REM is a period-based manufacturing type, the product costing team proceeds
to the settlement process, in which it settles variances and overheads during cost col-
lector settlement.
© 

339
8.1 Overview
Figure 8.1 REM in Production Planning
8.1.2 Repetitive Manufacturing Process Flow
Figure 8.2 illustrates the end-to-end process flow in REM. The process flow sequence is
as follows:
1. The process begins when requirements from MRP (through planned independent
requirements [PIRs]) or SAP S/4HANA Sales flow in, based on which the production
must be initiated.
The system covers the infinite requirements with planned orders during the MRP run.
2. The system performs the line loading and checks the capacity requirements to see if
it’s able to produce the required quantities. If it can’t, it’s overloaded.
3. The system checks for the availability of the components needed to produce the
material.
4. The operational methods sheet that contains detailed instructions for producing the
material can be printed.
5. It’s also possible to print the production list, which contains quantities to produce
and important dates. A production list specific to a material or a production line can
also be printed. Following the lean manufacturing principle, these are the only two
shop floor papers.
6. The material needs to be staged by undertaking a transfer posting from the main
store to the production floor, from where the components are issued to the produc-
tion lines.
Sales and Distribution Material Forecast
Costing
Capacity Requirements Planning
Demand Program
Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
Repetitive Manufacturing
Pl. Ind. Rqmts. Sales Orders
Planned Orders (PE)
Planned Orders
Production
Backflush
• Goods Issuance
• Confirmation
• Goods Receipt
Cost Collector Settlement

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
340
7. The production execution step is the actual and physical production of the material.
8. If the business process entails working and reporting with reporting point back-
flush (milestone confirmation), then the WIP can be calculated.
9. The production yield, record issuance, consumption of components, and postpro-
duction activities are recorded. Excess component consumption, excess activities
such as machine hours or labor hours consumed (as compared to standard dura-
tions), and scrap can also be recorded. It’s also possible to record any deviation in
consumption.
10. The last step reflects the integration of CO with PP, in which the product costing
team performs settlement using the product cost collector.
Figure 8.2 Process Flow in REM
Note
QM processes can also be integrated into the REM production process to ensure that all
products produced in-house will go through quality checks before being dispatched to
customers.
Requirements
Planning Line
Loading and
Capacity Checking
Availability Check
Print Operational
Method Sheet
Print Production
List
Material Staging
Production
Execution
WIP Determination
(Optional in Case of
Reporting Point
Backflush)
Final Confirmation
Goods Receipt
Backflushed Component
Withdrawal
Settlement Using
Product Cost
Collector
© 

341
8.2 Master Data
8.2 Master Data
Before running processes and jobs in the SAP S/4HANA system, you must set up com-
plete and comprehensive master data. REM has its own unique master data that often
overlaps with other production types, such as discrete manufacturing and process
manufacturing. If master data is set up in the right sequence, it’s much easier and more
logical to interconnect because the predecessor-successor relationship is already taken
care of.
Table 8.1 provides an overview of the master data that is needed in REM. It begins with
the material master of the product (a finished good or an assembly), which you specifi-
cally mark as the REM type in the MRP 4 view of the material master by checking the
relevant box. You also assign it the REM profile that you previously configured (in
Chapter 5).
You then create a BOM of the product that needs to be produced, and you assign the
components together with the quantities needed to produce that product. If needed,
you can also define a scrap percentage at the operation or component level.
Then, you create a work center or production line, followed by a rate routing for the
material, in which you assign the previously created work center.
Finally, you assign the material’s BOM and rate routing to a production version for the
material. You then check the REM box to denote that the material produced will follow
the REM process.
When all of the logistics master data is in place, the CO team can create product cost col-
lectors of the material. This can be specific to or independent of the production version.
Materials Production Versions
 Activating REM indicator
 Assigning REM profile
 Assigning production supervisor in Work
Scheduling
 Activating REM indicator
 Rate-based planning
 BOM assignment
 Production line
 Receipt storage location
Bill of Materials Cost Object Controlling
 Material BOM  Product cost collector or even product cost
estimate
Work Center (Production Line) Rate Routing
 Production line as work center  Assign work center (production line)—
usually one operation
 Production rate (quantity per time)
Table 8.1 Master Data of REM

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
342
Note
We strongly recommended that you maintain close coordination and liaison with the
CO team to ensure that when they’re working with PP, both teams are completely
aligned in terms of their working and reporting needs.
For example, each work center (production line) must be assigned a cost center, which
the CO team should provide. It may provide one cost center for multiple work centers
or one cost center for an individual work center, depending on how the team wants to
see the cost center reporting and evaluation.
One possible approach to having several cost centers within one production line is to
incorporate additional cost-relevant operations (in the rate routing) and still adhere to
the principle of just one work center for production (and PP).
Let’s now discuss how to set up each of the REM-specific master data areas.
8.2.1 Material Master
The first step in creating REM-specific master data begins with creating the necessary
settings in the material master of the product (an assembly or a finished good). To
change the material master, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management 
Material Master  Material  Change  Immediately or use Transaction MM02. On the
resulting screen, enter Material number “2256” and press (Enter) to bring up the orga-
nizational level, wherein you enter Plant “1710” and Stor. Loc. “171A.” Choose Continue
to bring up Select Views, in which you select MRP 4 and press (Enter) again.
On the screen shown in Figure 8.3, you maintain the details related to REM production
processes in the MRP 4 view of the material master. Then, you check the Repet. Manu-
facturing box to denote that the material will undergo the REM production process.
Then, select PP10 in the REM Profile field.
Note
This is the same REM profile that you configured in Chapter 5.
A REM profile is a four-digit code that determines the following:
 The movement type that the system uses to post GRs and GIs
 How the system reduces planned orders and run schedule quantities in the confir-
mation process
 How the system makes the correction in the assembly’s components (according to
BOM), which enables you to define whether GI posting isn’t possible in the system
due to forgotten GR postings or whether the system should process the same later
on in a separate backflush processing transaction
© 

343
8.2 Master Data
 How the system deals with production activities during confirmation, such as setup
or machine hours
You should then save the settings in the material master and assign a production
supervisor in the Work scheduling tab of the material master.
Figure 8.3 REM Indicator in MRP 4 View of Material Master
8.2.2 Bill of Materials
The process you use to create a material BOM for REM is no different from the process
in discrete manufacturing. Refer to Chapter 6, Section 6.2.2, which covers the details of
material BOM creation.
8.2.3 Work Center (Production Line)
The alternative term for a work center in the SAP S/4HANA system is a production line.
Work center is a term specific to discrete manufacturing, whereas production line is spe-
cific to REM. Production lines are usually created as simple work centers in the system
(and because the SAP system refers to these as work centers, we’ll use both terms
throughout this chapter to maintain a logical connection between the SAP terms and
real-world business scenarios). In the work center, you define the availability of the
production line. In REM, following the lean manufacturing principle, you don’t model
all workstations in the production line as individual work centers. Rather, you model
the entire production line (with all of its work centers) as one work center. You enter
the production line you create as the work center in the Production Line field in the pro-
duction version, and you specify this same work center in the single operation of the

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
344
rate routing. You can represent production lines that need to include or model more
than one work center in a line hierarchy.
To create a work center (production line), you follow menu path Logistics  Production 
Master Data  Work Centers  Work Center  Create or use Transaction CR01. On the ini-
tial screen you use to create the work center, enter Plant “1710” to create a production
line. It’s mandatory to assign a plant while creating a production line.
Enter “KBASSY” for Work Center. This is an alphanumeric identification to denote a
production line. A production line can be a production facility, a processing unit, a
machine or group of machines, or a laborer or group of laborers directly involved in the
production process.
For this example, set the Work Center Category field to “0007.” This control function
ensures that only the relevant screens will be eventually available during production
line creation. Work center category 0007 denotes a production line.
Other important functions in this screen are Copy from Plant and Copy from Ref. Work
Center. These functions help reduce data entry efforts during new production line cre-
ation by automatically making available all of the necessary information from the refer-
ence production line in the new production line, which you can then change as needed.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the five tabs for a work center and the information
that you need to maintained.
Basic Data
Figure 8.4 shows the Basic Data tab of a production line. Here, you enter a short descrip-
tion of the production line in the text box at the top-right of the screen. In the Usage
field, enter “009.” A usage is a control function that controls whether this production
line eventually becomes available for various other tasks, such as during the creation of
rate routing or the production version. A usage of 009 denotes that this production
line can subsequently be used in all task list types.
In the Standard Value Key field, enter “SAP3.” A standard value key denotes activities
performed on a specific production line, as well as the option to record them. Some of
the activities include production time, setup time, processing time, and labor time. For
REM, the SAP S/4HANA system already provides the SAP3 standard value key, which
you can used.
Next, enter the production time as a rule for maintenance. If you assign the Production
Time as Must Be Entered from the dropdown list, then users must enter the actual dura-
tion of the activity during confirmation of the REM planned order. Otherwise, the sys-
tem won’t allow the production supervisor to save the confirmation. Other mainte-
nance options are May Not Be Entered, May Be Entered, and Should Be Entered. These
offer various degrees of control, from an information message to a warning message, if
the requisite information (duration, in this case) isn’t recorded during confirmation.
© 

345
8.2 Master Data
Figure 8.4 Work Center: Basic Data
Default Values Tab
Choose the Default Values tab to see the screen shown in Figure 8.5, wherein you assign
the PP01 control key to the work center. A control key is a control function that man-
ages whether a production supervisor can perform an activity or a task associated with
the work center. Place the cursor on the Control Key field and press (F4).
While you have the cursor on the Control Key field, if you choose the Detailed Informa-
tion button, the system will show the details in the lower half of the same screen. Note
that in REM, only the following checkboxes are included: Scheduling, Det. Cap.
Reqmnts., Cost, Insp.char. required, and Confirmations. No other checkboxes work for
REM. If a given box isn’t checked, then that step or function can’t be performed on this
work center (KBASSY). For example, if the Scheduling box isn’t checked in the control
key, then the system won’t perform scheduling on this work center.
In addition, note that in the Confirmations field, you can use option 1 (milestone confir-
mation) to report point backflush during confirmation.
Note
You set up control key by using Transaction OPJ8.

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
346
Figure 8.5 Work Center: Default Values
Capacities Tab
Choose the Capacities tab in the work center (production line) creation screen to see
the options screen shown in Figure 8.6. In the Capacities tab, enter “SAPR01” in the
Setup Formula field; this denotes the setup formula that the system will use during
capacity calculation. For Processing Formula, enter “SAPR02.” This is for Capacity cate-
gory 001 for Machine (shown at the top of the screen).
Note
See Chapter 16 for information on capacity requirements planning.
Scroll down and choose the Capacity Header button at the bottom of the Capacities tab
(not shown). This brings up the header screen of the capacity for Work Center KBASSY,
as shown in Figure 8.7. The capacity header contains comprehensive details about the
capacity: the start and end time of the shift, the length of breaks, the overload percent-
age allowed for capacity, shifts and intervals, and the factory calendar.
Note
Refer to Chapter 2 for information on how to set up the factory calendar and capacity
planner group.
© 

347
8.2 Master Data
Figure 8.6 Work Center Capacities
Figure 8.7 Work Center Capacity Header

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
348
For this example, enter the following in each field or check the relevant box:
 Capacity Category short text (not shown): “Keyboard Assembly Line-1”
This short text describes the capacity category—for example, the specific produc-
tion line or machine.
 Capacity Responsible: “A”
It’s important to assign a capacity planner group to a production line, so that during
evaluation of capacities, the capacity planner group can be one of the evaluation cri-
teria. The capacity planner group shouldn’t be the name of the person but instead
the area or domain of responsibilities.
 Capacity Base Unit: “Min” (for minutes)
This is the unit of measure (in minutes) in which the system displays the capacity of
a production line.
 Start Time: “08:00:00”
This is the start time of a production line.
 End Time: “17:00:00”
This is the end time of a production line.
 Length of Breaks: “01:00:00”
This is the total length of breaks during the start and end of shifts. Break time
reduces the availability time of the capacity of a production line. For example, if a
machine runs 12 hours a day, with two hours of daily breaks, then the available
capacity of the production line is 10 hours. Note that this fact is considered in lead
time scheduling of orders.
 Capacity Utilization: “100” (percent)
This is the actual capacity utilization factor. This factor has to be as realistic and as
close to the actual capacity of a production line as possible to enable the system to
calculate the available capacity of the production line. The capacity utilization is
reduced accordingly if the production line can’t be used for a certain percentage of
time—for example, due to machine failure.
 No. Ind. Capacities: “1”
This is the number of individual capacities associated with a production line. For
example, four individual ice cream mixers are created as one production line, so the
individual capacities are four in number and directly multiplied by the timings of
the capacity of the production line. For example, if the available capacity of a pro-
duction line is 10 hours a day, and if there are four individual capacities, then the
total available capacity of the production line is 40 hours.
 Relevant to Finite Scheduling
Checking this box requires the system to consider capacity overload during capacity
evaluation.
 Overload %
A capacity overload of a certain percentage directly increases the capacity availabil-
ity of the production line by the defined percentage. If, for example, the total capac-
© 

349
8.2 Master Data

8.2.4 Rate Routing
Rate routings are designed for the maintenance of production rate−based task lists and
are used in REM, among other business scenarios. In a standard routing, the base quan-
tity (e.g., pieces) generally remains constant, and the time-related data, such as the pro-
cessing time, is maintained using standard values. Rate routing works in exactly the
opposite way. The production quantities are maintained on the basis of individual
items, and the reference time is constant. Thus, a rate routing describes the quantity of
an item that must be produced within a given period of time.
A rate routing can contain a series of operations. The production rate is defined for
each operation based on the production quantity, production time, and associated unit
of measure. The production rate is determined as the quotient of the production quan-
tity and production time. This enables a production planner to determine the produc-
tion quantity in terms of tons per shift, for example. Usually, only one operation is pro-
duction relevant, and the others may be controlling or print relevant. Using line hierar-
chies, several production-relevant operations can support capacity leveling in different
work centers along the production line.
All other aspects of the functionality and maintenance of standard routings and rate
routings are identical. Work centers are assigned to the rate routing, which should also
contain the standard value key in which the standard values for production time, setup
time, and teardown time are predefined.
A reference routing is used as a reference or template in routings. A reference rate rout-
ing contains a sequence of operations. As with a rate routing, the production rate for an
operation is defined on the basis of the production quantity and reference time. The
functionality and maintenance of reference routings are similar to those of reference
operation sets.
To create a rate routing, you follow menu path Logistics  Production  Master Data  Line
Design  Rate Routing  Create or use Transaction CA21. On the initial screen where you
create a rate routing, enter Material “2256” and Plant “1710.” In the Key Date field, enter
“07/01/2024” and denote the date when the rate routing becomes available in the pro-
duction process. Because the rate routing validity starts on July 01, 2024, this rate rout-
ing won’t be available if, for example, a production planner creates an REM planned
order for the month of April 2024.

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
352
If you provide no date during rate routing creation, then the system will automatically
assign the rate routing’s creation date as the valid from date.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the various elements of rate routing.
Header Details
Choose the Header icon. In the header details of the rate routing, enter the following:

available when a REM planned order for the material is created.

8.2.5 Production Version
Production versions denote different ways in which a company produces a material—
for example, a material with one rate routing but multiple BOMs. Therefore, for each
BOM and the same rate routing, the system needs a separate production version to
denote its uniqueness during REM planned order creation. The requirement to create a
separate production version also applies when there is one BOM for a material but
multiple routings. In the REM process, it’s mandatory to create a production version
and make it available.
The production version can also be specific to the quantity range, thus acting as a control
function. For example, you can create one production version that has a quantity limita-
tion of 1 to 1,000 PC and another production version for a quantity greater than 1,000 PC.
The production version can also be specific to the validity period, thus acting as a con-
trol function. For example, a production version can be valid from April 1, 2025, to
March 31, 2025, and from April 1, 2025, on, a new production version will be effective.
When the system has to assign the production version to the REM planned order, it
searches for specific validity dates and automatically assigns the relevant production
version when it finds it.
If the system can find multiple production versions that fulfill the search and applica-
tion criteria, it assigns the first available production version to the REM planned order.
The first available production version refers to the list that can be called from the mate-
rial master (in the MRP 4 or Work Scheduling views).
To create a new production version, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Pro-
duction  Master Data  Production Versions or use Transaction C223. Then, in the Selec-
tion Conditions area of the screen shown in Figure 8.13, enter Plant “1710” and Material

8.3 Material Requirements Planning in Repetitive Manufacturing
Extensive coverage of MRP is provided in Chapter 13. In this section, however, we will
show you how to use MRP to preplan REM-based manufacturing types. The preplanning
© 

359
8.3 Material Requirements Planning in Repetitive Manufacturing
process entails that a production planner has already planned to produce predeter-
mined quantities of a product and use MRP for comprehensive planning, including the
components (the BOM) of the product. It’s important to note that there are no REM-spe-
cific details for MRP; REM uses standard MRP. For example, preplanning isn’t a prereq-
uisite for REM. REM based planning can also be used on sales orders only (MTO). For this
example, we use strategy 40 (the MTS production method), which is a typical case in
REM. You can use any planning strategy in conjunction with REM (MTO and MTS), and
we’ll focus on a typical real-world example in the keyboard manufacturing industry.
For normal and standard planning, the planning process starts with either a sales order
forming the basis of planning or quantities calculated from forecasting. Any or all of
these quantities are forwarded to demand management, which ends up forming the
PIRs. The MRP considers PIRs and generates procurement proposals, including planned
orders. A production planner then evaluates the planning results and converts them
into procurement elements for external procurement; these don’t need to be con-
verted any further or for REM types. The steps are as follows:
1. Maintain planned independent requirements
You use Transaction MD61 to enter the PIRs into the screen shown in Figure 8.15.

horizon from “07/01/2024” to “08/05/2025.” Press (Enter). Next, enter the planning
quantities as maintained in Figure 8.15 and save the PIR. The next step is to run the
MRP.

Figure 8.16 shows the initial screen to run MRP. You set the parameters as shown and
choose Execute (not shown) to begin the process of running MRP. As soon as the sys-
tem completes the planning process (the MRP run), it displays the planning results.

8.4 Planning Table
The planning table in REM is highly interactive and enables the planner to perform
many functions and activities with all details automatically updated. For example, if
you create a new planned order in the planning table, the system automatically
updates the capacity requirements in the upper half of the planning table screen. The
planning table also provides comprehensive visibility into material and capacity avail-
abilities. In fact, important planning in REM, such as line loading planning, is generally
undertaken in the planning table. In the following sections, we’ll discuss the planning
table parameters and how to create orders.

To access the planning table in REM, you follow menu path Logistics  Production 
Repetitive Manufacturing  Planning  Planning Table  Change Mode or use Transaction

enter important selection parameters. Based on the input parameters you define on

8.4.5 Range of Coverage
If you’ve already made the necessary configuration for the range of coverage profile
and you’ve assigned this coverage profile to the material master in the MRP 2 view of
the material master, then you have the option to evaluate the coverage in the planning
table. However, you must first activate this in the View menu. The range of coverage
profile contains the parameters for calculating the dynamic safety stock, and this is a
statistical calculation on the basis of average daily requirements. The range of coverage
profile includes, for example, maximum stock level, minimum stock level, target stock,
and the statistical and actual range of coverage.
If you made the configuration to display Minimum Target Stock in the Coverage field
(refer to Chapter 5, Section 5.3.2, showing the Coverage field and the Target Stock Level
value), then the system will display Minimum Target Stock as a default setting in the
REM planning table. To change this display in the REM planning table, choose menu
path View  Actual Range of Coverage Profile (see Figure 8.28). If needed, the maximum
stock level can also be displayed in the planning table.

8.5 Material Staging
Material staging is the step in which the components from the replenishment storage
location are transferred to the issuing storage location. These components are con-
sumed during the actual production process. This step involves the stock transfer
transaction with standard movement type 311, and it transfers stock from the replen-
ishment storage location to the issuing storage location. The system issues or back-
flushes the components of the material from an issuing storage location.
Tips and Tricks
The alternative term used for material staging is pull list. A pull list provides details on
which material’s components are faced with shortages. At the same time, it also trig-
gers stock transfers. You can also use storage location MRP or Kanban as the pull list.
You can view the current or existing material situation of the planned orders before
proceeding to stage the materials. Evaluating the current material situation gives a
materials planner a better perspective, and it helps them decide which components
should be staged first. Material staging in REM is typically carried out with reference to
a certain time horizon or a certain time interval—for example, a shift or until the end
of the week. Except for MTO, materials aren’t staged with reference to any specific
planned order. Similarly, the GR of the material produced is period based, with or with-
out reference to planned orders.
In the following sections, we’ll explain how to view the current situation, trigger
replenishment, evaluate the material documents that the system created for material
staging, and perform collective availability checks.
8.5.1 Current Situation
To view the current situation in the pull list, you follow menu path Logistics  Produc-
tion  Repetitive Manufacturing  Material Staging  Pull List—Current Situation or use
Transaction MF63.
On the initial parameter selection screen, you choose from a large number of selection
options and also define the end date for requirements in the Selection Horizon for
Rqmts field. This is the date until which the system will consider the requirements of
materials. There are also selection options for when a company uses the Kanban produc-
tion process or has integrated with either warehouse management in SAP S/4HANA or
embedded extended warehouse management (embedded EWM) in SAP S/4HANA.
For this example, you define the Plant as “1710” (this is a mandatory entry requirement)
and set the Selection Horizon for Rqmts as “12/02/2025,” which is the end date until
which the system will consider all materials requirements. If the materials require-
ments lie outside the horizon, they won’t show up in the current list of material staging.

8.5.3 Collective Availability Check
As a production planner, you must frequently and consistently monitor the availabil-
ity of the components that are needed to produce materials. While you can always per-
form an availability check of an individual planned order in REM, it’s more efficient to

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
374
do a collective availability check on several materials simultaneously to gain a better
understanding of the material availability situation and then plan or take action
accordingly.
In the collective availability check option, you use a large number of selection parame-
ters to choose the information that the system should display.
To perform a collective availability check, use menu path Production  Repetitive Man-
ufacturing  Planning  Collective Availability Check or use Transaction MDVP. On the
initial screen, enter the desired parameters and choose Execute or press (F8). If the sys-
tem finds a materials shortage, it identifies the shortage with a red traffic light. Choose
the Missing Parts icon for an in-depth analysis and evaluation; it contains details of the
requirements versus available quantities, as well as the dates when the required and
shortage material should be in stock to initiate the production process on a timely
basis. The availability check function in SAP checks the availability but can’t trigger
replenishment.
8.6 Production List
A production list is a list of REM-planned orders that can be printed for information and
record-keeping purposes. A production list can also be created and printed for a specific
production line or work center hierarchy. This is helpful for production supervisors
and line operators (on the shop floor) who don’t have access to the SAP system, to give
them information access and for necessary planning and action, where needed. On the
selection screen, you can choose how the ensuing production list should appear as an
output.
To create a production list, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manu-
facturing  Planning  Production List or use Transaction MF51.
On the initial selection parameters screen, you can choose to print the production list
based on the defined period and whether the system should consider only firmed
planned orders, scheduled planned orders, or dispatched planned orders.
For this example, you define the period of examination as 07/01/2024 to 12/31/2024
and enter the Production Line as “KBASSY.” Then, choose Execute or press (F8) to open
the screen shown in Figure 8.32. This screen shows the production list of REM planned
orders. In the period under evaluation, there are a few REM planned orders in the pro-
duction list. These are run schedule quantities, which means that production can pro-
ceed because they are already assigned to the KBASSY production line.

The operational methods sheet is primarily used by production personnel to obtain a

needs to appear in it. It integrates with Microsoft Word, and you can make any last-

the header level or at the operation level.
To print an operational methods sheet, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Mas-

tion LDE1. On the initial parameter selection screen, enter Material “2256” and Plant

8.7 Confirmation
Assume that a company manufactures products on a long production line with a long
lead time. With long lead times, the GIs for the components may be posted much later
in the system than when they are actually physically withdrawn. All components used

8 Production Planning for Repetitive Manufacturing
376
along the production line are usually backflushed during final confirmation, but the
GIs for the first components at work centers are posted on time and not by backflush-
ing with a final confirmation at the end of the production line.
An REM confirmation indicates that the actual production has happened and that the
production supervisor is now entering the details of the goods produced in the system.
It’s important to note that during the assembly backflush, the system automatically
posts the component consumption (the GI).
The system performs the necessary functions depending on how the REM profile is set
up and assigned to a material. In this case, it makes sense to use reporting point confir-
mation to post the withdrawal of components (and production activities) at an earlier
stage, after operations are already completed.
In the following sections, we cover how to perform confirmation for REM assembly,
REM components, and REM activities. We’ll also give an overview of when and how
backflushing should be used in REM situations, as well as the different types of back-
flushing that are available in REM.
8.7.1 Overview
When the GR is posted for the finished parts during confirmation, a number of differ-
ent actions are performed by default (these are controlled to a large extent by using the
REM profile):
 Posting GI for the components used by using the backflush option
 Reducing run schedule quantities/planned orders and the associated capacity
requirements on the production lines
 Posting production costs incurred (material costs, production activities, and over-
head costs) to a product cost collector
 Creating a data entry document (a material and/or financial document)
 Updating statistics in the LIS
Other optional actions (see the Details icon in the confirmation transaction) include
the following:
 Taking account of the setup costs for material quantities that are independent of
their lot sizes
 Posting component and assembly scrap
 Performing collective entry for different materials in a line
 Archiving confirmation documents
 Performing GI confirmation only or activity confirmation only (more or less activity)
 Using reporting point confirmations
 Decoupling confirmation processes
© 

377
8.7 Confirmation
 Performing aggregation
 Using postprocessing and reversal options
In addition, production based on sales orders enables confirmation with reference to
sales orders.
Note
We configured REM profile PP10 in Chapter 5, Section 5.1; revisit that chapter to see the
settings that were made.
Although the consumption of system-recorded components is based on a BOM calcu-
lation, it’s possible to make changes to consumption quantities during the REM assem-
bly backflush. Separately, a production supervisor can also use this component’s back-
flush option to record excess component consumption, either against a specific REM
planned order or for general consumption during the production process.
The confirmation can be any of the following types:
 Assembly confirmation
The production of goods is complete, and the production supervisor wants to record
the yield of the produced material. At the same time, there’s a need to record the
consumption of the components against the produced goods based on the standard
BOM. The user can also record the assembly scrap on the same screen with a differ-
ent posting.
 Component confirmation
The user can record extra consumption of the components against the produced
goods, based on the standard BOM. The user can also record component scrap on the
same screen. Furthermore, components can be issued if backflush isn’t applicable.
(This scenario isn’t used often.)
 Activity confirmation
The user can record excess consumption of the activities, such as machine hours,
labor hours, or setup hours, against the produced goods based on the rate routing.
Furthermore, the user can record activity scrap on the same screen.
As with assembly backflush, several options are available on the component backflush
screen:
 Record corresponding information during REM confirmation and backflush. This
flexibility is available because the REM production type caters to all MTS and MTO
productions.
 Record components’ scrap, either with reference to the assembly material or stand-
alone. The movement type for scrap is 551.
 Reverse the material document for an already posted consumption or scrap.
 Perform a reversal function, without reference to any material document.

8.7.3 Component Confirmation
When you select the Component backflush radio button in Figure 8.33, the system will
offer you the following two options:
 No BOM explosion (Manual Component Entry)
In this option, you enter the assembly in the Material field so that the system can
find the link with the product cost collector. If you select No BOM Explosion, the Pro-
cess Component List will be empty.
 Propose Components Acc.to BOM
This option lets you enter a quantity of the assembly material, and the system will
explode the assembly BOM and propose the component quantities for backflush or
excess consumption. If you select this option, you must provide the reference quan-

A production supervisor can also select the relevant radio button to indicate whether
excess component consumption for a specific reporting point or after the last report-
ing point took place. This radio button only appears if a valid reporting point is entered.
8.7.4 Activities Confirmation
When you select the Activity backflush radio button in the REM confirmation screen
(Transaction MFBF) shown earlier in Figure 8.33, the system displays the following two
options:
 No Planned Activities from Rate Routing
In this option, you enter the material number together with the activity types, as
well as the corresponding duration of each activity. The system doesn’t make any
suggestions.
 Fetch Planned Activities from Rate Routing
With this option, you enter the quantity of the assembly material and the system will
refer to the rate routing of the assembly material and propose the details. When you
select this option, you must give the reference quantity of the assembly material.

8.7.5 Actual Assembly Confirmation
In the screen shown in Figure 8.34, you perform the actual assembly backflush posting
for Material 2256, Plant 1710, and Production Version 0001. The yield confirmation for
the material in this REM assembly backflush is 10 EA. Because the configured and
assigned REM profile PP10 stipulates that there should be mandatory reporting point
backflushing, you check the RP Confirmation box and also assign Reporting Point 0010.
Figure 8.34 Assembly Backflush against Planned Order
Reporting point backflush in REM occurs when the system is able to automatically
backflush several operations in the processing sequence. Using the reporting point
backflush procedure, a production supervisor can backflush components synchro-
nized with actual consumption as much as possible. From the controlling perspective,
reporting point backflush displays WIP for assemblies that haven’t completely been
backflushed. The REM profile controls whether to use the reporting point backflush for
a material; furthermore, the control key is used to determine whether an operation is a
reporting point operation (a milestone). If so, then all previous operations that haven’t
been confirmed yet or all previous operations that aren’t mandatory reporting points
are automatically backflushed together. If several operations are marked as reporting

points, the system should backflush components according to the processing sequence
of the reporting point operations.
You save the entries at this stage, and the system performs the following three func-

 It performs GR (yield confirmation) of 10 PC for material 2256.
 It performs backflush (GI) of components according to the BOM of 2256.
 It records and updates activities according to the rate routing of material 2256.

First, it’s confirming the yield of 10 EA for assembly Material 2256; then, in the second

If you make any changes to durations to reflect actual durations (whether on the higher

In the Reason field, you can define the reason for the excess consumption of activities.
This is also known as the reason for variance. You can also give reasons for variance as
one of the selection criteria to generate a standard SAP system report, which can even-

malfunctions, electrical faults, labor shortages, emergency shutdowns, and so on.

When you’re defining reasons for variance, we recommend that you take past experi-

sumption, and so on. Also, consider which reasons are important for analysis and
reporting purposes and thus should be available as selection options when running
standard or custom-developed reports.

activities you’re posting: GR and GI with document XXX and activities posted. This
means that the system successfully posted the GR (production yield), GI (components
consumption), and recording of the actual production activities’ durations. It’s import-
ant to add that the business process depends on whether or not the planned activities,
per the rate routing, have been adjusted manually during the confirmation according
to the real production efforts.

8.7.6 Separated Backflush
If you need to manage a large number of backflush processes—that is, a high volume of
data entry for GI and for activity postings—then you should use the separated back-
flush functionality. In separated backflush, the system posts GI and activities during a
slower time of the day (or even at night). You control the separated backflush option in
the REM profile creation (configuration).
To perform separated backflushing, follow menu path Logistics  Production  Repeti-
tive Manufacturing  Data Entry  Separated Backflush or use Transaction MF70.
Enter the appropriate parameters on the initial screen and check the appropriate boxes,
such as Post Goods Issue and/or Post Production Activities.
8.7.7 Postprocessing of Components
If you’ve defined that the system should not perform online error correction during
REM confirmation, then you can correct these errors later on. The nature of the error
can be that there were insufficient component quantities for backflush (GI) during
confirmation—for instance, due to incorrect or delayed postings in MM. Alternatively,
there can be errors when the system is unable to find the storage location to backflush
(GI) components from.
It’s important to attend to these errors in a timely manner because they’ll continue to
accumulate in the incorrect goods movement log. To postprocess components (or
other errors), follow the SAP menu path Logistics  Production  Repetitive Manufactur-
ing  Data Entry  Postprocess  Postprocessing Individual Components or use Transac-
tion COGI.
Note
To use Transaction COGI, select the Also Create Individual Postprocessing Records
option in the REM profile.
Enter the appropriate parameters on the initial screen and then choose Execute or
press (F8). The ensuing screen will list all the confirmations in which the system found
errors and wasn’t able to process them successfully. The details of the errors can be dis-
played to help a production supervisor understand why they occurred—for example,
because of a missing storage location.
Note
The standard tool for REM postprocessing is Transaction MF47.

The SAP system provides an option for you to collectively enter a large volume of data

assembly backflush, the system records production yield and consumes components
according to the BOM. For assembly scrap, it consumes additional components of the
assembly with reference to the assembly’s BOM.
To use the collective confirmation functionality, follow menu path Logistics  Produc-
tion  Repetitive Manufacturing  Data Entry  Collective Entry or use Transaction MF42N
(see Figure 8.37).
Figure 8.37 Collective Confirmations

enter the Material “2256” and the Scrap quantity of “1” PC. The system will then create
two material documents, one for the assembly backflush and another for the assembly

8.8 Reversals and Scrap
In a normal business scenario, the process operator occasionally faces a situation in
which there has been a data entry error or the confirmation details posted in the sys-
tem differ from the actual production figures. In such cases, reversal becomes inevita-
ble. In REM, you can perform document-specific reversal or document-neutral reversal.
You can also perform a reversal while remaining on the REM confirmation screen
(Transaction MFBF) and selecting the Doc-Specific Cancellation icon. When you select
© 

385
8.8 Reversals and Scrap
this option, the system navigates to Transaction MF41. Alternatively, you can use a sep-
arate transaction code (Transaction MF41) to reverse a specific document. (We use this
option in this example.)
In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to perform reversal of the documents
already posted in the system, such as during reporting point backflush. As already
stated, there are two types of document reversals. In document-specific reversal, you
select a specific document that needs to be reversed. On the other hand, in document-
neutral reversal, you simply correct the total confirmed quantity—for example, for a
day. In this case, you just reverse the difference between the previously posted quan-
tity and the correct quantity, which reduces the overall posting efforts. Later in this sec-
tion, we also cover how to manage REM actual scrap for components, assembly, and
activity.
