9.7 Evaluating and Reporting Kanban
On the initial screen that appears, enter the plant, the delivery date (and the time), and
other selection parameters in the relevant fields so that the system displays the desired
information. If needed, check the Trigger Alerts box to enable the system to issue alerts
for all delayed kanbans.
Choose Execute and the screen shown in Figure 9.59 appears. In this example, at least
three kanbans are awaiting replenishment. The kanbans’ statuses are set to 2 (container
empty, with replenishment).
Figure 9.59 Delayed Replenishment Elements
9.7.4 Kanban Plant Overview
To get a plant-wide overview of the kanbans’ replenishment statuses, you use Transac-
tion PK11 or follow menu path Logistics  Production Planning  Production  Kanban 
Evaluations  Plant Overview.
On the initial screen, enter selection parameters so that the system only displays the
information needed. The screen shown in Figure 9.60 shows the status of each mate-
rial, the supply area, the number of kanbans, and their statuses. For example, for mate-
rial 2262, there are four kanbans, each with a quantity of 50. The system shows that two
kanbans (50%) have a WAIT status, one kanban (25%) is FULL, and one kanban (25%) is IN
TRANSIT.
Figure 9.60 Kanban Plant Overview
Figure 9.61 shows the Set Kanban Container Status app. You can use this app to update
the status of various kanbans. To do so, place the cursor in the Enter Barcode field, and
in the ensuing dropdown, choose from a list of all the available kanbans. After you
make the relevant selection, the right-hand side of the screen will enable you to make

9 Kanban
454
the desired changes or updates, such as changing from one status to another. Once
you’ve successfully saved the entries, they’ll appear in the Saved tab.
Figure 9.61 Set Kanban Container Status App
In addition, you can use the Schedule Kanban Control Cycle Status Change app to
schedule regular updates to the statuses of various kanbans without making manual
entries.
9.7.5 Kanban Control Cycle Analysis
While Chapter 21 is focused on discussing many SAP Fiori apps for production plan-
ning, we’ll discuss one app here that is specific to Kanban: the Kanban Control Cycle
Analysis app.
The Kanban Control Cycle Analysis app evaluates the Kanban control cycles with focus
on the status of the circulating kanban containers. It identifies critical situations early
and quickly, for example, Kanban control cycles containing errors or having too many
open replenishments (visualized by the number of empty kanban containers). You can
use it to get insights into the overall Kanban replenishment situation at the aggregated
level (for example, at the plant level) and then drill down to the details at the Kanban
control cycle level. A range of chart types are available for evaluations (see Figure 9.62).
Note
Refer to SAP PRESS E-Bite Kanban in SAP S/4HANA for a detailed understanding on this
topic.
© 

455
9.8 Summary
Figure 9.62 Kanban Control Cycle Analysis app
9.8 Summary
Implementing Kanban isn’t restricted to the production process; it can be imple-
mented in procurement, stock transfer, and interplant transfer processes. In this chap-
ter, we covered the Kanban configuration that needs to be in place so that configured
objects can be assigned to the master data. We discussed the entire production process
using Kanban, including the use of the Kanban Board app. We also discussed handling
Kanban errors, as well as Kanban evaluation and reporting.
The next chapter is about batch management.

©

chapters.title: Batch Management
chapters.text:
457
Chapter 10
Batch Management
Batch management is an important part of a company’s ability to pro-
cure, produce, store, and sell materials. The batch defines a quantity of
material by characteristics unique to that batch. Those characteristics
determine how the material in that batch is used, sold, or moved.
Certain materials can be defined in SAP S/4HANA as being batch managed. A batch is a
quantity of material that represents a homogeneous unit with unique specifications.
The batch of material may refer to a quantity of a chemical that is produced in one pro-
cess or a quantity of bottles of water that were filled on a certain filling line from a spe-
cific tank. There are many ways in which a batch can be defined. In SAP S/4HANA, a
batch is used to identify units of material as they move through the system. A batch
can also have specific characteristics that enable users to identify and use it within the
MM or PP functionality. This chapter describes the processes involved in creating and
changing a batch and the process of batch determination. The first section gives an
overview of batches and how they’re used in industry.
We’ll start out this chapter by covering the configuration settings you need to start
using the batch management functionality in the SAP S/4HANA system. After configu-
ration, we cover the requisite master data, followed by the business processes associ-
ated with batch management, such as manually creating a batch master and how the
system automatically creates the batch master during various business transactions.
We’ll then discuss specialized but important batch management topics, such as auto-
matic batch determination, active ingredient management, batch derivation, and shelf
life expiration of batches. The chapter will conclude with some important batch man-
agement reports, including the Batch Information Cockpit.
10.1 Batch Management Overview
The definition of a batch differs among companies, industries, and countries. For exam-
ple, in the pharmaceutical industry, strict guidelines and regulations determine what a
batch is. These regulations on batches and batch control include the ANSI/ISA-88 stan-
dard and the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) 21 CFR Part 11 specifications in the
United States.

10 Batch Management
458
Although there is no one exact definition of a batch, the following definition from
ExxonMobil Aviation may help:
A batch is the specific quantity of a material produced in a single manufacturing
process (i.e., under the same conditions, thus featuring identical properties). Each
batch of material is given a batch number. Each batch of a material is tested with
regard to relevant characteristics to ensure it meets the values or is within the
range for those characteristics.
A second definition of a batch is from Marathon Oil Company, and it significantly dif-
fers from other definitions: “A batch is a shipment of a single product that is handled
through the pipeline without mixing with preceding or following shipments.”
This third definition of a batch is from the Hawaiian Coffee Association:
A batch refers to a quantity of coffee coming to the roaster. Quantities of the same
coffee arriving at different times would be viewed as separate batches. Changes
from batch to batch—even of the same variety of bean—must be detected by the
roaster if he is to produce coffees that are consistently the same.
Whatever the definition, the fact is that the batch has to be identified by a batch record.
This can be as simple as identifying bags of coffee beans as they arrive at the plant or as
complex as identifying a batch by numerous qualifying characteristics to ensure qual-
ity and safety.
A batch of material can either be purchased from a supplier or produced internally.
We’ve discussed the need to manage materials by batch; however, in SAP, the material
must be identified as batch relevant.
The identification of a batch record is especially important for the pharmaceutical
industry, due to the regulations set down by the FDA in the United States and other reg-
ulatory bodies around the world, such as the Drugs Controller General of India, Bundes-
gesundheitsamt in Germany, Health Canada, and the Medicines and Healthcare Prod-
ucts Regulatory Agency in the United Kingdom.
These regulatory bodies are primarily interested in public safety. Regulations such as
FDA 21 CFR Part 11 in the United States are aimed at improving the efficiency of quality
control and the quality assurance process. Each batch produced has to be quality
tested, with the results stored electronically against the batch number.
The most critical use of batch numbers in the pharmaceutical industry is for product
recall. Batch numbers also can be used as tracking devices for companies in case of
errors or contamination. Manufacturers publish product recalls every day, but for the
pharmaceutical industry, product recalls can save lives.
A pharmaceutical company can voluntarily recall a product. If the company finds that
a result from a test on a batch was incorrect, putting the batch out of tolerance, then the
product made from that batch could be hazardous.
© 

459
10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management
The errors could go all the way back to the supplier, if any of the material was pur-
chased. If a supplier informs the company that a batch of purchased material was out
of tolerance, then that batch must be traced through the production process to find all
finished goods batches that may have used components or ingredients from the faulty
batch.
In the United States, the FDA has the power to request that a company initiate a recall
when it believes that a drug violates the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act. The FDA
will request a recall when it concludes the following:
 A drug that has been distributed presents a risk of illness, injury, or gross consumer
deception.
 The manufacturer or distributor hasn’t recalled the drug.
 FDA action is necessary to protect public health.
In a recall, the manufacturer informs the retailer, wholesaler, or even consumer about
how to identify the batch number on the product and which batch numbers are a part
of the recall.
Let’s begin with the configuration basics of batch management.
10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management
In the following sections, we’ll follow a step-by-step approach to the configuration
steps required to set up batch management in the SAP system.
10.2.1 Defining the Batch Level
A batch can be defined at different levels, but it’s defined at the plant level by default.
At the beginning of an SAP S/4HANA implementation project, an MM consultant helps
the company decide the batch level at which it requires batch monitoring and report-
ing.
Use the following guidelines to determine that batch numbers are unique at the follow-
ing three levels:
 Plant and material combination level
At this level, the plant and material combination have unique batch numbers. Dif-
ferent materials in the same plant can be assigned the same batch number, and the
same material in different plants can also be assigned the same batch number. In
this context, the material is transferable from one plant to another, and although
the batch number is the same, the specification of the destination batch will remain
unchanged.
 Material number level
As batch numbers are created at the material level, the same batch numbers can be

10 Batch Management
460
used for other materials as well. After setting the batch level as the material in the
system, a material can have the same batch number even though the materials are
in different plants with the same specifications. The same batch number can be reas-
signed to other materials with different specifications.
 Client level
The batch level is set at the client level, which is the highest level. The same batch
number can’t be used for different materials or different plants. The batch number
assigned is unique and can be assigned only once; thus, every material will always
have a new or different batch number.
The system’s default setting is the plant level. As mentioned earlier, this can be custom-
ized to the batch level as needed. However, it’s important to note that while switching
to a higher level is always possible, it’s only possible to lower the batch level from the
client level to the material level. This is because the batch data is organized so that it
has to be converted whenever it’s switched to another batch level.
To define the batch level, you follow these steps:
1. Go to SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Specify Batch Level and
Activate Status Management. Choose the batch level as we just discussed (client,
plant, or material level) and then save.
2. Choose Batch Status Management to activate batch management status in the cli-
ent, check the Active box, and then save.
3. Choose Plants with Batch Status Management and check the Batch Status Manage-
ment box in the resulting list for those plants where batch status management has
to be active. Check the boxes and save.
4. Choose Initial Status of a New Batch, specify which initial new batches are to be
assigned for each material type, and check the Initial Status box for each material
type that will be batch managed.
10.2.2 Assigning a Batch Number
In this step, you define the batch number range and assignments. To do so, follow
menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Number Assign-
ment  Activate Internal Batch Number Assignment. On the resulting screen, select the
Active radio button to activate the internal batch number assignment. You can also
define whether internal batch number assignment is allowed for goods receipts that
have an account assignment via menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch
Management  Batch Number Assignment  Internal Batch Number Assignment for
Assigned Goods Receipt.
In the next step, define the number ranges for internal batch number assignment by
following menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Batch Num-
ber Assignment  Maintain Internal Batch Number Assignment Range.
© 

461
10.2 Batch Level and Batch Status Management
In the standard SAP S/4HANA system, you can set the number range object BATCH_CLT.
Number range 01 from 0000000001 to 9999999999 has already been defined.
As with internal batch number assignment, you can assign a number range for external
number assignment. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Manage-
ment  Batch Number Assignment  Maintain Number Range for External Batch Number
Assignment.
Maintain the external number range by clicking the Number Ranges button and then
the Change Intervals button. Here, you can use the already-defined number range or
create a new number range by clicking the Inset Interval button. Enter the number
(No.), From number, and To number, and then choose Save.
Note
You can use SAP enhancement SAPLNOIZ for internal batch number assignment. This
enhancement comprises two function module exits to define number ranges or tem-
plates for batch numbers:
 EXI_SAPLVO1Z_001
This exit is used to replace the number range object and/or interval proposed with
its own number range object. It’s also used to stop the system from assigning an
internal number based on the material or plant. This exit can be used to stop the
dialog box from appearing.
 EXIT_SAPLVO1Z_002
This exit helps assign its own number or even change the number assigned by the
system.
10.2.3 Creating New Batches in the Production Process
This configuration step is how the system creates new batches for processes and process
orders. To define the new batch creation for processes and process orders, you follow
menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New Batches 
Define Batch Creation for Production Order/Process Order or use Transaction CORW.
You can make specific settings in batch management for the existing production con-
trol profiles. You can also choose the production control profile for making the settings
by selecting the required option (see Figure 10.1) for automatic batch creation:
 Automatic batch creation at order creation
 Automatic batch creation at order release
 No automatic batch creation in production/process order
Then, you select the batch classification option in the Batch Classification field. In addi-
tion, check the Extended Classification box so that more batch-specific functionalities
become available.

10 Batch Management
462
The production scheduling profile (PS Profile), which in this example is YB0001, is then
assigned in the Work Scheduling view of a material master.
Figure 10.1 Batch Management in Production Process
10.2.4 Creating Batches for Goods Movement
You can define the new batch creation settings for movement types. To do so, follow
menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New
Batches  Define Batch Creation for Goods Movements.
For instance, when batch numbers aren’t determined by the system automatically, the
user may want to assign a batch number for goods receipt on a production or a process
order (movement type 101) manually. To achieve this, you choose the Automatic/man-
ual without check option for Movement Type 101, as shown in Figure 10.2.
Figure 10.2 Batch Management with Movement Type
© 

463
10.3 Master Data in Batch Management
For each material type, you can also define whether a new batch can be created via the
batch master creation transaction (Transaction MSC1N). To do so, follow menu path
SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Creation of New Batches  Define
Initial Creation of Data for Batch Master Transactions. If a company only wants the sys-
tem to assign the batch numbers automatically and doesn’t want users to have the
option to assign them manually, especially for finished products, then you choose
Automatic / no manual creation for material type FERT.
This completes our discussion of the basic configuration settings needed for batch
management. In the next section, we discuss how to set up master data in batch man-
agement.
10.3 Master Data in Batch Management
A first step when working with batch management is to identify the material that
needs to be batch managed and then update the material master record by setting the
batch management requirement. In the following sections, let’s go over the different
master data management processes and steps specific to batch management.
10.3.1 Activating Batch Management in the Material Master
In the SAP S/4HANA system, batch master records always depend on material master
records. Therefore, the first step is to define batch management requirements in the
material master. Go to Transaction MM02 (Change Material Master), enter all the
required details, and check the Batch mgmt (batch management) box to denote that
the material has to be handled in batches (see Figure 10.3). The following views of the
material master contain the checkbox for batch management:
 Sales: General/Plant
 Plant Data/storage 1
 Purchasing
 Work scheduling
All views are the same, and any changes and updates in one view appear in all views.
Tips
The indicator for the material can be changed from batch-managed to nonbatch-
managed only if there has been no stock for the current period and the previous period.
This is to allow for transaction posting of batch-managed materials in any previous period.
In the SAP S/4HANA system, batches are posted for a material, so the batch master
records are always dependent on material master records. If there is a mismatch
between subjected material and the batch requirement, then you need to post all
stocks from the previous fiscal year, the previous period, and the current period. To do
this, uncheck the Batch mgmt box and repost the stock in batches into the system.

10 Batch Management
464
Similarly, you can cancel the batch requirement for any material that your company
doesn’t want to batch manage anymore. If there’s a need to reorganize the batch master
records, then you must uncheck the Batch mgmt box, post the required batch requirement,
and then post the stock back into the SAP S/4HANA system using Transaction MIGO.
Figure 10.3 Activation of Batch Management in Material Master
10.3.2 Batch Classification
To differentiate batches of material depending on their individual properties, you use
characteristics to define the properties. For example, the paint industry uses color, cov-
erage, and viscosity as characteristics to classify different batches. The cold-roll coil
industry uses finish, thickness, and quality to classify different batches.
These characteristics are created in the SAP S/4HANA system and assigned to the mate-
rials. The same classification concept as in materials classification is applied in defining
batch classification.
Say that a company that deals with various types of paints wants to capture the charac-
teristics of paint: shine, color, and viscosity. A class has to be created with the character-
istics for coverage, color, and viscosity wavelength, and this class has to be assigned to
a material. Also, every time a new batch is created for the material, the characteristic
values need to be entered. Let’s briefly define characteristics and assign them to a class
specific to batch management.
© 

465
10.3 Master Data in Batch Management
Defining Characteristics
First, you create the various characteristics for the materials, such as thickness, viscos-
ity, density, and so on.
To create characteristics, follow menu path SAP Menu  Cross-Application Components 
Classification System  Master Data  Characteristics or use Transaction CT04. In the
resulting screen, enter the Characteristic name and choose Create. Then, enter a
description, as shown in Figure 10.4. The Status field has In Preparation, Locked, and
Released options. A Released status means that the characteristic can be used; a status
of In Preparation or Locked means that it can’t be used.
Figure 10.4 Characteristic
In the Value assignment area, select either Single-value or Multiple Values. Clicking the
Single-value radio button specifies that only one value can be assigned to this character-
istic; you use the Multiple Values radio button when more than one value is possible.
Next, select the Values tab and enter the possible values for this characteristic, as
shown in Figure 10.5. Checking the Additional Values box allows you to enter character-
istic values that aren’t defined. After defining the values, save the characteristic. In this
example, we’ve set the Thickness characteristic, and the value of thickness can be
within a range of 8.00 to 12.00 mm.

10 Batch Management
466
Figure 10.5 Characteristic Values
Now that you’ve created a characteristic, the next step is to assign existing characteris-
tics to the class, which we explain in the following section.
Defining a Class and Assigning Characteristics
A class represents a group of characteristics. Classes are defined based on the material
classification, such as garments, oil and grease, or metals. To define a class, you follow
menu path SAP Menu  Cross-Application Components  Classification System  Master
Data  Classes or use Transaction CL02. Enter the Class type as “023” (Batch) and fill in
the appropriate Class name, which in this example is “ZPP.”
Figure 10.6 Assignment of Characteristics to Class
© 

467
10.3 Master Data in Batch Management
Then, choose New. Enter a description, set the Status to Released, and enter the validity
dates. On the Char. tab, assign the characteristics to the class, as shown in Figure 10.6.
In this case, we’ve assigned three characteristics: MM02 (Thickness), MM10 (Product
Class), and MM11 (Date of Manufacture).
10.3.3 Assigning a Classification in the Material Master
After you’ve defined a class, the next step is to assign it to the material master via
Transaction MM02. Choose the Classification view for Material 2264 and enter Class
Type “023” to assign the previously created ZPP class. Doing so will automatically bring
up all three characteristics assigned to the class, as shown in Figure 10.7. Here, you can
maintain specific characteristic values pertaining to this material. Because this is a
master data step, note that characteristic values assigned are for the material master
only and not for batches of a material.
Figure 10.7 Classification View of Material Master
Now that you’ve created the requisite master data for batch management, the next sec-
tion discusses the business processes associated with batch management.

10 Batch Management
468
10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management
In the following sections, we’ll cover the business processes of batch management in
the SAP S/4HANA system. Some of these business processes include manually or auto-
matically creating a batch, changing or deleting a batch, using the classification func-
tion in a batch, and attaching documents to a batch.
10.4.1 Creating a Batch Master Manually
A batch master record for a material can be created either manually by a user or auto-
matically in the background by the system when goods are received for the first time.
A manually created batch of a material can then be assigned during stock posting, such
as during goods receipt of a material from the procurement or production processes.
To create batch master records manually, you follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics 
Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Create or use Transaction MSC1N (see
Figure 10.8).
Figure 10.8 Manually Creating Batch of Material
Enter a material, plant, and storage location to create a batch master record. If an internal
number range is defined, then the system will create the batch number automatically
© 

469
10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management
after you press (Enter). The data in a material master applies in general to all the batches
assigned to this master record. By contrast, a batch master record contains data that
uniquely identifies the corresponding batch and characterizes the unit as one that can’t
be reproduced.
Various types of information are stored in the batch master record. Some can be
updated manually, and some can be updated automatically. We’ll discuss some of the
relevant information in different tabs in the following sections.
Basic Data 1
Important fields and checkboxes in the Basic Data 1 tab shown in Figure 10.8 are as fol-
lows:
 Date of Manufacture
You can enter the date when the batch was produced into this field. In some indus-
tries, this field is also used to enter the date when the material was tested or retested.
If a material is found to be still in tolerance after the shelf life date has expired, the
material can be retested, and the date of the retest is entered into this field, in addi-
tion to a new Shelf Life Exp. Date. We recommend that you check with clients to
determine whether they need to use this field.
 SLED/BBD
The SLED/BBD (shelf life expiration date/best by date) field is where you enter the
date when the shelf life of this batch will expire. The shelf life of a product can vary
between plants. This date can be used in the sales process, as customers may have
set a requirement for the acceptable number of days of shelf life remaining. Some
companies use this field to indicate the date on when a batch needs to be retested. If
the company has implemented the QM functionality, then the system uses this field
to see if the material’s batch expiration is about to be reached so that the system can
create a quality inspection lot for retesting the material.
 Available From
This field indicates when the batch will be available. For example, if a material needs
to remain in the quality inspection process for a certain number of days after test-
ing, then the quality department can enter a date here to inform other departments
of when the batch is expected to be available.
 Batch Status
These radio buttons allow you to classify the batch as having restricted or unre-
stricted use. If you click the Unrestricted radio button, then the batch has no restric-
tion placed on its use. If you click the Restricted radio button, the batch will be
treated like blocked stock in planning but can be selected by batch determination if
the search includes restricted-use batches.
You can change the Batch Status to restricted from unrestricted by changing the
checkbox in the batch record. A material document will be posted that shows the
movement of stock between the two statuses.

10 Batch Management
470
 Next Inspection (not shown)
This date field allows the quality department to enter the date of the next quality
inspection of the batch, if applicable to this material. This field is helpful when a
company must retest a material’s batch after every predefined period. This material
retesting process is known as recurring inspection.
 Supplier Batch (not shown)
If the material is purchased, then you can add the batch number assigned by the sup-
plier to the batch record. It’s important to any product recall procedure to note the
supplier batch number here. The Supplier Batch field allows you to enter a 15-charac-
ter string.
Basic Data 2
In this tab, you can maintain the Texts and Freely Definable Date fields. There are six
date fields, and maintaining them is optional and only necessary if a user wants to use
these dates somewhere in the process. The other fields on the screen are six date fields:
Date 1, Date 2, and so on. These fields don’t have any standard functionality. The six Date
fields can be used for whatever purpose is defined by the client. For example, these fields
could contain the dates on which the material was inspected by the quality department.
Classification
In this tab, you use the class that was assigned to a material during material master
classification, and you maintain the characteristics values here by clicking the New
button (not shown). After clicking the New button, you maintain the values of all the
characteristics listed based on the material class, as shown in Figure 10.9.
Figure 10.9 Classification Tab of Batch Master
© 

471
10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management
Material Data
In this tab, you use the information from the material master record. Choosing the All
Changes icon will display the change history of the batch. This tab won’t appear when a
batch is created for the first time but will appear in the change or display batch transac-
tions (Transaction MSC2N and Transaction MSC3N, respectively).
Tips and Tricks
If there’s also a business need to link documents to a batch, then you must complete
another configuration step. Follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch
Management  Batch Master  Activate Document Management for Batches or use
Transaction ODOC.
The configuration in Transaction ODOC consists of simply switching on the field to allow
SAP Document Management service for batches. The options are Active and Inactive.
10.4.2 Changing a Batch
After you’ve created the batch, there may be occasion to amend the batch record, either
to modify a characteristic or to add a new linked document. To change a batch record,
follow menu path SAP Menu  Logistics  Materials Management  Material Master 
Batch  Change or use Transaction MSC2N.
You can make changes to the batch record, but these changes will be recorded and
available to review. The Change Batch screen has an extra tab in change mode, and it
accesses a screen to view all changes made to the batch record. The Changes screen
(refer to Figure 10.8) shows the changes made to the batch record. The information
recorded includes the user who created and changed the record, as well as the fields
that have been changed, including their values. This information is important to some
companies because a strict audit record is needed to show compliance with federal or
local regulations. In the pharmaceutical industry, companies manufacturing items
that are to be consumed are under strict regulations from the FDA. Companies should
at all times ensure that their record keeping is compliant with FDA regulations.
10.4.3 Deleting a Batch
There is no transaction used solely for deleting a batch, but a batch can be deleted
through Transaction MSC2N. The screen shown back in Figure 10.8 shows the initial
screen of the change batch transaction, which has a Batch Deletion Flag box that you
can check if the batch is to be deleted. However, checking the deletion flag box doesn’t
immediately delete the batch. Checking the box allows the batch to be processed by an
archiving program that will determine whether the batch can be deleted. If the batch
can’t be deleted, the deletion flag will remain until either the archiving program deter-
mines that the batch can be deleted or until the deletion flag is removed.

10 Batch Management
472
10.4.4 Automatic Creation of a Batch in Goods Movement
It’s possible to define the automatic creation of batch numbers in the SAP S/4HANA sys-
tem, whether for movement types or production and process orders. For instance, the
system can be customized to automatically create unique batch numbers whenever
goods are delivered against a process order. This means that every time goods with
movement type 101 are received, the unique batch number for the material received is
created by the system automatically.
Here, we present an example of how the system automatically creates a batch master
upon goods receipt with reference to a process order (movement type 101).
To receive goods with reference to a process order and via movement type 101, you go
to Transaction MIGO (see Figure 10.10). Select Goods Receipt and R08 Order from the
two dropdowns located in the top-left corner of the screen, and then enter process
order number “1002203.” In the item details, there’s one more tab: Batch. Notice that
the system has automatically created batch 0000000127.
Choose the Classification button (not shown but located further down the same screen)
in the Batch tab in Figure 10.10 to enter the values of the characteristics, as shown in
Figure 10.11.
Figure 10.10 Goods Receipt of Batch-Managed Material
On the screen shown in Figure 10.11, maintain characteristic values such as Thickness,
Product Class, and Date of Manufacture.
© 

473
10.4 Business Processes of Batch Management
Figure 10.11 Classification View of Batch during Goods Receipt

10.5 Batch Determination
The batch determination process can be implemented in one or more logistics and sup-
ply chain components of SAP S/4HANA, including in PP, QM, MM, SAP S/4HANA Sales,
and embedded EWM. Batch determination uses strategy types, search strategies, and
search procedures to identify a batch in the relevant area.
In a real-world scenario, a warehouse supervisor receives one material in a number of
batches, and this will be kept in storage location stock or in warehouse storage bins.
When the material needs to be issued to production, a strategy needs to be in place for
the system to choose different batches. Batch determination is the process of defining
the strategy in the system by which the system determines and offers the right batch
to pick. Batch determination uses the condition technique. Strategy records of the
respective application—such as PP, QM, MM, SAP S/4HANA Sales, or WM/embedded
extended warehouse management (embedded EWM)—determine batch determina-
tion. Different strategy records in the system have to be created for different purposes,
such as goods issue to production, goods issue to customer, goods issue to cost center,
© 

475
10.5 Batch Determination
and so on. Batch determination can be used in production order, process order, goods
movement, sales order/delivery, cost center, and transfer order functions.
Configurations for each application—such as production, inventory, sales, and stock
transfers—are stored in search procedures, which run the batch determination. After
the batch determination procedure is set up, the system automatically accesses the
corresponding entry of the application, which further refers to the relevant entries in
configuration.
Figure 10.13 shows how the system searches for the batch number by using the batch
determination procedure. In any transaction, the system will first determine the appli-
cation area—which will be PP and PP-PI, MM, WM and embedded EWM, or SAP
S/4HANA Sales—and then, it will determine the search procedures, which are assigned
based on different combinations (1 and 2). In the case of PP or PP-PI, the system will
determine the order type and plant from the transaction and then find a search proce-
dure (3 and 4). For MM, the system will find a search procedure based on movement
type. For SAP S/4HANA Sales, the search procedure will be based on sales organization,
distribution channel, division, and sales document type.
The search procedure helps the system determine the search type 5, and the search
type helps to identify the condition types and condition tables 6. The system will find
the appropriate batch number based on the access sequence system.

10.5.1 Defining Condition Tables
The batch determination condition table includes a number of fields that you can
select and use to record and assign values. There are five options for condition table
creation, depending on which business process your company wants to use batch
determination for:
 Production order (Transaction OPLB)
 Process order (Transaction OPLB)
 Inventory management (Transaction OMA1)
 SAP S/4HANA Sales (Transaction V/C7)
 WM (Transaction OMK4)
To define the condition table of a process order, you follow menu path SAP IMG 
Logistic—General  Batch Management  Batch Determination and Check  Condition
Tables  Define Process Order Condition Tables or use Transaction OPLB.
There are also options to define condition tables separately for each application area—
that is, production order, process order, inventory management, SAP S/4HANA Sales,
and WM and embedded EWM.
For this example, as shown in Figure 10.14, we created a new table (table 550) by copying
it from standard condition table 030. Copying a table allows you to use the batch deter-
mination procedure specific to the Order Type/Plant/Component combination.
Tips and Tricks
We recommend that you always copy any new configuration object from an existing
one and then make the desired changes to the newly configured object. Doing so not
only saves you time and effort but also reduces the chances that you’ll miss important
configuration settings that will then be unable to give the desired results in transac-
tion processing.

10.5.7 Creating Sort Rules
A sort sequence enables the system to choose batches that meet the user-defined sort
criteria of characteristics. For example, batches of a material expiring first are prime
candidates to be sorted in ascending order so they can be consumed on the earliest
possible date. This sort strategy is known as first expiration, first out. Other sort strate-
gies include first in, first out (FIFO) and last in, first out (LIFO). Any other type of sort
sequence is also possible, which we’ll soon show with an example.
First, you access the screen shown in Figure 10.24 by following menu path Logistics 
Central Functions  Batch Management  Batch Determination  Selection and Sort  Sort
Rule  Create or by using Transaction CU70.
Next, you create new sort sequence ZPP, assign previously created characteristic MM02,
and check the Ascending box. Doing so will lead to the system suggesting batches of
material with values in ascending order during the batch determination process. For
example, say there are four batches of a material with thicknesses (characteristic MM02)
of 0.6 mm, 0.5 mm, 0.9 mm, and 0.35 mm. To adhere to the ascending order of thickness
during the batch determination procedure, the system will first suggest batches of
material with a thickness of 0.35 mm, then suggest batches with a thickness of 0.5 mm,
and so on.
Figure 10.24 Creating a Sort Sequence in Batch Determination

Tips and Tricks
Before running the business process to test batch determination of a component, you
should follow the steps in this checklist:
1. Make sure the component is managed in batches (i.e., the Batch Management box
is checked in the component’s material master).
2. Ensure that the component is part of the material’s BOM, for which the process
order is created.
3. Ensure that along with sufficient stock of the component’s batches, there are also
multiple batches with different characteristic values (in this example, thicknesses)
so that the completeness and correctness of the batch determination process can
be tested.
4. Reconfirm the entry in the Batch Entry field in the Plant/Storage 1 view of the com-
ponent’s material master. For example, choose whether you want to automatically
or manually trigger batch determination upon release of a process order.
5. If the same material is batch managed in one plant but not in another, then check
or uncheck the Batch Management (Plant) box based on your business needs.
10.5.9 Business Process
First, you use Transaction COR1 to create a process order of order type YBM2 for plant
1710. Then, enter “2264” for the header material and maintain other basic process order
details, such as order quantity and dates. Next, navigate to the Component Overview
shown in Figure 10.28 and take note of the fact that component 2265 needs a total of
100 PC to produce main material 2264. Select component 2265 and then choose Exe-
cute batch determntn (batch determination). Then, the system will not only determine
the relevant batches that meet the sort sequence of the thickness characteristic but
also split the total required quantity of 100 PC into multiple batches. It will also display
a popup to inform you that while 100 PC of component 2265 are needed, only 96 PC of
various batches are available.
Here, the system has automatically determined and then assigned four batches of
material 2265: 130, 141, 143, and 144. Let’s evaluate this in more detail. The system first
evaluates the thickness sort criteria to see which batch has the lowest thickness value
and assign that batch’s quantity to the process order. However, if the assigned batch’s
quantity isn’t sufficient to cater to the complete requirement (in this case, 100 PC), the
system proceeds to the next batch with the next-highest thickness value and assigns
the batch’s quantity to the process order. The system continues in this way until multi-
ple batches in the total required quantity are assigned to the process order.

Now, let’s discuss recurring inspection and the expiration dates of batches.
10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates
The ability to inspect batch-managed goods—whether procured raw materials or pro-
duced semifinished or finished goods—before their expiration dates ensures that
there will be sufficient time and flexibility for companies to respond to any unforeseen
situations.
© 

489
10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates
Recurring inspection (also called repeat inspection) at regular intervals allows business
users to take advantage of standard functionalities to automatically create inspection
lots when the expiration dates of batch-managed goods approach. The details related
to the shelf life of materials are managed in individual batches. The batches nearing
expiration can go through quality control checks of results recording and usage deci-
sions to help managers decide whether they can still be used. This functionality offers
time and cost savings by enabling the business to act proactively and thus optimizes
the full logistics and supply chain operations of the company.
A significant challenge for any organization is to keep track of batch-managed materials
with shelf-life expiration dates. This is especially the case in process-based industries for
slow-moving materials or materials with a short shelf life. Tracking this information
allows an organization to ensure that those materials are consumed first in the case of
raw materials and sold first in the case of finished goods. This minimizes the material
and financial costs associated with the need to destroy and scrap goods or materials.
At the time of goods production, the system is able to calculate and suggest a best
before date. Similarly, upon procurement of goods, the system is able to calculate and
suggest a shelf-life expiration date (SLED). Therefore, it makes sense to ensure that
recurring inspection takes place before the goods are about to expire.
Note
Although this example shows the integration between PP and QM in the SAP S/4HANA
system, a company can still use several batch-specific functions even if QM isn’t yet
implemented.
We recommend that you engage a PP consultant to ensure end-to-end integration
among MM, PP, and QM functionalities for the batch-managed business processes cov-
ered in this section.
This section outlines the important master data needed to successfully set up recurring
inspection. We’ll show how to manually create an inspection lot that triggers the recur-
ring inspection program, and we’ll also touch on an automatic trigger option. For the
QM part of the master data setup, we recommend that you engage a QM consultant to
cover the following QM-specific master data setup.
10.6.1 Quality Management Master Data Checklist
For recurring inspection to work correctly, you need to set up the following QM master
data:
 Activation of recurring Inspection Type 09 in the material master
 Inspection plan usage 9 (Material Check)
 Status set to 4 (Released)

10 Batch Management
490
In the following sections, we’ll discuss how to activate the recurring inspection type, as
well as the steps required to set up an inspection plan and set its status to Released.
Activation of Recurring Inspection Type in the Material Master
Although the material may have been created previously, its QM view may still need to
be activated. If the QM view is already activated, then you need to activate the new
inspection type for recurring inspection at the organizational level (plant). To activate
the recurring inspection type, follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management 
Material Master  Material  Create (General)  Immediately or use Transaction MM02.
Then, enter material “2264” and choose the Select View(s) button. Select the Quality
Management view and then choose Organizational Levels. Enter plant “1710” and
choose Continue. This leads to the Quality Management view of the material master
Here, you should maintain an Inspection Interval of 10 days to denote that material
inspection is due every 10 days. In the case of recurring inspection, the system refers to
the information maintained in this field to calculate the next inspection date. Choose
Insp. Setup, and in the ensuing popup, maintain Inspection Type 09 for recurring
inspection. Also make sure to check the Active box to activate this inspection type.
Back in the Quality Management view, this time, you should choose the Plant Data/
Stor. 1 view. The screen shown in Figure 10.32 will appear, and it will show the Shelf life
data area, in which you should maintain the shelf-life data, including the minimum,
maximum, and total shelf life of a material. While calculating or updating the SLED, the
system looks for the date of manufacturing against the production/process order or
the date of goods receipt against the purchase order. It then adds up the days or weeks
in the period, such as the days or weeks from the total shelf life, to come up with the
SLED or the best before date. In this example, the system adds 20 days from the Total
shelf life field to the date of manufacture to suggest a SLED or best before date. For the
SLED or best before date calculation, the system considers calendar days.

10.6 Recurring Inspection and Expiration Dates
Tips and Tricks
The maximum storage period of a material should have the longest duration. The total
shelf life of a material must be greater than its minimum remaining shelf life. Further-
more, it makes sense for an inspection interval (see Figure 10.33) to be less than the
total shelf life of a material.
Figure 10.33 Inspection Interval in QM View of Material Master
Batch Master
Let’s look at some of the details of the material’s batch, which is about to expire. When
materials are batch managed, it’s always the batch—not the individual material—that
expires. To change the batch, you follow menu path Logistics  Materials Management 
Material Master  Batch  Change or use Transaction MSC2N. Enter Material “2264,” Plant
“1710,” “171A” for the Storage Location where the batch is stored, and material Batch
number 129. Then, press (Enter) and the screen shown in Figure 10.34 will appear.
The Next Inspection field is of paramount importance for recurring inspection.
Together with the inspection interval that we previously covered (in the QM view of the
material master), the system calculates the next inspection date whenever a business
user records the usage decision of an inspection. Here the next inspection date is 07/
24/2024, and inspection should occur every 20 days. Upon executing the recurring
inspection program, which we’ll cover next, the system adds another 20 days to this
date. In reality, the system comes up with a date that is one day earlier than the date
maintained in the Next Inspection field. This is to ensure that the quality inspector can
inspect the material, record the results, and decide on the material’s usage (make the
usage decision) at least a day before the product is set to expire.

a process or process order. If this batch contains material (e.g., raw material) received
against a procurement process, then at the time of recording its goods receipt, the system
prompts the warehouse person to enter the date of manufacturing mentioned with the
accompanying supplier’s quality certificate or supporting documents, such as a packing
list. On executing the recurring inspection program, the system looks for the data main-
tained on shelf-life expiration to come up with a new SLED or best before date.

The next section is on the configuration, master data, and business process of active
ingredient management in SAP S/4HANA.
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
Active ingredient management in batch management enables organizations to take
advantage of material valuation at the batch level, as well as the potency or active propor-
tion in the overall quantity of material. This functionality is especially helpful when
you’re using an active element or ingredient of a material that contains carrier materials,
including impurities. If the total physical quantity of a material is a mixture, then the
active or potent material within the mixture is known as the active ingredient quantity.
This section begins by covering configuration basics and important master data that
forms an integral part in running the end-to-end business processes that involve active
ingredient management. Finally, for business processes in active ingredient manage-
ment, we run an entire procurement process that consists of creating a purchase order
for an active ingredient material, moving to goods receipt, recording results, making
usage decisions, and posting stock.

10 Batch Management
496
10.7.1 Configuration Basics
In this section, we’ll list the most important settings and steps necessary to implement
batch ingredient management in procurement.
Activating Active Ingredient Management
To activate ingredient management functionality at the client level, you use Transac-
tion OMWS or follow menu path SAP IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management 
Batch-Specific Material Units of Measure  Activate Batch-Specific Material Units of
Measure. You choose the Active radio button to ensure that batch management is
active and then save your settings.
Creating a Unit of Measure for Proportion Units
For batch-specific units of measure, you need to add a characteristic to the batch class
that carries the conversion factor for deriving the alternative batch-specific unit of mea-
sure. You’ll likely need to create this unit of measure before proceeding further, but if
the unit of measure already exists in the system, then you can continue to the next step.
To create a unit of measure for an active ingredient that should act as a proportion unit
of measure, you use Transaction CUNI or follow menu path SAP IMG  SAP NetWeaver 
General Settings  Check Units of Measurement. Choose the Units of Measurement (no
dimensions) button and then choose Create. For this example, on the screen shown in

10.7.3 Business Processes
Let’s now proceed to the actual business processes of active ingredient management.
We first create a purchase order of the same material (2242) and record its goods
receipt, and then, we perform results recording, which consists of recording the details
of the actual ingredient proportion, making a usage decision, and posting the mate-
rial’s stock. We also show how the SAP S/4HANA system keeps updating the informa-
tion in each step of the business process.
Purchase Order
Before you create a purchase order for material 2242, quickly refer to Figure 10.50,
which shows the purchasing view of the material where you set the (purchase) Order
Unit to be the AIU. If you create the purchase orders in active ingredient quantities, the
system updates the purchase order quantity and physical quantity in the purchase
order history. In this way, you can ensure greater control of and visibility into the
degree of order quantity fulfillment based on active ingredient quantities.
To create a purchase order for material 2242 that contains an active ingredient mea-
sured in the AIU proportion, use Transaction ME21N or follow menu path Logistics 
Materials Management  Purchasing  Purchase Order  Create  Supplier/Supplying
Plant Known. Enter Supplier “EWM17-SU01,” Material “2242,” a PO Quantity of “100
AIU,” a Net Price of “100 USD,” and a Plant of “Plant1 US.” (You can also enter plant code
1710, in which case, the system will use the plant’s description instead of its code.) In the
Quantities/Weights tab, the system automatically converts not just the purchase order
quantity into a stock-keeping unit (which is kg, and in which you maintain the mate-
rial’s inventory) but also shows the conversion factor between order units and stock
keeping units. This is the same information that you maintained previously in Figure
10.50.
Choose the Delivery tab and the screen in Figure 10.55 appears. It accounts for the fact
that the quantity of an active ingredient that the supplier actually delivers may vary
slightly from the quantity ordered. Therefore, you need to maintain the tolerances for
under- and overdelivery. For this example, check the Unlimited box to denote unlim-
ited overdelivery; for underdelivery, enter 20%. Then, you save the purchase order, and
in this example, the system creates a purchase order with the number 4500001882.

Displaying the Purchase Order History
Let’s now look at the updated purchase order history and check out the physical and
financial adjustment entries.
To display the updated purchase order history, use Transaction ME23N. On the Item
Detail subscreen, choose the Purchase Order History tab (see Figure 10.63). The system
displays not just the original goods receipt with movement type 101 but also subse-
quent adjustments of the purchase order using movement type 142. It also updates the
purchase order quantity to 76.471 AIU by subtracting (deducting) a quantity of 23.529
AIU from the ordered and delivered quantity of 100 AIU. Similarly, the financial value is
reduced by 2,352.90 USD to come up with the revised value of 7,647.10 USD.
Figure 10.63 Displaying Purchase Order History
Let’s now delve into how and why the system made these stock and financial adjust-
ments. As explained earlier, 100 kg of material is expected to have a planned propor-
tion of 85 AIU (85%). In this case, and after results recording, the actual percentage of
© 

515
10.7 Active Ingredient Management
the active ingredient was found and recorded to be 65%. Hence, the system undertakes
the following calculation:
((100 AIU × 0.65) ÷ 0.85) = 76.471 AIU
This is the quantity of the active ingredient that the supplier has delivered and should
accordingly be paid for. Because it costs 100 USD for each unit (AIU) of material, we get
100 USD/KG × 76.471 KG = 7,647.10 USD. Also, the delivered percentage of the active
ingredient for the material is less than expected (65% actual, as compared to 85%
expected), so the system uses movement type 142 to make necessary quantity and
financial adjustments. Had this been the other way around, with the supplier deliver-
ing a greater proportion of the active ingredient than ordered (any active ingredient
percentage greater than 85.00%), the system would have made use of movement type
141 to make positive adjustment entries.

The next section is on batch derivation, which entails an automatic transfer of the clas-
sification data of one batch to another batch of the same or different materials, thereby
saving you time and effort, as well as minimizing data entry errors.
10.8 Batch Derivation
With batch derivation, a user can efficiently and automatically transfer a batch’s charac-
teristics values from the sender material’s batches to the receiver material’s batches,
thereby saving time, data entry effort, and cost. Batch derivation enables a user to main-
tain one-to-one, one-to-many, and many-to-one relationships between the sender’s and
the receiver’s materials for complete visibility of information and data.
Whenever a batch-managed material is processed in the production process, its charac-
teristics change and the batch-managed material attains different physical and chemi-
cal attributes. These changed attributes are maintained as characteristic values of the
© 

519
10.8 Batch Derivation
batch-managed materials. With batch derivation, you can control how the system man-
ages the characteristic values flowing from a sender material to a receiver material.
In this section, we’ll start by covering the basic configuration settings needed to activate
batch derivation. Then, we’ll move on to the master data you need to set up, including
settings in the material master, classification, and batch derivation–specific settings.
Finally, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process, in which we show how the settings
made for master data are reflected in the batch derivation process. For this example, the
sender material is 2265 (a raw material that is consumed during the production process
to produce finished good 2264) and the receiver material is 2264 (a finished good).
Let’s start with the configuration basics of batch derivation.
10.8.1 Configuration Basics
You activate batch derivation in the configuration settings by following menu path SAP
IMG  Logistics—General  Batch Management  Derivation of Batch Data  Activate
Batch Derivation or by using Transaction DVSP. On the screen that appears, select the
Derivation Active radio button and save the settings.
In the following sections, we’ll cover the end-to-end business process of setting up

10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
10.9 Batch Traceability of Work in Process Batches
Batch traceability is a key functionality that’s used in almost all industries, including in
discrete manufacturing. SAP delivers the batch where-used list (Transaction MB56) in
the SAP S/4HANA system. This where-used list enables business users to trace the
batch from the finished product to the raw material (from the top down) or from the
raw material to the finished product (from the bottom up). The WIP batch is used to
keep the batch traceability for a process order when several batches of raw material are
consumed and several batches of a finished product are generated.
Transaction MB56 works well in most cases. However, there is a limitation on a process
order with N:N batches. In a standard process order, there is no link between the raw
material batch consumed and the finished product’s batch received in the warehouse;
therefore, the batch where-used list determines all the batches of raw material for each
batch of the finished product. Imagine, for example, that a problem was detected in a
raw material batch after production. In this case, it isn’t possible to identify exactly
which batch of finished product was produced with this problematic batch of raw
material. In previous releases, the only solution for this scenario was to create separate
process orders for each raw material batch, but that required more data entry, ineffi-
cient business processes, and a significant amount of manual work.
In this example, we focus on the WIP batch functionality that is available in the system
for production and process orders in SAP S/4HANA. More specifically, we explain how
to enhance the batch where-used list to show a link between each raw material batch
consumed and the respective finished product batch produced in a process order. Our
example involves a simple process order with two operations and a few components.
In the following sections, we start with the necessary configuration needed to set up
WIP batches, followed by the required master data setup. Then, we transition to the
business processes involved in WIP batches, and we conclude by using the Batch Infor-
mation Cockpit reporting tool to trace WIP batches.
10.9.1 Configuration Settings
The following are the configuration steps you need to perform before creating the con-
firmation profile.
Activating Work-in-Process Batch Business Functions
Two business functions are related to the WIP batch functionality that you must acti-
vate by using Transaction SFW5: LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH and LOG_PP_WIP_BATCH_02. For the
basic use of the WIP batch and for the scenario covered here, you only need to activate
the first business function. However, you should activate the second business function
as well because it brings additional functionalities for the WIP batch, such as inventory
valuation, use with documentary batches, and use on an order split.

10 Batch Management
530
To activate the business function using the Switch Framework (Transaction SFW5),
select the business function and choose the Activate Changes button.
Warning
You can’t deactivate these business functions after they’re activated.
Activating the Work-in-Process Batch
After you activate the business function, you also need to activate the WIP batch func-
tionality in configuration. To complete this step, use Transaction OMCWB, choose the
Active radio button, and then save.
Warning
Once you activate this configuration setting, it can’t be reversed. In other words, when
a user allows the use of the WIP batch functionality in the SAP system, it isn’t possible
to get back to the original state in which WIP batches can’t be used.
Activating the Original Batch
The next configuration step is to activate original batch reference material. To com-
plete this step, you execute Transaction OMT0. On the initial screen that appears (see
Figure 10.81), check the Original Batch Reference Material box and then save. Doing this
enables the system to use this functionality in master data.

10.10 Batch Information Cockpit
In process industries, such as food and pharmaceuticals, traceability of the ingredients
or components within a production or procurement process is of paramount impor-
tance. Batch traceability across logistics and supply chain processes is also a legal
requirement in many industries and a good manufacturing practice to maintain in
case there is a batch recall. Companies often develop complex custom reports to view
and analyze batch traceability when the Batch Information Cockpit can serve the same
purpose.
With the Batch Information Cockpit, you can do the following:
 You can perform top-down and bottom-up traceability analysis. Top-down traceabil-
ity analysis of a finished good traces all the raw, packaging, and semifinished mate-
rials that were used in making the finished product’s batch. With bottom-up trace-
ability analysis, you trace the ingredients or raw materials and trace all the finished
goods that were made using these ingredients or raw materials batches.
 You can access the batch master directly from the Batch Information Cockpit to dis-
play and change information related to the batch while performing the traceability
analysis of the batch.
 You can display the stock information for the batch.
 You can display the stock position of a material and its associated batches.
To access the Batch Information Cockpit, follow menu path Logistics  Central Functions 
Batch Management  Batch Information Cockpit or use Transaction BMBC. Figure 10.94
shows the initial screen where you set parameters that the system then considers to
bring up relevant batches of materials. Notice the various tabs available on the right-
hand side, which you can use to enter specific information. The left-hand side of the
screen provides search functionality.

then Execute Selection. This brings up the screen shown in Figure 10.95, where the left-

10.11 Electronic Batch Record
In highly regulated industries, such as pharmaceuticals, there’s often a legal require-
ment to maintain a complete trail and record of all the batches used in the logistics and
supply chain of manufacturing a product. Often, it’s not just important to keep records
on the batches received from the procurement process or batches consumed in the
production process; you must maintain a complete record of various quality inspec-
tions that took place in the manufacturing process. The electronic batch record, or batch
record for short, captures these inspection details.
You access the screen shown in Figure 10.97 by following menu path Logistics  Central
Functions  Batch Management  Tools  Batch Record or using Transaction COEBR. On
the left-hand side, you can see a complete trail for material 2264 and its associated
Batch 136. As you drill down in the same node, the related process orders will appear: (1)
the two types of quality inspections (04 for finished goods inspection and 03 for in-pro-
cess inspection) if the PP-QM integration is in place and (2) the Material List (BOM) used
in the production process.
In the upper-right corner of the same screen, you can only approve an electronic batch
record for archiving with the Approve button if the statuses of various elements (e.g.,
process or process order, inspection lots) are set as required for electronic archiving.
Here, the system also guides you to check all the prerequisites for electronic batch
records. After approval, you can archive an electronic batch record by clicking the
Archive button.
© 

543
10.11 Electronic Batch Record
Figure 10.97 Electronic Batch Record
The lower-right corner shows comprehensive details of the electronic batch record in
PDF format.
Note
We recommend that you engage an SAP NetWeaver consultant to ensure that the nec-
essary authorizations required for archiving electronic batches are granted. The SAP
NetWeaver consultant will also set up a document repository required to store the
electronic batch records being archived.
In some industries, such as consumer products and automotive suppliers, there are
legal requirements to store where-used data of materials used for production and
delivered to customers. Enabling recall actions is a mandatory and critical issue for
these industries. Data can be recorded by managing all relevant materials in batches,
but this method has a negative impact on data volume. When turning on batch man-
agement, entering a batch number becomes obligatory for all goods movements. Then
all inventory postings must be executed on the batch level, and labor costs for inven-
tory management will increase.
With the documentary batch record, it’s important to ensure that a partial stock of a
material is traceable without having to maintain a batch’s data. This reduces the effort
and complexities involved in maintaining batch data. The documentary batch record
finds greater use in automotive and consumer goods industries but is unsuitable for
use in dangerous goods.
You can perform documentary batch processing in the following ways:
 You can enter documentary batches during goods movements.
 In a process order, you can enter documentary batches only at backflush.
 In embedded EWM, you must enable entering documentary batches during trans-
port order confirmation.

10 Batch Management
544
10.12 Summary
Batch management is important to a large number of process-based industries. It has
evolved from just the identification of groups of items into a process that allows com-
panies to perform product recalls, select and sell by batch characteristics, and identify
expiring stock. As the drive for competitive advantage continues, companies will fur-
ther investigate how batch information can lower production time and hasten delivery
materials to customers.
Chapter 11 will discuss sales and operations planning.
©

chapters.title: Sales and Operations Planning
chapters.text:
545
Chapter 11
Sales and Operations Planning
Sales and operations planning strives to maintain a balance between
demand planning and operations planning, the latter of which takes ini-
tial stocks, machine capacities, and constraints into account to finalize a
realistic production plan. A planner can use standard sales and opera-
tions planning to come up with a feasible production plan, or they can
use flexible planning if there are complex and diverse planning needs.
Sales and operations planning (S&OP) is an iterative form of business process manage-
ment, in which a planner uses several planning scenarios and versions until arriving at
a production plan. A planner can then confidently use that plan in production, procure-
ment, and capital investment processes.
With the complexities involved in global supply chain management processes, there
has never been a greater appreciation for ensuring effective and efficient use of S&OP.
S&OP is mainly about sales-driven forecasting and consolidating forecasts, and it uses
only neutral key figures (numbers) for that. In contrast to this, production planning
(PP)—including material requirements planning (MRP) and capacity requirements
planning—is all about production, and it takes into account requirements instead of
key figures. Still, MRP in SAP S/4HANA doesn’t consider capacities, but capacity
requirements planning does. For a value-based plan, a planner needs to transfer sales
or production figures to profitability analysis.
The biggest incentive to implement S&OP in a company comes from its ability to let a
planner evaluate various what-if models and perform scenario planning in simulative
modes, before passing on the finalized operations plan to demand management in the
form of planned independent requirements (PIRs). Forecasting plays a major role in
helping the planner arrive at a plausible operations plan.
In this chapter, we’ll start by giving an overview of what S&OP is, and then, we’ll intro-
duce flexible planning, which a planner can use as an alternative. We’ll explain all func-
tions and tools that are a part of these planning types, and we’ll do a walkthrough of
how to work with them and interpret their results. We’ll also cover a lesser-known and
used application of flexible planning, known as standard analysis reporting. Standard
analysis in flexible planning uses all the concepts and fundamentals that we discuss in
this chapter.
Now, let’s start with our overview.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
546
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
The following sections give an overview of S&OP and the similarities and differences
between standard S&OP and flexible planning. They then move on to cover forecast-
ing, and they conclude with coverage of standard analysis in flexible planning.
11.1.1 Overview
In standard S&OP, a planner can plan individual materials or a group of materials
known as a product group. A product group consists of individual materials or other
product groups, and it enables a planner to define the proportion factor (percentage) for
each material in the overall product group. It also offers the option to aggregate and dis-
aggregate at various planning levels. If the planning processes in a company are rela-
tively simple and straightforward—that is, if they are restricted to individual materials
or a group of materials—then standard S&OP can fulfill the company’s business needs.
Example
Consider the following business scenario: Before each financial year begins, the sales
and production teams spend countless hours working on planning figures that are
acceptable to both. The sales team uses forecasting tools to arrive at next year’s sales
figures (targets), based on historical data. The production team looks at things differ-
ently. They evaluate whether they are able to meet the demand for sales with existing
production capacities or not. The procurement planner needs a better understanding
of how the production figures will impact the procurement process: do the vendors
even have the capabilities to meet the supply requirements of the company? The
inventory controller (of the warehouse) is concerned about whether there is enough
space in the warehouse to manage and store the produced quantities. The manage-
ment of the company is interested in not only increasing the profitability of the prod-
uct (and at the same time, reducing costs) but also gaining a broader understanding of
the capital tie-up involved and whether new investment (e.g., capacity enhancement,
increasing the number of working shifts) is warranted. Furthermore, knowing which
products (or groups of products), regions, or markets can bring in greater revenue for
the company also helps in the decision-making process.
This is where S&OP can help. In general, the entire planning exercise entails the cre-
ation of various planning versions in the system and adjusting sales or production fig-
ures until there is a mutual consensus. The finalized planning figures, which usually
reflect a production plan, are sent forward to demand management, where they
appear as planned PIRs with an active version. When the system runs the MRP, it con-
siders the active PIRs to arrive at procurement proposals (for in-house production and
external procurement).
In the planning process, the teams need a planning table in the SAP S/4HANA system
that can take all of the important planning considerations into account and at the
same time account for the dependencies of various factors on others.
© 

547
11.1 Standard Sales and Operations Planning
Flexible planning functionality, while very sophisticated, is also a tool that is slightly
complex to manage. But if a planner can gain a better understanding of flexible plan-
ning, they can reap higher dividends from it, and it will help them produce much more
realistic planning figures, which their company can use to reap greater financial bene-
fits. For example, with flexible planning, a planner can configure a planning layout
(known as a planning table) of important key figures; include self-defined macros to
manage complex calculations; perform forecasting (which is also possible in standard
S&OP); take into account special events (e.g., trade shows, the Olympics) for increases
in sales or take into account natural calamities (e.g., droughts, floods) to factor in
decreases in sales (or increases, if their company manufactures relevant products); and
gain a broader understanding of commitments (known as pegged requirements), such
as capacities, materials, and PRTs.
When you’re deciding which S&OP option (standard S&OP or flexible planning) to use,
it makes sense for you to evaluate business requirements and at the same time strive
to maintain simplicity and a straightforward approach to business processes. As men-
tioned already, standard S&OP offers a very limited set of functions, but being almost
completely predefined, it enables you to immediately start taking advantage of those
functionalities without any configuration. In general, we recommend that first, you
should try to cover your business process with standard S&OP, and then, if important
functions are still missing, you should consider using the rather complex and sophisti-
cated flexible planning tool instead. Flexible planning offers full functionality but
requires configuration, many presettings, and definitions.
SAP offers several standard analysis reports in all logistics areas, and they consist of
characteristics and key figures for a period. A characteristic value combination is the
combination of characteristic values with which a planner wants to plan. Characteris-
tics can be materials, plants, sales organizations, distribution channels, or purchase
organizations. Key figures (values or quantities) can be quantity produced, quantity
procured, operations quantity confirmed, production scrap (quantity), invoiced value,
or purchasing value. The period can be an interval, such as six months.
For example, standard analyses bring forth information such as the purchase and
invoiced values (key figures) of all the materials (characteristics) during the past six
months (the period). The standard analysis reports that SAP offers for each logistics
functional area have predefined information structures with no option to add new key
figures. However, you can create your own self-defined information structure in flexi-
ble planning by choosing the desired key figures from several available catalogs. A cat-
alog consists of a large number of characteristics of and key figures from a specific
application area, such as PP QM. Furthermore, you can even define how frequently the
system should update the values of key figures in flexible planning standard analysis.
Hence, standard analysis in flexible planning isn’t just applicable to the PP functional-
ity; the concepts and details we cover in this chapter are equally applicable to other
functionalities such as QM, PM, MM, and SAP S/4HANA Sales.

11 Sales and Operations Planning
548
Note
See Chapter 19 for more on reporting, including standard analyses in PP.
Our example of flexible planning from both perspectives (flexible planning and stan-
dard analysis in flexible planning) in this chapter remains primarily focused on SAP
S/4HANA Sales and its integral correlation with PP processes.
Note
Regardless of whether your company implements standard S&OP or flexible planning,
we encourage you to read the entire chapter because several features and functional-
ities are applicable to both planning types, and we eventually cover them all (not neces-
sarily in the same section). For example, we cover the forecasting functionality within
flexible planning, but it’s also available and can be used in standard S&OP for a material
or material group. We’ve also dedicated a separate section to forecasting. This is also
true for events and rough-cut planning. An event tends to have an impact on planning
figures, and a rough-cut planning profile provides better visibility into capacity, mate-
rial, and PRT situations. Similarly, we cover aggregation/disaggregation in standard S&
OP but not in flexible planning, although the option is available in both planning types.
Figure 11.1 Process Overview of Sales and Operations Planning
Demand Management
Planned Independent Requirements
in Demand Management
Sales and Operations Planning

Analysis (CO-PA)

Operations
