15.2 Setup
 BOM
A valid BOM with the BOM usage set to 1—Production should exist for a material that
will be used in a simulation.
 Work center
The work center used in a simulation should have at least one capacity relevant for
finite scheduling.
 Routing
A valid routing should exist, and the operation in the bottleneck resource operation
should be relevant to capacity planning.
 Production version
At least one valid production version referring to the BOM and routing should exist.
If there is more than one valid production version, then you can change the source
of supply during the simulation.
 Purchasing info record
A purchasing info record is a one-to-one material-to-supplier relationship that is
needed for the pMRP process so that the system can assign at least one source of
supply to each material that is procured.
 Planned independent requirements
PIRs for the relevant materials must be maintained.
All of this data is also used by MRP for planning materials.
Before you start to use pMRP, all of the relevant master data must be available. As one
of the main objectives behind pMRP is to allow the user to check whether there is
enough capacity to produce the forecasted quantities, it’s very important to correctly
set up the routing and the work center with the standard values and the proper formu-
las.
From a transactional data perspective, the main input for pMRP is a demand plan,
which is represented by PIRs (Transaction MD61). The planning strategy used isn’t rele-
vant because it supports any requirement type, and you can use an active or an inactive
PIR version. When choosing an inactive version, the capacity planner will need to select
the version number during simulation creation.
15.2 Setup
The first step in the pMRP process is to create a pMRP simulation, wherein you as a
planner can choose to restrict a set of master data for simulation purposes. When you
create a simulation, you can select which top-level materials, components, or work cen-
ters will be considered for pMRP. In pMRP, the planning will happen during the simula-
tion creation itself. With pMRP, you will be able to process the simulation immediately
after the creation is finished and will not have to wait for a planning run. The pMRP

15 Predictive Material and Resource Planning
840
process uses simplified planning logic, in which all materials use a deterministic MRP
and a lot-for-lot procedure.
Before creating the simulation, you must ensure that the relevant master data has been
created. You must also have created the PIRs, since they will be the main input into the
pMRP simulation. The system will plan the selected materials and determine the work
center capacity requirements based on those PIRs.
A word of caution here: when a simulation is created, master data will be copied into it,
so subsequent changes to master data may not be considered.
Creating a pMRP simulation is basically a background job that you can schedule
through the Schedule pMRP Simulation Creation app. You can also use this application
to manage background jobs that are already finished, scheduled, or in process.
As shown in Figure 15.2, an icon in the Log column will show the status of the finished
background executions, and another icon appears in the Results column. Choosing the
icon in the Log column will show the details of success, warning, or error messages that
may have been triggered during each step of simulation creation.
You can create, copy, and delete a simulation creation (although deletion is only possi-
ble for scheduled jobs). To create a new application job, choose Create on the screen
shown in Figure 15.2, which will bring up the screen shown in Figure 15.3.
Figure 15.2 Application Jobs
On the screen shown in Figure 15.3. you can use the dropdown list in the Job Template
field to choose from the following three job templates, which have different selection
criteria:
 Creation of pMRP Data via Top-Level Materials
In this template, object selection is based on the material master, so the selection cri-
teria will be focused on material master information, such as material number, MRP
controller, and low-level code. The system will look for PIRs for the relevant materi-
als and run simplified MRP to determine the required quantity for each level and the
capacity allocated in the work center.

15.2 Setup
 Creation of pMRP Data via Components
Selecting this template will enable you to select components as inputs for creating
the simulation. In this case, pMRP will use the BOM to find relevant upper-level
materials and look for demands for these materials.
 Creation of pMRP Data via Work Center
In this template, object selection is based on the work center, so the selection criteria
will be focused on the work center. The system will look for capacity requirements in
the selected work centers and then identify the top-level materials and the respec-
tive PIRs.

15.3 Managing pMRP
In the Process pMRP Simulations app, you as a planner will actually work with the sim-
ulation, analyze the planning results, identify and resolve capacity issues, and validate
the forecast you’ve created. Once the simulation is processed, you can release the
results to operational planning in the form of PIRs. Then, the forecast can become rele-
vant to MRP.
When you open this app, it will display a list of all the created simulations, including
the total number of capacity issues, the delivery performance, and the simulation sta-
tus. These appear in separate columns. The number of capacity issues represents the
total number of buckets in which a capacity issue was identified, and the delivery per-
formance represents the percentage of the originally planned forecast that can be ful-
filled. For example, if a capacity issue is found and the forecast quantity was reduced to
resolve the issue, then the delivery performance will be adjusted accordingly.
The Simulation Status column is related to the results of the simulation creation; if the
status is Created with Warnings or Fatal Errors, you can refer to the simulation creation
logs shown in Figure 15.7.
Figure 15.7 Processing pMRP Simulations
In this application, you also have the option to copy, rename, or delete an existing sim-
ulation or override errors. When you copy a simulation, it will be linked to the same ref-
erence data as the original simulation. Also, you can choose whether to copy data from
the simulation or from the reference data, as shown in Figure 15.8. If you choose to copy
from the simulation, then changes already made to the simulation will also be copied;
if you choose to copy from the reference data, changes made to the simulation will not
be considered and the original forecast quantities from the reference data will be cop-
ied. You can also add a percentage for the Overload Tolerance value so that pMRP will
not consider a work center overload until reaching the tolerance.

15.4 Releasing Results to Operative Material Requirements Planning
Once the simulation process is complete and you’ve identified and resolved all of the
capacity issues, or if you as a capacity planner decide to disregard them or compromise
on delivery performance, then you can release the pMRP simulation. You accomplish
this by choosing the Release button on the initial screen of the Process pMRP Simula-
tions app. This brings up a popup (see Figure 15.21) in which you can confirm whether
the simulation can be released, and the forecast will be transferred to the top-level
materials in an inactive version 01, even if you created the simulation with active PIRs
as references. The option to transfer PIRs into an active version also exists in the same
©

15.4 Releasing Results to Operative Material Requirements Planning
popup (see Version Active: Yes). Also, as mentioned at the beginning of this chapter, in
the latest version of SAP S/4HANA, DDMRP can also consider PIRs for the buffer calcu-
lation. This means that the pMRP simulation results will be an input for DDMRP. When
the simulation is released, if there are DDMRP materials in the BOM’s lower levels, then
PIRs will be created for DDMRP materials, even if there was no PIR for those materials
before the simulation creation. Therefore, the buffer calculation will take into consider-
ation any demand variability that can be anticipated by the forecast.

15.5 Using Flexible Constraints
15.5 Using Flexible Constraints
The pMRP functionality allows you as a planner to define flexible constraints for prod-
ucts. For example, you can define that a maximum quantity of a certain material can be
produced or purchased in any given time period (week or month). If this quantity is
exceeded, pMRP will generate an alert when processing the pMRP simulation. You can
also define flexible constraints for a single material or multiple materials, and you can
used them for materials produced in house, externally procured materials, or stock
transfer materials. The available periodicities are weeks and months.
To define a flexible constraint, you use the Define Flexible Constraints app (see Figure
15.25 1). In this example, we use the same material, 2269, in plant 1710, and we have
defined a constraint that whenever the production quantity is more than 75 pieces (PC)
during the period from August 2024 to February 2025, then the system should consider
this constraint while processing pMRP. When you’ve scheduled the pMRP and you pro-
ceed to process it, the system will show the Violated Constraints (see Figure 15.25 2).
Figure 15.25 Flexible Constraint

Since pMRP is an interactive tool, any changes made during processing will immediately
be reflected. For example, in 2, if you reduce the quantity of Material 2269 below 75 PC,
then the system will accordingly update (reduce) the status of the violated constraints.
Clicking on the Violated Constraints field will bring up detailed information on all the
constraints that have been violated.
15.6 Summary
The pMRP functionality is the latest innovation in SAP S/4HANA, and it offers simula-
tion capabilities that can be used by production and capacity planners to make fore-
casting from a work center capacity point of view both practical and achievable. With
pMRP, a supply chain planner can change forecasted quantities and evaluate the
results, test different sources of supply for different products, and consider the prepro-
duction of certain critical products to avoid a capacity overload in a specific month. The
pMRP functionality is still in the development phase, and more innovations are
expected to be delivered in future releases of SAP S/4HANA.
The next chapter covers capacity requirements planning, which includes capacity eval-
uation and capacity leveling.

Chapter 16
Capacity Requirements Planning
Apart from materials availability, capacity availability plays a central
role in the entire production process. Capacity requirements planning
helps companies ensure that the relevant resources become available at
the required time for optimum and uninterrupted production.
Building on the concepts and understanding that you developed in Chapter 3 and
Chapter 6 for capacity requirements planning, this chapter focuses on the short-term
capacity planning after a production planner creates or releases production orders.
Medium- to long-term capacity planning was covered by the chapters on sales and
operations planning (S&OP in Chapter 11) and predictive material and resource plan-
ning (pMRP in Chapter 15). The capacity requirements planning details for discrete
manufacturing that we cover in this chapter are equally relevant and applicable to pro-
cess manufacturing and, to a certain extent, repetitive manufacturing (REM). For
example, when you decide to assign relevant profiles for capacity evaluation or capac-
ity leveling, the system offers profiles with REM in the naming convention for REM and
with PI in the naming convention for process manufacturing. For discrete manufactur-
ing, the profiles have a naming convention that includes SFC (for shop floor control).
Because the system uses the same principles that we cover in this chapter in plant
maintenance and project systems, the available profiles have naming conventions that
include PM and PS, respectively.
We’ll start this chapter with an overview of the processes involved in capacity planning
and then cover capacity requirements and capacity evaluations. We’ll cover both stan-
dard and variable capacity evaluations. Next, we’ll cover how capacity requirements at
different work centers can be combined and how to perform real-time checks on capac-
ity availability, either during order creation or order release.
Then, we’ll move on to cover dispatching and the associated profiles, the dispatching
sequence, sequence-dependent setup, and midpoint scheduling. We’ll also cover how
to use the mass processing function to expedite business processes, such as dispatch-
ing and deallocation. Finally, we’ll cover some of the more important features that are
available in the graphical and tabular versions of capacity planning tables.

16 Capacity Requirements Planning
858
16.1 Process Overview
The objective of capacity requirements planning is to schedule operations within the
availability capacity and to check their feasibility when creating logical sequences of
operations.
You can implement capacity requirements planning for production orders and for
planned orders from MRP. You can also make the relevant configuration settings to
enable the system to consider planned orders in capacity planning. This option works
best if your company’s business processes are mature enough and there’s significantly
high confidence in the planning results that MRP creates. Because there are often fre-
quent and at times high numbers of last-minute changes between planning and actual
production and execution, it may not be wise for you to book capacities based only on
planning results. If you do that, you may compromise your company’s capacity avail-
ability, which in turn may have an impact on your company’s actual production sched-
ule. At the same time, you may have a genuine business need to convert planned
orders into production orders earlier so as to book capacities based on production
orders. Still, others may prepare a comprehensive and feasible production plan based
only on planned orders.
Apart from scheduling operations, capacity requirements planning involves two
important processes:
 Capacity evaluation
A capacity evaluation compares available capacities with capacity requirements,
with reference to the work center or specific production order.
 Capacity availability check
A capacity availability check uses the same information to check whether the avail-
able capacity at the requisite work center is sufficient to manage the operation of the
production order. Depending on the configuration settings, the system can perform
a capacity availability check during the creation of a production order or during its
release. If there is insufficient capacity, three options are available:
– The system won’t create or release the production order.
– The system will create or release the production order.
– The system will prompt the production planner to decide on creating or releasing
the production order, despite the capacity shortage.
Note
Refer to Chapter 6, in which we covered available capacity.
If there is insufficient capacity, or to account for capacity restrictions, the system per-
forms finite scheduling to look for periods when sufficient capacity is available. Insuffi-
cient capacity at the work center leads the system to rescheduling the operation, but
© 

859
16.1 Process Overview
you as a production planner will still have to perform the dispatch function to give a
formal go-ahead to proceed with production at the given work center. The dispatch
function can take place either from the capacity planning table or from the tabular
capacity planning table. If there is an incorrect or inadvertent dispatch, you can inter-
actively deallocate the operation.
Upon dispatch, the system performs the following four functions:
 It determines the dispatch dates.
 It creates the dispatching sequence.
 It performs finite scheduling of the operation to be dispatched.
 It performs midpoint scheduling of nondispatched operations of the production
order.
Upon dispatch of an operation, the system assigns it the status DISP (dispatched) and
no longer schedules the operation automatically.
Note
Implementing capacity requirements planning makes greater sense in companies that
have a diverse range of products and that also want to ensure that all of their products
are produced in sufficient quantities for availability in the market. If a company pro-
duces products that use up all of its available capacities or produces individual prod-
ucts such as fertilizer, cement, caustic soda, or iron bars, then implementing capacity
requirements planning may not add much value to its business processes.
During SAP S/4HANA system implementation, you should discuss and evaluate how
implementing capacity requirements planning will improve your business processes
and then decide whether to implement capacity requirements planning or not.
Capacity management consists of a sequenced system of repeatable tasks. Each of these
tasks serves to balance resource availability with the capacity load it has to cope with.
Capacity management can be broken down into the following phases (see Figure 16.1, in
which the broad arrows denote the capacity management process):
 Planning
 Sequencing
 Leveling
 Scheduling
 Evaluation
Say that a production scheduler is tasked with scheduling a pool of orders within a cer-
tain time frame. The scheduler must first put the orders into a sequence according to
some sort of criteria (maybe heijunka or setup optimization) and then level them on
the work centers or production line as available hours permit. For sequencing, many

16 Capacity Requirements Planning
860
options are preconfigured and available in the SAP S/4HANA system. Orders can be
sorted according to setup optimization, first in, first out (FIFO), or heijunka, and there
are many more options that can be customized. For any given period, the scheduler
saves a sequence record, which is used in the next step for distribution within available
capacity. Leveling uses the order’s load and the work center’s availability to figure out
at what specific point in time the order will be placed.
Figure 16.1 Capacity Management
Two Invaluable Rules of Planning
For production and capacity planners, following two specific rules in capacity planning
will bring tremendous sanity to the production process:
 There is a point in time at which planning activities must end.
 Plan resources in the medium and long term and manage demand. Plan resources
and sequence in the short term. Then, schedule and manage the supply of materi-
als required for production.
Now that you have a basic understanding of how capacity requirements planning
works, let’s delve into the details you need to know to understand how capacity
requirements planning works in the system, alongside the relevant business require-
ments.
16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation
The system reflects the day-to-day business processes in place, including creation and
release of production orders, confirming them, delays in production, and the extra time
it takes to set up or process the product in capacity requirements. Capacity evaluation

16.2 Capacity Requirements and Capacity Evaluation
enables you as a capacity planner to view the capacity load or overload at various work
centers with a large number of standard and variable evaluation options. It’s essential
for you to understand these two business processes—capacity requirements and capac-
ity evaluation—before you get started with the actual capacity requirements planning
process. We’ll discuss them in more detail in the following sections. We’ll cover how to
understand the capacity requirements that the system creates, how to evaluate them,
and the steps that you can take for capacity leveling.
16.2.1 Capacity Requirements
As already mentioned, the system uses the settings that you already configured in
Chapter 3 for capacity requirements planning and also the information that you
entered in the PP master data, such as the work center and routing from Chapter 6.
Here, we briefly cover these details.
The system determines the capacity requirements during lead time scheduling if the
Det. Cap. Reqmnts option in the control key has been set. Furthermore, the system
takes into consideration the formulas that were entered in the work center, the default
values in the routing, and the order quantity. It’s important to emphasize that the for-
mulas for scheduling aren’t used in capacity requirements planning. For each compo-
nent of an operation, such as setup, processing, and wait times, there is an earliest and
latest date. The schedule formula is set to one of the operation dates. In the standard
settings, this is the latest setup start date or processing start date, if the operation
doesn’t provide for any setup time. An operation can span several time periods, and
because the system bases the capacity evaluation on this information, the distribution
key determines how the system distributes capacity to different periods.
The distribution key itself consists of the distribution strategy and the distribution
function. The distribution key defines the operation dates and the basis upon which it
carries out the distribution—for example, the factory calendar, the Gregorian calendar,
or the operation time. You can maintain the distribution key, distribution strategy, and
distribution function through menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Require-
ments Planning  Operations  Capacity Requirement  Distribution.
The system determines the capacity requirement during lead time scheduling and has
to go through the capacity availability check and finally the dispatching process. If the
system confirms the operation during the capacity availability check, it assigns the
capacity requirement with the system status BSTKZ. It does the same when an opera-
tion is dispatched. In this way, the system converts the capacity requirement into a
component called a basic load.
The basic load has an important role to play in the capacity availability check because
available capacity represents the difference between the available capacity and the
basic load. The system reduces the capacity requirements when any of the following
tasks are performed:

16 Capacity Requirements Planning
862
 Confirming an operation
 Setting the status of a production order to technically complete
 Locking the production order
 Setting the deletion flag in the production order
The system also updates the capacity requirements when you partially confirm an
operation, and it correspondingly reduces part of the capacity requirements for the
operation.
16.2.2 Standard Evaluation of Capacity Utilization
The system offers several methods of capacity evaluation. Each standard evaluation
has its own transaction code, whereas for variable evaluation, a user selects the rele-
vant profile to enable the system to display the requisite information.
In standard evaluations, the system displays the capacity requirements in direct com-
parison to the available capacities. The following are some of the standard capacity
evaluations:
 Standard overview (Transaction CM01)
The system aggregates the load on a weekly basis.
 Detailed capacity list (Transaction CM02)
The system shows the load per order and week.
 Pool of orders/operations (Transaction CM03)
The system presents only the load of released operations.
 Overload (Transaction CM05)
The system only shows work centers with overload.
 Extended selection, work center view (Transaction CM50)
The system displays an aggregated view of the daily load.
 Extended selection, order view (Transaction CM52)
The system displays an aggregated view of the weekly load for all work centers that
the selected orders occupy.
To access the capacity planning evaluation, you follow menu path Logistics  Produc-
tion  Capacity Planning  Evaluation  Work Center View  Load or use Transaction
CM01. Figure 16.2 shows the standard overview (load) of the Work center ASSEMBLY and
Plant 1710 combination. It shows the capacity requirements for each calendar week that
result from the creation or release of production orders. The initial screen also shows
the available capacity (AvailCap.) of the work center and the resulting percentage of
load (CapLoad), as well as the remaining available capacity (RemAvailCap).
For example, in calendar week 28/2024, a capacity requirement of 166.23 hours leads to
a utilization (or an overload) of 415.6% based on an available capacity of 40.00 hours.
The system displays the periods with an overload in red.

Warning
The utilization rate on the production lines should never exceed 100% for an extended
period of time. It shouldn’t even get close to that because the closer it gets to full utili-
zation (100%), the less a capacity planner can cope with variability in the process. It’s a
fact that when capacity utilization approaches 100%, variability multiplies. However,
the urge to let it approach 100% comes from the standard model of finance or costing
departments that asks for maximum utilization of resources because the business
should make as many products or units as possible, with the available capacity used to
spread the overhead costs thinner over more units and make the individual product
cheaper.

16.2.4 Cumulating the Capacity Requirements
For evaluation purposes, especially when large numbers of orders at various work cen-
ters are taken into consideration, it’s often helpful to cumulate the capacity require-
ments. This provides a broader overview of the capacity situation at each work center.
Capacities can be cumulated either statically or dynamically from work center hierar-
chies. When statically cumulating the capacity, the system only refers to the work cen-
ter and its available capacity. To carry out capacity evaluation in cumulated form—that
is, to cumulate both available capacity and capacity requirements—capacity require-
ments planning must be cumulated dynamically.
In the Hierarchy area shown previously in Figure 16.10, options to cumulate capacities
dynamically and the Hierarchy work center for the plant are available.
16.2.5 Checking Capacity Availability
When a production order is created, the system can check capacity availability in the
order before you dispatch it. This step helps a production planner to decide whether it’s
possible to dispatch the order or not. The system considers period-based information
by checking it to find out whether the available capacity in the period of operation is
greater than or equal to the capacity requirement of the operation. The length of the
period is set in the period profile, which we’ll cover later in this chapter. The shortest
possible period is one day. When the system performs a period-based check, it only
indicates that the capacity is available (or unavailable) within the given period, not on
the planned operation date.
The available capacity represents the difference between the permitted capacity over-
load defined in the work center and the basic load. The basic load doesn’t contain all of
the capacity requirements. By default, only dispatched operations and those for which
the capacity has been confirmed affect the basic load. You can display the basic load by
using the variable evaluation (Transaction CM07) and profile SAPSFC010.
Tips and Tricks
If no capacity availability check has been carried out over a long period of time, or if it’s
being used for the first time, then we recommend that you create the basic load on the

16 Capacity Requirements Planning
870
basis of the existing (unchecked) capacity requirements by using Transaction CM99. In
this case, all capacity requirements will be confirmed.
Often, due to delays in actual production, the system creates a backlog (e.g., noncon-
firmed operations from the past). Check the Overall Capacity Load box in the strategy
profile and the backlog dispatching date (BacklogDispDate 0; refer to Figure 16.10) to
define whether to include them in the determination of the available capacity. We cover
strategy profiles in Section 16.4.2. If these are included, the capacity requirements of the
past will be temporarily scheduled forward into the current period and, if necessary,
into subsequent periods and added to the basic load.
An operation can last for several periods. In this case, the distribution key determines
the way in which the system distributes the capacity requirement to different periods.
The capacity check uses the distribution key that is assigned to the evaluation profile
(Section 16.4.2). The capacity availability check is carried out for each individual order,
and all operations are checked one after the other, according to their numerical order.
A restriction on this capability is that only those operations are checked that exist in
work centers that are relevant to finite scheduling. Therefore, it makes sense to define
critical work centers (work centers with bottleneck capacities) as finite to ensure that
there are enough degrees of freedom in the capacity planning.
You can check capacity availability interactively while creating a production order or
releasing it. Alternatively, you can perform an interactive capacity availability check
from the production order, as shown in Figure 16.11.

16.3 Finite Scheduling
If the available capacity is insufficient to meet the capacity requirement of an opera-
tion on the requested date, you can use the finite scheduling option to find the next
possible date on which the available capacity is sufficient for the operation. You can
perform finite scheduling during either the capacity availability check or dispatching.
While performing finite scheduling, the system refers to the strategy profile to search
for the direction in which to perform the scheduling (backward or forward scheduling).
A search in the direction of today’s date is based on backward scheduling and rep-
resents the standard case, whereas a search into the future is based on forward sched-
uling. However, much of this also depends on the company’s business processes and
needs, in which only forward scheduling is considered to look for free or available
capacity in the future. If insufficient capacity is found in one direction, then the search
direction can be reversed.
The screen shown in Figure 16.16 appears during the capacity availability check. In the
next capacity overload popup, you choose the Finite scheduling icon (not shown).

Then, the system will propose the new latest date (NewLtStart) and time (NewStTim)
after it performs finite scheduling. At this stage, you as a capacity or production planner
can confirm the capacity by checking the Cap.confirm. box and then choosing Adopt.

16.4 Dispatching
In the dispatching process, the system creates a workable or feasible production plan in
which sufficient capacity is available. For this process to be successful, there needs to be
a sequence of dates set up for the individual operations in the work centers. The non-
dispatched operations form the pool of orders and operations for the capacity planner/
production scheduler to work on. To dispatch an operation, the capacity planner
selects an operation that is ready for dispatch from the capacity planning table or from
the tabular capacity planning table. They then initiate the dispatching function.
In this section, we’ll go over the process steps for dispatching and then get into the
details of the different profiles, sequencing, and setup.
16.4.1 Process Steps
When dispatching takes place, the system carries out the following steps:
 Determine dispatching dates
The standard setting uses the operation dates as dispatching dates. However, a plan-
ner can also enter dates interactively or define the dispatching date using drag and
drop in the capacity planning table.
© 

877
16.4 Dispatching
 Create dispatching sequence
If a planner wants to dispatch several operations in one step, then they create a dis-
patching sequence based on specific sorting criteria (Section 16.4.3) or optimal setup
times. The default settings stipulate dispatch at the latest date.
 Finite scheduling if no capacity is available
If no capacity exists on the dispatching date, then the system needs to run finite
scheduling to come up with a feasible dispatching date.
 Dispatch operation
The system dispatches the operation and assigns it the DISP status (if successful).
 Midpoint scheduling
A planner needs to adjust the dates of the nondispatched operations in such a way
that the system carries out the midpoint scheduling process for them. In midpoint
scheduling, the system schedules the nondispatched operations so that earlier ones
are scheduled backward and later ones forward. Midpoint scheduling is covered
later in Section 16.4.5.
Note
It may be impractical from a particular practice or business point of view, but you as a
planner can also base the dispatching processing on an infinite capacity assumption.
To do this, you deselect the finite scheduling option in the strategy profile. However,
with bottleneck capacities at critical levels, work centers hardly ever permit this option.
During dispatching, you as a planner may realize that it’s not possible to meet the basic
dates of the order. Because the system carries out dispatching for each operation, you
can also compromise on the sequence of operations for the dispatch. For this, you use
the strategy profile to define the extent to which the system allows inconsistencies
within the operations of an order. However, the capacity planner will need to monitor
the external dependencies, such as the date-based dependency between orders and
customer requirements, to ensure timely production.
Whenever a planner performs the dispatch or the deallocate function, the system
updates the planning and the scheduling logs. In fact, it issues a message to inform the
capacity planner to check the planning and/or scheduling logs. The planning log can be
called from the respective application—for instance, from the capacity planning table
or from finite scheduling during the interactive capacity availability check. Figure 16.18
displays a sample planning log that includes two information messages and one warn-
ing message.

16.4 Dispatching
The Scheduling control parameters shown at the top of the screen allow you to make
the following settings:
 Set whether to carry out finite scheduling during dispatching. If the Finite schedul-
ing parameter isn’t set, the dispatch process assumes that unlimited capacity is
available. If there is insufficient available capacity, then the dispatching attempt
becomes unsuccessful.
 When the Dispatch at earliest point in time parameter is used, the system doesn’t
consider interdependencies with other operations and dispatches the operation as
early as possible.
 The Dispatch at best time for setup parameter chooses the dispatch date on the basis
of the existing setup sequence in such a way that the increase in the overall setup
time is kept as small as possible. This entails the system having the provision to shift
the already dispatched operations. To attend to this need, you also set the Insert
operation parameter. The system supports this function in the capacity planning
table, and it makes sense only if setup matrices for sequence-based setup are in
place. We explain the setup matrices later in Section 16.4.4.
 The Date entry when dispatching parameter ensures that instead of the system
using the operation date during dispatching, it will bring up a popup window and
prompt the user for an interactive date entry.
 The default setting for the planning direction is backward from the dispatch date.
However, this setting can be changed when the Plan. direction forwards parameter
is activated. When the system is unable to find available capacity within the plan-
ning horizon, and when the Change planning direction parameter has also been set,
the system will continue to search for available capacity in the opposite direction.
 The Planning in non-work periods parameter overrides the nonwork periods in the
work center and suggests 24-hour capacity availability with a full rate of capacity uti-
lization in the dispatch process.
 The Close gaps parameter enables the system to automatically close gaps that are
caused by deallocations or rescheduling. This again entails rescheduling of already
dispatched operations and is only possible on the capacity planning table.
The parameters in the Dispatching functions area at the bottom of the screen define
which options are available for dispatching:
 Sort operations to be dispatched determines how to create the right sequence of
operations if a planner wants the system to dispatch several operations simultane-
ously.
 The Consider operation sequence in the order parameter enables the system to
adhere to the sequence of operations in the production order. When this parameter
is used with forward scheduling, the system doesn’t dispatch the operation prior to
its predecessor. Similarly, with backward scheduling, the system makes sure that it
doesn’t dispatch the operation after its successor.

 If the Operation date check is used, a planner can check whether the new operation
dates lie within the floats that can be selected using the following parameters:
– Use operation floats
– Use float bef. prod. (use before production)
– Use float aft. prod. (use after production)
 If you as a planner want the system to immediately perform the midpoint schedul-
ing process, then you select the Midpoint scheduling parameter. When you select

 Setup time optimization creates the dispatching sequence according to the setup
time minimization criterion.
 If the Cancel dispatching due to error parameter is set, the system doesn’t allow dis-
patch of operations outside the floats.

The selection profile enables you as a planner to define which capacity requirements
the system should consider for dispatch. To create or make changes to the selection

Control Profile
The control profile defines how the system presents the data. It can be presented con-
tinuously over time for the capacity planning table, or it can be based on periods for the
tabular capacity planning table. An important parameter of the control profile defines
whether a planner can modify the operations and at which stage the system should set
the locks to avoid any further changes to the operations.
To create or make changes to the control profile, you follow the configuration menu

Extended Evaluation  Define Control Profile or use Transaction OPDE (see Figure 16.24).

IMG  Production  Capacity Requirements Planning  Capacity Leveling and Extended
Evaluation  Strategy  Define Evaluation Profile or use Transaction OPD3.
Time Profile
The time profile contains the time horizons for importing data records, as well as for
evaluation and planning. The planning horizon must be shorter than or equal to the
evaluation period, which in turn must be shorter than or equal to the database read

16.4.3 Dispatching Sequence
In general, a planner will carry out the dispatching function manually, individually,
and interactively. But when there’s a large and drastic change in the production plan,
which also greatly impacts the operations involved, it makes sense to dispatch several
operations according to specific rules. The easiest and most straightforward rule that
most companies follow is to perform dispatching according to the start dates and
times of operations. Similar rules can be implemented by using the sorting function,
whereas the layout key enables a planner to implement sorting from a large number of
available fields.
You can set the layout key by using Transaction CY39 in Customizing (see Figure 16.26).
On this screen, you select the fields that you as a planner want to sort and then eventu-
ally make available in dispatching. After selecting the fields, choose the Sequence/
Heading button.
The field details allow you to manage the sorting in ascending or descending order.
Then, the layout key is assigned to the strategy profile.
© 

885
16.4 Dispatching
Figure 16.26 Layout Key for Sequence Creation during Dispatching
Tips and Tricks
In addition to the layout key, you can use user exit CYPP0001 to sort the capacity
requirements. We recommend that you engage an ABAP resource to define the param-
eters in the user exit.
The sequence in which the operations are dispatched may not necessarily match the
sequence in the work center, as certain conditions of the dispatching process can trig-
ger the system to dispatch some operations differently. For example, if a long-duration
operation doesn’t fit into any gap that has been created on the basis of operations
already dispatched, the system will dispatch that long-duration operation further in
the planning horizon.
16.4.4 Sequence-Dependent Setup
In some production processes, the setup time depends on the sequence in which the
product is produced or on the physical state of the machine left behind after an opera-
tion. For example, if an automotive company has a paint shop and it paints doors and
hoods in a light color such as gray or white first and then a dark color such as dark blue
or black afterwards, this might not be that critical because the setup time for dark

16 Capacity Requirements Planning
886
might be of standard length. But if the dark color is painted first and the light color after-
ward, the setup time for gray might be much higher because workers may need to clean
the paint shop machines very thoroughly to get rid of the dark color.
You can configure the dependency of setup work on a processing sequence by assign-
ing the setup groups in respective operations of the routing. The assignment takes
place at two levels: the setup group category and the setup group key. A setup group
category can contain several setup group keys; for example, a setup group category can
be color, and the setup group keys can be black, white, and yellow. The fact that the assign-
ment occurs at two different levels has the advantage of enabling both detailed main-
tenance between setup group keys and less detailed maintenance between setup group
categories.
Setup Group Categories
You can configure group categories and setup group keys by using Transaction OP43.
Figure 16.27 shows the list of setup groups that are available for Plant 1710.
Figure 16.27 Setup Group Category
Select the Setup group category folder and then double-click the Setup group key folder
on the left-hand side of the screen. Finally, in the operations details of the routing (in
Transaction CA02), you assign the setup group categories and keys (see Figure 16.28).
You can maintain the setup time between the setup group categories and the setup
group key in a setup matrix via menu path SAP IMG  Production  Capacity Require-
ments Planning  Master Data  Routing Data  Define Setup Matrix or by using Transac-
tion OPDA. The setup matrix is unique for each plant.

to 25 minutes. Maintaining a setup matrix enables you to assign individual operations

Setup Time
When a planner is using the capacity planning table, the system offers the following
planning functions for planning with sequence-dependent setup times:
 Interactive change of default values
 Automatic adjustment of default values
 Dispatch at best time for setup
 Setup time optimization
In the capacity planning table, you as a planner can also make automatic or manual
changes to the setup time by following menu path Functions  Adjust Setup Time  Man-
ually. In the screen that appears, make changes to the setup time in the Setup field.
The setup time can also be increased or decreased. When making this setup time
adjustment, the system carries out a new scheduling process. If an operation was
already dispatched, the system deallocates it when the setup adjustment takes place.
With automatic setup time adjustment, the system calculates the setup times of oper-
ations on the basis of their sequence and reschedules them accordingly. To carry out
the automatic setup function, you first set the Plan. Direction Forward and Insert Ope-
ration parameters in the strategy profile. To close gaps that occur when the system
reduces the setup times, you also set the Close Gaps parameter in the strategy profile.
When you’re using the Dispatch at Best Time for Setup function, the system doesn’t try
to dispatch the operation at the predefined dispatch date. It will do this at a point when
the overall setup time is minimal for all operations increases. To use this function, you
set the Dispatch at Best Time for Setup and Insert Operation parameters in the strategy
profile and make sure that the parameter for the Setup Time Optimization dispatch
function is not set.
To enable itself to propose an optimum time for setup, the system compares the dis-
patch times of operations with each other. If several optimal setup times exist, the sys-
tem uses the latest of the optimal setup times. During dispatch, the system doesn’t
adjust the setup time of operations according to the sequence of operations. Instead, it
requires an automatic setup time adjustment in the next step. To dispatch several
operations at the same time, the system uses the dispatch sequence defined in the
strategy profile.
Setup time optimization establishes an optimal setup sequence for a group of ope-
rations. The system dispatches the sequence in the work center without gaps and also
© 

889
16.4 Dispatching
automatically adjusts the setup times. The setup time optimization function is useful
for redesigning the planning because it ensures that the sequence of operations is opti-
mal even after the dispatch. But if the dispatch period contains operations that have
already been dispatched, the system deviates the new sequences from the previously
determined optimal sequence.
For setup time optimization, you select the Setup Time Optimization and the Plan.
Direction Forward parameters in the strategy profile.
16.4.5 Midpoint Scheduling
Midpoint scheduling behaves in the same way as lead time scheduling, except that the
basis of scheduling isn’t the order start or finish date. Instead, it starts with the date of
the dispatched operation of the order and carries out a backward or forward scheduling
process from the operation, according to the planning direction that you’ve defined in
the strategy profile. The system considers the other dispatched operations of the order
as fixed, and the dispatch occurs for each individual operation. During the dispatch of
an operation, the dates of the operation can change. In such cases, you can use mid-
point scheduling to adjust the dates of nondispatched operations to the changed dates
of a dispatched operation.
For example, if the dispatch of only the second of three operations took place, then
midpoint scheduling can adjust the date of the nondispatched operations, which are
the first and third ones. See Figure 16.30 for midpoint scheduling of three operations of
an order.
Figure 16.30 Midpoint Scheduling

ality that’s available in capacity planning. To maintain the mass processing settings,

16.5 Capacity Planning Table
Two types of capacity planning tables are available in the system: graphical and tabular.
Our focus will primarily remain on the graphical capacity planning table, but all of the
concepts and functions available in the graphical capacity planning table are also avail-
able in the tabular capacity planning table.
The highly interactive and intuitive nature of the graphical capacity planning table
enables a capacity planner to perform several activities and functions:
 Make changes to an order.
 Make changes to an operation.
 Make changes to capacity.
 Dispatch operations.
 Deallocate operations.
While we’ll discuss the final two functions in the preceding list in individual sections,
there are relevant options available on the graphical capacity planning table for all the
remaining change functions. Some of these change functions that we’ll discuss in the
following sections are making real-time changes to production or process orders, mak-
ing changes to operations, and making changes to available capacities.
16.5.1 Changing Orders, Operations, or Capacity
To provide an example of the change functions, let’s say you as a planner want to make
changes to a specific order. First, you choose Change Order, and the system will navi-
gate you to the production order change screen (Transaction CO02), on which you can

Capacity Planning  Leveling  Work Center View  Planning Table (Graphical) or use

16.5.2 Dispatch Operations
On the capacity planning table, there are two ways to dispatch operations. You can call
the dispatch function by clicking Dispatch from the menu bar, or you can drag and drop
the operation using the mouse. In both cases, you first select the operations that need to
be dispatched from the pool of orders/operations. The screen shown in Figure 16.33 dis-
plays the dispatch using the dispatch function in the menu bar. In the lower-right pane,

The dispatch date is the scheduled operation date, which by default is the latest start

dispatch date. If capacity is unavailable on the dispatch date, finite scheduling deter-

16.5.3 Deallocation
The deallocation process is similar to the dispatching process. You can deallocate an

pool of orders/operations pane.

In the sequence-dependent setup, we’ve already covered the fact that the system

the strategy profile by navigating to Settings  Strategy. You can also make temporary
changes to the selected strategy profile, and these changes then apply to the dispatches
that are carried out immediately after that. However, the system won’t store these set-

If the available capacity is insufficient, you can increase it from within the capacity

The planned increase in material stock can be displayed in the capacity planning table.

Depending on how the capacity master data is set up, several operations can occupy a

a multiple commitment. Choose Edit  Multiple Commitment  Show to display the
multiple commitments of the selected work centers.

16.6 Summary
Capacity requirements planning helps a planner ensure that the system plays its part
in making important business decisions, from dispatching the right operation in the
right work center to evaluating the possibilities of increasing a shift or an interval. It
can also help ensure that the system will perform finite scheduling if there is a capacity
shortage or midpoint scheduling to account for on-the-ground production and capac-
ity realities.
The next chapter discusses the production planning and detailed scheduling function-
ality.
© 

897
Chapter 17
Production Planning and
Detailed Scheduling
The primary objective of the production planning part of production
planning and detailed scheduling (PP/DS) is to ensure that the right
product is available at the right time and in the right place to be deliv-
ered to customers and to ensure that the raw materials are available for
production. The detailed scheduling part of PP/DS supports the sequenc-
ing and scheduling of production operations by taking into consider-
ation the available resource capacity (machines and human resources)
and the availability of materials. In addition, detailed scheduling
ensures optimal usage of resources.
SAP S/4HANA offers a new PP/DS area, which means that the PP/DS functionality is now
part of the SAP S/4HANA core and there’s no longer a need to have an SAP Advanced
Planning and Optimization (SAP APO) system to use PP/DS. The embedded PP/DS func-
tionality offers most of the features present in PP/DS in SAP APO. All PP/DS transactions
can be accessed directly in SAP S/4HANA. In addition, new SAP Fiori apps for capacity
monitoring, capacity planning, and detailed scheduling are available, and most existing
PP/DS transactions can be accessed through the SAP Fiori launchpad. Embedded PP/DS
also ensures master data harmonization, and creating sources of supply has been sim-
plified.
In embedded PP/DS, the core interface (CIF) has also been simplified. The CIF is respon-
sible for integrating PP/DS with SAP S/4HANA. Originally, CIF integration occurred
through remote function calls (RFCs) and inbound and outbound queues in which data
was processed when received or sent. But now, because PP/DS runs on an SAP S/4HANA
core, there’s no longer a need to use RFCs to integrate data between systems, which
means simplified integration, synchronous commits, and consistent data between the
two systems. Immediate error handling in most situations is possible. The new MRP
Live, introduced in SAP S/4HANA and already covered in Chapter 13, is now also capable
of planning with PP/DS heuristics. Due to the embedded nature of PP/DS in SAP
S/4HANA, the material master and work center creation is also synchronous. In other
words, a material or a work center that has just been created in SAP S/4HANA can be
immediately available for planning in PP/DS.

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
898
In the following sections on PP/DS, it will instantly become clear that many of the con-
cepts, foundations, and pieces of knowledge developed in this book will be equally
applicable to PP/DS processes. For example, by now, you’re familiar with concepts
related to work centers, BOMs, routings, and production versions. Similarly, you’re
familiar with pegging, PIRs, demand management, stock/requirements lists, and MRP
Live, among other concepts. So, in the interest of giving maximum coverage to the
topic under discussion—that is, PP/DS—we won’t review these topics here. Instead,
we’ll only discuss topics or definitions that are relevant to PP/DS and that require more
detailed coverage to provide you with understanding of PP/DS itself.
Furthermore, while it’s true that PP/DS is now embedded in SAP S/4HANA, you still
need to set up and configure the CIF, even when the source and the target SAP systems
are the same—that is, SAP S/4HANA. Similarly, to use the PP/DS optimizer tool to opti-
mize production flow, you still need to set it up in SAP S/4HANA. Finally, due to the fact
that SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS use the same or similar terminology (such as planned
orders and PIRs), the following sections will specify whether any given element belongs
to SAP S/4HANA or PP/DS.
The following are some of the key features in PP/DS that we’ll cover in this chapter:
 Production planning heuristics
 Interactive planning tools
 Detailed scheduling heuristics
 The PP/DS optimizer
 Interactive, detailed scheduling tools
 Background planning and scheduling
tools
 Planning procedures
 Pegging relationships
 The alert monitor
 The plan monitor
In the following sections, we adopt the same approach to covering PP/DS in SAP
S/4HANA that we’ve followed throughout. We’ll cover the basic configuration needed
to set up PP/DS, and then we’ll cover master data specific to PP/DS. Next, we’ll put the
PP/DS master data to use by covering the associated PP/DS business processes. We’ll
also discuss heuristics, the production planning run, and the optimizer. In the last sec-
tion, we’ll cover the alert monitor and the plan monitor. Finally, we’ll discuss reporting,
including SAP Fiori apps that are relevant to PP/DS.
Let’s begin with the necessary PP/DS configuration.
17.1 Configuration
You need to perform a number of steps before you can start using embedded PP/DS in
SAP S/4HANA. These include checking the relevant business functions and other con-
figuration settings.
© 

899
17.1 Configuration
Embedded PP/DS was introduced as business function SCM_PPDS_ON_ERP, and it should
be active by default on an SAP S/4HANA system. Still, we recommend that you confirm
whether this business function is active by executing Transaction SFW5 and checking
whether the traffic light is green on the screen the system displays. To activate embed-
ded PP/DS, you need to check the Advanced Planning box by following menu path SAP
IMG  Advanced Planning  Basic Settings  Activate Advanced Planning and Scheduling.
Note
All PP/DS configuration options are available via menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Plan-
ning.
With embedded PP/DS, there’s no need to create integration models to transfer trans-
action data between SAP S/4HANA and PP/DS or vice versa. You can activate the trans-
fer of the transaction by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Basic Set-
tings  Activate Advanced Planning and Scheduling  Settings for Data Transfer. Then,
you can set the order types that are relevant for advanced planning as relevant for
transfers between PP/DS and SAP S/4HANA (see Figure 17.1).
Figure 17.1 Order Types for Transfer
Embedded PP/DS needs to have the model and version set up. Model 000 and version
000 are the locations in which the active planning data products and resources
relevant to advanced planning are stored. You dan also create additional simulated

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
900
planning versions for assessing the planning situation. You create the model and ver-
sion via Transaction /SAPAPO/MVM (see Figure 17.2). Here, you maintain the PP/DS
Horizon in Days as a time frame during which the system will automatically be able to
create, change, or delete receipts. Outside of the PP/DS horizon, you can only plan
receipts manually.
Figure 17.2 Model and Planning Version Combination
Tips and Tricks
The system doesn’t recognize any PP/DS transaction that’s entered in the command
bar. Hence, you must precede all PP/DS transactions with “/N” and then enter the PP/DS
transaction code. For example, you should enter “/N/SAPAPO/MVM” in the command
bar instead of “/SAPAPO/MVM.” To avoid repetition, we’ll denote the relevant PP/DS
transactions in this chapter without including “/N.”
Next, check the Change Planning Active box and then make the following key settings:
 PP/DS Horizon in Days
The PP/DS horizon in days defines the number of calendar days within which the
PP/DS system is allowed to create, change, and delete procurement proposals during
a planning run execution. Beyond the PP/DS horizon, you can create procurement
proposals manually by using the PP/DS transactions. There is no option to set any
material-specific planning horizon in embedded PP/DS, and all the advanced plan-
ning products will use the PP/DS horizon defined in the planning version settings.
© 

901
17.1 Configuration
 Determine Priority
In PP/DS, you can assign priorities to the procurement proposals (e.g., planned
orders). These order priorities are used in PP/DS functions such as scheduling heu-
ristics to reduce the delays in manufacturing an order that has higher priority.
 Take Safety Stock into Account
Safety stock is the requirement element used in the plan to cover any unplanned
demand that may result in a stock-out situation. PP/DS supports safety stocks in
planning.
 No Order Without Source of Supply
In PP/DS, when orders are created, the source of supply, such as a production data
structure or an external procurement relationship (e.g., an info record), is assigned
to the orders. If no suitable sources of supply are found, the orders can still be cre-
ated, but the order dates can’t be correct because the source of supply determines
the time it takes to procure or produce a material. In the planning version, you
define whether to create orders when no suitable source of supply is available and
how the system should react when no source of supply is determined.
Next, you activate the business transfer event (BTE) application indicator. You must
activate this indicator to trigger the transfer of the relevant data into the embedded
PP/DS. In Transaction BF11, you check the ND-APO box to set the flag (see Figure 17.3).
Figure 17.3 Activating ND-APO Checkbox
Although you’re not required to create integration models for the transaction data
transfer, you still need to maintain basic settings for the CIF. In addition, you need to
create certain master data objects such as purchasing contracts, info records, and
scheduling agreements in an integration model to send those objects to the PP/DS side.
Therefore, maintaining the basic CIF settings is an important step of using embedded
PP/DS. You can change basic settings for creating the system landscape and for includ-
ing SAP S/4HANA with system type SAP_APO and the corresponding release so that an
integration model can point to itself (act as its own system and client) and logical sys-
tem and queue types can be maintained. You maintain basic CIF settings by following
menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning  Integration via Core Interface.

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
902
You must also maintain global settings and defaults for PP/DS, and you do it via Trans-
action /SAPAPO/RRPCUST1 or by following menu path SAP IMG  Advanced Planning 
Global Settings  Maintain Global Parameters and Defaults (see Figure 17.4).
Figure 17.4 Global Parameters and Default Values
Let’s discuss some of the important fields and data transfer options shown in Figure
17.4:
 Number Range Number
This optional setting determines whether orders created in PP/DS contain a number
that corresponds to the PP/DS number range. As soon as an order is transmitted to
SAP S/4HANA via CIF, the order receives an order number in SAP S/4HANA that is
based on the SAP S/4HANA number range. The order number is then transferred to
PP/DS. You need to define a number range for PP/DS orders in advance, and you can
assign it here.

17.1 Configuration
 Transfer to R/3 In-House Production
Here, you define whether planned orders or purchase requisitions are transferred to
SAP S/4HANA or whether the transfer occurs only after conversion in production
orders or purchase orders.
 Planning Run
The settings you make here are relevant if no other explicit settings are made within
specific applications.
Note
Notice the available-to-promise (ATP) categories for PP/DS. You use these to define the
receipt and requirement elements of production and detailed planning. They are
already present in the delivery system, so you specify a category only if the executing
system isn’t an SAP S/4HANA system (see the ATP Categories tab in Figure 17.4).
The MRP-based detailed scheduling scenario is a special form of process modeling that
uses PP/DS in the context of production order processing in a targeted manner. Before
the advent of embedded PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA, basic material requirements planning
(MRP) was supposed to be done in SAP S/4HANA, while PP/DS was supposed to be used
only for detailed scheduling. Against this background, MRP-based DS in master data
modeling doesn’t require the transfer and mapping of routings and BOMs in produc-
tion data structures or production process models, and it can omit the associated
implementation effort (in production versions and associated integration with change
transfers).
You can consider manufacturing order networks from SAP S/4HANA (in the direct pro-
curement/in-house production special procurement key) in a PP/DS capacity planning
in this scenario. Within the scope, you can implement scheduling and retransfer of the
new time schedule to SAP S/4HANA when converting planned orders into production
orders. A special feature is that this conversion is done locally in SAP S/4HANA by way
of exception; but when the production orders are posted, the earlier PP/DS scheduling
takes effect nevertheless. Additional changes and transfers can follow iteratively. To
make the relevant configuration settings, you access the screen shown in Figure 17.5 via
Transaction CFDS and select the relevant options to automatically transfer or not
transfer some or all planning and scheduling data from PP/DS to SAP S/4HANA.
You can also set another retransfer of the planning result between PP/DS and SAP
S/4HANA. These settings are relevant if the quantity of a production order (or any other
data element) that originates from PP/DS is changed in SAP S/4HANA, for example. The
changed quantity is transferred to PP/DS, where the order is rescheduled. The new dates
are retransferred to SAP S/4HANA only if you configure this function in Transaction
/SAPAPO/CP3 (see Figure 17.6). Here, you choose elements such as in-house production,
external procurement, PIRs, and reservation data to retransfer between PP/DS and SAP
S/4HANA in a bidirectional manner.

17.2 Master Data in PP/DS
The following sections will cover the master data required to run PP/DS in SAP
S/4HANA.
17.2.1 Location
A location can represent a plant, a business partner, a shipping or receiving point, or an
MRP area. To create locations in PP/DS, you use Transaction SE38 and then execute
report /SAPAPO/CREATE_LOCATION. This report immediately creates a location, with
no integration queues, and an error message will be displayed immediately if an error
occurs (see Figure 17.7).
Figure 17.7 Creating Locations, Business Partners, Plants, and Shipping Points

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
906
You can also use ABAP program /SAPAPO/UPD_ LOC_SP_PL to update the location
data in embedded PP/DS when changes are made to the corresponding data in SAP
S/4HANA.
Note
You can access transactions pertaining to the master data, business processes, and
reporting of PP/DS via SAP Menu  Logistics  Advanced Planning.
After you transfer the plant location, a plant location of location type 1001 (production
plant) is created in embedded PP/DS. You can access the location master from Transac-
tion /SAPAPO/LOC3 or via menu path Logistics  Advanced Planning  Master Data 
Location  Location. After you enter the Location and Location Type, choose Change to
change the information and add additional PP/DS information to the location master.
17.2.2 Material Master
When you plan an advanced planning material in MRP Live, the system uses the default
heuristic and further settings defined in the Advanced Planning tab of the material
master. To plan materials with PP/DS heuristics, you define MRP type “X0” in the MRP1
tab. Whenever you want to plan a semifinished material with PP/DS heuristics, you
should also plan the parent material with PP/DS. Therefore, when designing a BOM,
you must ensure that the parent material is an advanced planning material if at least
one of its components is an advanced planning material. This design is necessary to
ensure that all the dependent requirements are relevant for PP/DS.
In SAP S/4HANA, the Advanced Planning tab has been introduced into the material
master. In this new tab, you define a material as relevant to the PP/DS planning process
by simply checking the Advanced Planning box, as shown in Figure 17.8.
Note
We’ll be using the material 2269 and plant 1710 combination throughout this chapter
while discussing PP/DS. In addition, the example here is based on discrete manufactur-
ing, but the same concepts and understanding of PP/DS apply if the manufacturing type
is either process or repetitive. Furthermore, as the focus of this chapter is on covering
the production processes in PP/DS, procurement and sales processes specific to PP/DS
are beyond the scope of this discussion.
Let’s discuss some of the important fields of PP/DS. Figure 17.8 shows the Advanced
Planning view of the material master. There are many important fields, as follows (note
that only a few of them are shown in Figure 17.8):
© 

907
17.2 Master Data in PP/DS
 PP Plng Procedure
For each planning-relevant material in PP/DS, and for any event occurring at a loca-
tion product level (that is a material), an action is triggered by PP/DS. For customer
requirements, the PP planning procedure also determines whether the desired
quantity or the confirmed quantity of a schedule line is relevant for pegging. For
example, say a planning-relevant event such as a goods movement for a product or
a change in the product master takes place. Possible actions by PP/DS can include
immediately calling up a product heuristic for the product or creating a planning file
entry. A heuristic is a planning function in PP/DS that executes planning for selected
objects, such as products, resources, operations, or line networks.
Figure 17.8 Advanced Planning Tab in Material Master
Tips and Tricks
Planning in Planning Run (planning procedure 111) should always be the standard pro-
cedure you select for planning products in PP/DS. It corresponds to the procedure used
in SAP S/4HANA. When a planning-relevant change occurs, a planning file entry is writ-
ten that is processed in the planning run with the planning of the product. Planning
procedure 111 contains heuristic SAP_PP_002 (Planning Standard Lots), and this heuris-
tic corresponds to the net requirements and lot-size calculation that you’re familiar
with from SAP S/4HANA.

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
908
 Plan Explosion
This field specifies the task list type, such as a BOM and routing, that the system uses
to create receipts in PP/DS.
 Product Heuristic
Just as there are MRP types in SAP S/4HANA that control the type of planning a
material will have, product heuristics are the planning methods for PP/DS.
 Avoid Alerts
When you check this box, the system will attempt to create the pegging relation-
ships between requirements and receipts without quantity or date alerts. The sys-
tem first links receipts and requirements that are compatible on a time and quantity
basis and then links the remaining receipts and requirements in a second step.
Warning
This method can reduce system performance.
 Alert Threshold for Earliness
The system creates a date/time alert for a fixed or dynamic pegging relationship if
the earliness exceeds the alert threshold—that is, if the availability date/time is ear-
lier than the specified time frame before the requirements date. To specify the time
frame, you use format HHHHHH:MM (hours:minutes). For example, 1000:10 means
1,000 hours and 10 minutes and 20:20 means 20 hours and 20 minutes. If no value
is specified, then the system uses the value 100000:00. Therefore, the system only
creates an alert if an availability date/time is more than 100,000 hours before the
requirements date.
 Alert Threshold for Lateness
As with the alert threshold for early receipt, the system creates a date/time alert for
a fixed or dynamic pegging relationship if the delay exceeds the alert threshold—
that is, if the availability date/time is later than the specified time frame after the
requirements date.
 Pegging Strategy
Using the pegging strategy, you can determine in which time sequence the system
should cover requirements for the product with dynamic pegging and in which time
sequence the system should use the product receipts in the pegging interval to cover
a requirement. Thus, the pegging strategy controls the following:
– Which requirement the system should cover first
– Which receipts the system should use first
In general, for dynamic pegging, the system first covers the earliest requirement,
then processes the next requirement, and so on. Because there may be several
receipts for the availability dates and times within the pegging interval, you must
© 

909
17.2 Master Data in PP/DS
specify which receipt the system should use first. You can choose from the following
options:
– Use current receipts
Here, the system uses the current receipt where possible to cover a requirement.
Starting from the requirement date, the system first searches to the beginning of
the pegging interval. If there are no receipts in this direction, the system searches
to the end of the pegging interval.
– Use the earliest receipt (FIFO)
Here, the system uses the earliest receipts in the pegging interval to cover a
requirement—that is, it uses the first receipt in the pegging interval, then the sec-
ond, and so on. With this strategy, excess receipts only become available later.
 Res. Network Name
This indicates the name of the resource network. A resource network consists of the
physical links between resources in a plant, such as processing units, reactors, and
vessels. It facilitates the flow of materials through a plant.
 Product Alerts
In this field, you specify which of the following is true:
– The system determines direct alerts for a requirement or a receipt of a product
– The product is relevant to network alerts
If a product is relevant to network alerts, then the system evaluates the direct alerts
that it determines for this product for receipts or requirements at the superordinate
levels of the pegging structure as well. You can also hide alerts for less critical com-
ponents and thus increase the clarity and transparency of network alerts and
improve performance.
If you select the Avoid Alerts option, the system won’t determine any direct alerts
for the product. Thus, the product also won’t be relevant in regard to the network.
Note
You as a planner need to specify whether and which alerts are actually displayed by the
system in the planning interface or in the alert monitor in the PP/DS alert profile to be
used for planning. We discuss the alert monitor in detail in Section 17.7.
 Lot Size Unit
This indicates the valid unit of measure (UoM). The following options are available:
– No lot size units are maintained: The base UoM maintained for the product
applies.
– You can maintain a unit of measure (an alternative or base UoM) and then con-
vert it to the base UoM. If you enter an alternative UoM, you must maintain the

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
910
base UoM on the Units of Measure tab. You must be able to convert the alterna-
tive units of measure to the base UoM for the product; otherwise, the unit won’t
be accepted. For example, say the alternative UoM is kilograms (kg) and the base
UoM is pieces (PC). In this case, 5 kg corresponds to 3 PC.
 Target Days’ Supply
The standard PP/DS SAP_PP_002 (Planning of Standard Lots) heuristic and the sup-
ply network planning (SNP) heuristic take into account the target days’ supply if one
of the following target stock level methods is selected:
– No entry: Target days’ supply from the product master
– 111: Target stock level equals maximum stock level + safety stock
– 5: Maximum from maximum stock level/target days’ supply (product master)
– 6: Total from maximum stock level/target days’ supply (product master)
– 7: No target stock level
To plan location products using target stock level methods, you use the standard
SAP_PP_002 heuristic (planning of standard lot sizes).
Heuristic-based planning uses the SNP heuristic, which plans the demand over the
entire supply chain network (via cross-location planning) and creates a medium-
term production and distribution plan. It doesn’t take into account any constraints
or costs, so the plan created won’t necessarily be feasible. In a second step, after the
heuristic run, you as a planner can then adjust the plan using capacity leveling in
interactive SNP planning to create a plan that is feasible.
 Reorder Days’ Supply
You specify the reorder days’ supply if Reorder Point Method 2 for the location prod-
uct has been defined.
 GR Processing Time
This is the time in between the delivery or the production of a product and its avail-
ability as stock. You use this time, for example, as handling time or a time for quality
checks, and you add it to the transportation duration or the production time of a
product.
 GR Handling Cap. Cons.
This is used by the heuristic and PP/DS optimizer to calculate how much handling
resource capacity is consumed by the product for a particular plan. For example, if
the handling resource can handle 1,000 liters per day and the handling capacity con-
sumption of 10 liters per piece is defined, then the maximum rate is 100 pieces per
day.
 Loc. Shelf Life
In this area, you as a planner can control whether the system should consider the
resource location-dependent shelf life of a product and, if so, the standard, mini-
mum, and maximum shelf lives a product needs to have. A location in PP/DS can be
© 

911
17.2 Master Data in PP/DS
a plant, a distribution center, a storage location, an MRP area, a customer, a transpor-
tation lane, or a business partner (supplier).
 Proposed Strategy
The strategy group maintained in the MRP 3 view of the material master is directly
linked to the PP/DS strategy, as shown in Table 17.1. If you enter other strategies or
keys into SAP S/4HANA, then the relevant field in the PP/DS product master will
remain blank or unchanged.
After you check the Advanced Planning box for an existing material, you use Transac-
tion SE38 to execute report /SAPAPO/PPDS_DELTA_ORD_TRANS for the material. This
report allows the integration of existing planning elements for this material into PP/DS
without the creation of an integration model. The only input parameters to add are the
material and the plant. You enter those parameters in the Material and Plant fields
shown in Figure 17.9.

17.2.4 Sources of Supply
Sources of supply define how the materials are procured. In the following sections, we
examine sources of supply for materials produced in house (via internal procurement)
and sources of supply for materials sourced from a supplier or another plant (via exter-
nal procurement).
Internal Procurement
For a material produced internally, the PP/DS source of supply is the production data
structure, which represents a combination of a BOM, routing, and production version.
Although creating a production data structure in PP/DS isn’t as simple as creating a
product (i.e., a material in SAP S/4HANA) and a resource (i.e., a work center in SAP
S/4HANA), there’s still no need create an integration model to do it. By using Transac-
tion SE38 and executing report PPDS_ERP_CUSLNTRTO_CIF_REPORT, you can gener-
ate the production data structure based on production versions. Figure 17.16 shows the
selection screen of report PPDS_ERP_CUSLNTRTO_CIF_REPORT 1. The results of the
report execution 2 show a successful transfer of the production data structure.

17.2.5 Stock and Batches
The inventory data in SAP S/4HANA includes stocks and the associated batches. Stock
information is transferred to embedded PP/DS along with batch information if the
material is batch-managed. Stocks are maintained at the plant level, and for planning
purposes, stock is considered at the MRP area level in SAP S/4HANA if an MRP area is
assigned to the plant. Plant-level stock will be transferred as stock to the PP/DS plant
location, and stocks in the MRP area in SAP S/4HANA are transferred to the MRP area
location in embedded PP/DS. Batches associated with stock are transferred to embed-
ded PP/DS; they are referred to as versions in the PP/DS planning tools. In embedded
PP/DS, the stock update counters are activated by default and can’t be turned off. The
stock data that’s transferred to embedded PP/DS uses relative stocks, which refers to
the delta between the current stock and the stock to be received or issued. The initial
transfer of stock can be triggered by the CIF reconciliation tool (Transaction /SAPAPO/
CCR) or the initial transfer report (Transaction /SAPAPO/PPDS_DELTA_ORD_TRANS).
Whenever a stock posting happens in SAP S/4HANA, it’s immediately transferred to
embedded PP/DS, along with its batch and storage location information.
This completes our discussion of PP/DS master data. In the following sections, we’ll dis-
cuss the important business processes of PP/DS.
