Pegging involves a link that creates a logical relationship between the receipt elements
and requirements elements of a product within a location. The system uses these peg-
ging relationships to assign requirements to receipt elements. The links always result
from availability and requirements dates within a pegging area, in which product, loca-
tion, and account assignment (MTS or MTO, etc.) correspond to each other. Pegging
involves an analysis that refers to existing orders and requirements, and it occurs inde-
pendently of requirements planning. The primary applications of pegging include
analysis of production planning, capacity planning, and optimization.
©

17.3 Business Processes
There are two types of pegging relationships: fixed and dynamic. Fixed pegging enables
a planner to fix a pegging relationship that will not be changed automatically by the
system, while in dynamic pegging, the system attempts to adjust the dates and quanti-
ties to closely meet the production and procurement plan.
For pegging relationships to be created, the product, location, account assignment
(MTS and MTO production), and planning versions must be the same.

17.4 Heuristics
A heuristic is a planning function that can be parameterized and used to execute plan-
ning or a planning step for selected objects. The planning objects of heuristics can be
products, resources, orders, and operations. The concept of heuristics is a central con-
cept in planning with PP/DS. A number of planning steps can be executed by heuristics.
Accordingly, you can easily design the planning steps in various ways—by selecting the
appropriate heuristic. In PP/DS, you as a planner simply have to select a different heu-
ristic or create a new heuristic that then becomes part of the overall planning process.
You can use the heuristics of a given group for the same task or issue; they represent
different planning and scheduling alternatives.
Two heuristic options are available:
 Product heuristic
You use a product heuristic to plan products with heuristics that are defined via the
planning procedure or product masters.
 Variable heuristic
With the variable heuristic, you as a planner can perform planning interactively with
a heuristic other than the one stored in the product master and without having to
change the entry in the product master. Furthermore, you can change the heuristic
settings before executing planning.
In any case, execution of the heuristic first treats data only in a transactional simula-
tion, and the data is written to the database only when you save the planning results.
You use the heuristic profile (configuration in PP/DS) to define which variable heuris-
tics can be selected. Three standard heuristic profiles are available:

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
928
 Profile SAP001, for the detailed planning board
 Profile SAP002, for the product view
 Profile SAPREM, for the product planning table
With interactive heuristic planning, you as a planner execute planning for a product.
You can also perform this function from the product view, the product planning board,
the detailed planning board, and so on. You can also use it when the planning situation
of a product has been changed manually and you want to determine the effects of the
change on planning right away.
Selecting the Product Heuristic option will run the heuristic assigned to the product
master; however, you can directly execute other heuristics assigned to the heuristics
profile within the product view by selecting the More  Variable Heuristic option.
To execute the variable heuristic, you go to the screen shown in Figure 17.20 and choose
More  Execute Variable Heuristic from the top menu bar. On the next screen that
appears (see Figure 17.29 1), you select the relevant heuristic, which in this example is
SAP_PP_002 Planning of Standard Lots.

cute planning in interactive (foreground) mode or in batch (background) mode.

In the production planning run, you can execute various planning steps in several con-
secutive processing phases by using different heuristics or functions. For example, you

17.5.1 Executing Planning in SAP S/4HANA
The basic rule for planning in PP/DS is that detailed production planning and schedul-
ing occurs in PP/DS, while this planning is executed in SAP S/4HANA. The planned
order or the purchase requisition is converted into a manufacturing order or purchase
order (respectively) in PP/DS to execute PP/DS planning. Unlike SAP S/4HANA planned
orders, the PP/DS planned orders already contain all the production dates. If a PP/DS
planned order is transferred to SAP S/4HANA, then the SAP S/4HANA planned order
contains only the basic dates and all dependent requirements. The production start
and end dates are transferred as basic dates.
Integration at the detailed scheduling level isn’t realized for SAP S/4HANA planned
orders. For this reason, the conversion of planned orders must occur in PP/DS. Convert-
ing a PP/DS planned order corresponds only to checking the box to set the conversion
flag (manually for one order or via mass conversion for orders and purchase requisi-
tions). If a PP/DS planned order with the conversion flag is transferred to SAP S/4HANA,
then a corresponding manufacturing order is created in SAP S/4HANA. The SAP
S/4HANA planned order assumes the production dates determined in the PP/DS
planned order, and the integration of the orders is maintained.
Status information or production confirmations that are created in SAP S/4HANA and
are relevant to planning are transferred from SAP S/4HANA to the PP/DS order. All data
relevant to planning must remain consistent. In addition, the SAP S/4HANA produc-
tion order contains a variety of functions to execute production—functions that don’t
require integration with the PP/DS order, such as the printing of order papers and con-
sumption.

The following sections will discuss the scheduling options that are available via the
resource planning table and the DS planning board, as well as the large number of
material and capacity planning options they offer.
Scheduling in Production Planning: Detailed Scheduling
Performing detailed scheduling in PP/DS helps you as a planner schedule the operation
of orders while considering constraints such as material and resource availability. The
basic functions of the scheduling tools are to schedule, reschedule, and deallocate oper-
ations. You can also use other parameters, such as scheduling direction (forward/back-
ward) and order relationships, to influence detailed scheduling behavior. You can per-
form detailed scheduling interactively from the scheduling tools or execute it in the
planning run as a step.
Scheduling via the Resource Planning Table
A resource planning table is an alternative to the DS planning board that displays
scheduling data in tabular format. You can use a resource planning table to handle
sequencing or scheduling problems with resources. The selection screen for including

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling

data in the resource planning table is similar to the selection screen for the DS planning
board. Additional tools, such as the alert monitor, plan monitor, and PP/DS optimizer,
are integrated into resource planning tables, as shown in Figure 17.36. A clipboard func-
tion is available in the resource planning table so that you as a planner can copy a list
of operations and sequence and schedule them on the resource. You can interactively
sequence operations and activities by using drag-and-drop or pick-and-drop options.
The scheduling heuristic that’s assigned to the heuristics profile used in the resource
planning table enables you to execute the scheduling heuristic. Planning multiple
resources or bucket resources isn’t supported by the resource planning table, but the
Alert Monitor and Plan Monitor functions are available in the resource planning table.

board, you can use all scheduling options, including executing various detailed sched-
uling heuristics, using the PP/DS optimizer, and manual scheduling. Detailed schedul-
ing is launched with a time profile that determines the time period during which data
is displayed (in the Display Start and End of Display fields) and the data is available for
detailed scheduling (in the Planning Start and Planning End fields).
You as a planner can manually select resources, products, and orders via Transaction

17.5.3 Production Planning: Detailed Scheduling Optimizer
The PP/DS optimizer has specific properties that distinguish it significantly from the
calculation procedure of heuristics. Solving complex scheduling problems requires
consideration of several constraints that the PP/DS optimizer can address. The effort
needed to find an exact solution to the problem increases with the number of possible
permutations. To put it more exactly, the effort needed increases by the factorial of the
orders to be planned. In this case, planning even a small, two-digit number of orders
would require hundreds of years of computer time to find an exact analytical solution.
Therefore, the PP/DS optimizer uses selective procedures that lead to realistic comput-
ing times (in minutes)—without a guarantee that the absolute optimum solution for
the problem has been found. From the set of possible solutions, it’s very likely that the
PP/DS optimizer will find some solutions, but not all of them. In comparison, a heuris-
tic leads to exactly one solution. Using the same constraints, several optimization runs
can produce different results. Based on these properties, it’s clear that the computing
time entered by the user as a termination criterion for the optimization run is
extremely important. In general, the higher the amount of computing time, the higher
the quality of the solution. However, the reverse isn’t always true: more computing
time doesn’t guarantee the improvement of a solution that has already been found.
The PP/DS optimizer isn’t called as an independent transaction; instead, it’s called
from within corresponding applications. It can be called interactively in the DS plan-
ning board or product planning table (via Transaction /SAPAPO/PPT1). The PP/DS opti-
mizer can also be run with specification of an optimization profile in the production
planning run. The PP/DS optimizer in embedded PP/DS can’t create orders, but other
functionalities are supported. The PP/DS optimizer can be executed in the background
in a production planning run (via Transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSB1) or via interactive
scheduling tools such as the DS planning board (refer to Figure 17.37).
To use the PP/DS optimizer, you choose Optimize on the screen shown in Figure 17.37.
In the ensuing popup, you enter the start and end dates of the optimization horizon
and then choose Optimize again to open the PP/DS Optimizer screen shown in Figure
17.40.
Before we discuss Figure 17.40 in detail, you should choose the Optimization profile
icon (the wrench) located in the top left-hand corner of the screen to bring up the Set-
tings box shown in Figure 17.41. Here, you can maintain different information—such as
the total setup costs, maximum delay costs, and total mode costs—in relative terms.
You can also make various optimization settings in different tabs.

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling

Figure 17.40 PP/DS Optimization Log

by customizing the DS planning board or the product planning table, or by using Trans-
action /SAPAPO/CDPSC5. When you start the PP/DS optimizer, you can adjust or
change some of these basic settings based on the current planning situation (see Figure
17.41).
Figure 17.41 PP/DS Optimization Profile
© 

941
17.5 Production Planning Run
The PP/DS optimizer works based on costs, and therefore, the objective of optimization
is to minimize overall production costs. You can achieve this cost reduction with an
objective function made up of individually weighted key figures. Use the following
weighting factors (Wi, where i = 1…6) to influence the orientation of optimization:
Production costs = (W1 × Makespan) + (W2 × Total of the setup times) + (W3 × Total of
the setup costs) + (W4 × Maximum delay costs) + (W5 × Total of the delay costs) +
(W6 × Total of the mode costs)
Now, let’s discuss the fields shown in Figure 17.41:
 Makespan
Minimizing the makespan, or the overall lead time, aims at a maximum load on the
optimized bottleneck resources in the short term. The system attempts to minimize
the overall time needed to process orders and operations with consideration of the
existing constraints.
 Total Setup Times
Minimizing the total of the setup times is possible if the time needed to set up from
one setup status to the next is dependent on exactly that sequence (setup status-
dependent setup time with dynamic setup). In a paint shop, for example, the time
required to set up the use of a light color after using a dark one is likely longer than
the time to set up the opposite. In such cases and similar cases, an optimization
objective can be to create an optimal sequence from the standpoint of the total of
the required setup times. The result is that the system summarizes the operations of
similar setup statuses in blocks. Long setup times would then appear only when
changing between two different setup statuses.
 Total Setup Costs
Minimizing the total of the setup costs is necessary only when the system needs to
have the costs and setup times described independently of each other. This can be
the case when identical setup times differ for various resources in terms of cost-
related evaluation and must therefore be decoupled from the setup time. Otherwise,
the setup time criterion is sufficient.
 Maximum Delay Costs
The PP/DS optimizer can penalize delays that occur in terms of the defined require-
ments dates/times that lie within the optimization horizon with delay costs. Two
approaches are available:
– You can permit short delays in similar forms, which will help you avoid individ-
ual, significant delays, resulting in a minimization of the maximum delay costs.
– You can formulate the total of the delays as a target value and calculate a mini-
mum value across the overall delay for all orders. This approach doesn’t exclude
individual, obvious delays. In general, you can define delay costs as dependent on
the order priority; you would use higher costs for sales orders with a higher prior-
ity. This approach should help to avoid delays for high-priority orders.

17 Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling
942
 Total Delay Costs
This is the cost of delayed production, and it enables planners to decide which mate-
rials need to be produced first so as to minimize the associated costs.
 Total Mode Costs
Mode costs penalize the frequent (via fixed costs) and long-lasting (as defined with
variable costs) use of alternative work centers. The alternative work centers must
already be available in the system in master data and contained in the propagation
range. You can link them to optimization via alternative modes.
 Trade-offs
It should be obvious that optimization usually works within somewhat contradic-
tory constraints and different interests, such as sales interests and production inter-
ests. This is referred to as a trade-off.
It’s also clear that the influencing factors have somewhat different natures (costs and
times) and therefore possess different physical units. To enable a comparison of the
individual totals within the formula for calculating the production costs, costs are con-
verted into times. The following rule applies here: a standardized cost of 30 corre-
sponds to 30 seconds.
Referring to Figure 17.40 again, note the information about the quality of the solution
that has been found both during and after successful optimization. The three bars
shown here graphically display information on the status of the optimization mes-
sages, the values of the related optimization parameters (including deallocation costs),
and the solution process over time. From this evaluation view, you can use a button to
view the parameters of the optimization and to change the parameters set by the opti-
mization profile to some extent, as well as to start optimization (or restart it) interac-
tively.
To start the PP/DS optimizer, you choose the Start Optimization Run icon (the clock or
the execute icon) in the top left-hand corner of Figure 17.40. Then, the system will start
generating messages as shown on the right-hand side of the screen. Notice that the
optimization run shows that quite a few orders can only be scheduled with a delay,
while the two bottom-half sections show the numerical and weighted values of the
optimized solution. Next, choose the Sol. tab located in the top-left-hand corner of
Figure 17.40 to open the results screen shown in Figure 17.42.
Figure 17.42 shows the graphical representation of the results of the PP/DS optimiza-
tion run and the associated Total Delay Costs (the tallest bar) due to a delayed start of
the order. You can carry out more optimization runs if needed. Alternatively, you can
make changes to the settings shown in Figure 17.41 and then rerun the PP/DS optimizer.

The next section discusses the alert monitor in PP/DS.
17.7 Alert Monitor
The alert monitor in PP/DS is a tool that a planner can use during planning to manage
and evaluate exception messages centrally. Messages about problems are transferred
automaticallly from the application to the alert monitor, and each exception message
relates to a specific problem situation. The primary task of the alert monitor is to list
exception messages in a structured manner, and the messages correspond to the
defined selection criteria. The alert monitor provides information on situations that
require adjustments in planning. Note the distinction between the reason for the
exception and its properties. Using exception messages, the alert monitor can provide
general information on the problem situation itself but not necessarily on why the
exception occurred.
Let’s first discuss how to set up the alert monitor before showing how it works in plan-
ning and scheduling.
To create a new alert monitor profile, you use Transaction /SAPAPO/AMON1 and
choose Alert Profile on the initial screen. Figure 17.51 shows alert profile ZPPDSALTMON,
in which checking the relevant boxes in the Selection column will trigger relevant
alerts. You can also define ranges or specific values so that the system brings up infor-
mation, warnings, or error alerts upon meeting the specified values. In this example,
the Product Too Early (Fixed Pegging) alert type shows alert thresholds for information,
warnings, and error alerts.
Note
After you create an alert profile, you assign it to an overall profile so that the system
can display alerts within an application (refer to Figure 17.27 to see how defined alert
profile ZPPDSALTMON in Figure 17.51 is assigned to User Settings).
Next, access the screen shown in Figure 17.52 by opening Transaction /SAPAPO/RRP3
(Product View) and choosing the Alert Monitor icon. Note the large number of error,
warning, and information alerts in the top half of the screen. When you check any given
box in the Selection column, details of each alert will be displayed in the lower half of the
screen. For this example, resource WASSEMBLY_1710_001 has several error alerts.

17.8 Plan Monitor
17.8 Plan Monitor
The plan monitor evaluates the key figures for a plan in relation to specific objects, ver-
sions, and periods, and it then assigns scores to the results. A planner can define their
own calculation rules according to which the scores are to be calculated, or they can
simply use standard aggregations (e.g., sum, mean value).
The plan monitor can be used to do the following:
 Determine the quality of the current planning situation.
 Compare various planning and simulation versions with each other.
 Compare planning over different periods.
The plan monitor considers the key figures for which a planner has created key figure
variants in the key figure schema. These key figures can be displayed in a tabular or
graphical version or both.
For example, say a production planner plans over a three- to four-week horizon but is
faced with new expectations every day that impact many areas of the plan over the
upcoming weeks. Here, the plan monitor can help.
Key figures in the following areas of the plan monitor are available:
 Key figures for order quantities
 Key figures for order times
 Key figures for pegging relationships
 Key figures for stocks
 Key figures for resources
Let’s see how you can put the plan monitor to use in a step-by-step way:
1. On the initial screen of Transaction /SAPAPO/PMON, choose Definition: Key Figures
Schema to open the screen shown in Figure 17.53.
2. In the General tab, maintain initial details such as Key figure schema—which, in this
example, is, “PPDSS4HANA”—and then add a short description.
3. In the Display area, choose the relevant display options, such as to display only the
grid or the graphic or to display both. Check the relevant box to choose whether indi-
vidual values in the graphic can be displayed.
4. Choose the Key Figures tab.
5. On the screen shown in Figure 17.54, select Key Figure Cat. (category) from the drop-
down list (in this example, 9033 Orders: Times) and then choose the relevant key fig-
ure (Open production time). The lower left-hand corner of the screen offers the avail-
able calculations, such as using minimum, maximum, or average values of key fig-
ures.

17.9 Summary
17.9 Summary
In this chapter, we covered important configurations, master data, business processes,
and reporting of PP/DS in SAP S/4HANA. The PP/DS optimizer attempts to maintain a
balance between costs and benefits for producing or not producing products and to
determine where trade-offs are necessary. The user-defined alerts in the alert monitor
and monitoring key figures in the plan monitor keep various checks and balances in
place for smoother production and procurement planning. SAP Fiori apps for PP/DS
not only report via dashboards but also enable intuitive planning.
The next chapter discusses SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering
and operations.

© 

957
Chapter 18
Production Engineering and
Operations
SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and
operations is one of the latest additions to the manufacturing space
in SAP S/4HANA as an embedded solution. It caters to large, complex,
turnkey projects, such as those in the aerospace, aircraft, shipbuilding,
defense, and space industries, and it’s an extension of discrete manufac-
turing production types.
SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for production engineering and operations is gaining
significant traction in complex, low-volume, and extended manufacturing processes,
and it’s an extension of discrete manufacturing processes. Due to the extensive design
and development stages involved in a complex, large-scale, turnkey manufacturing
process, production engineering and operations attempts to bring a greater balance
between design changes (engineering bills of materials [EBOMs]) and their impact on
operations/manufacturing (manufacturing bills of materials [MBOMs]).
Here are some of the traits to consider when deciding whether to implement produc-
tion engineering and operations:
 Highly engineered products
 Engineer-to-order (ETO)
 Make-to-order (MTO and configure-to-order
 Complex assembly production
 Low volume of orders
 High numbers of engineering change requests
 Labor-intensive processes
 Long cycle times (days, weeks, and months)
 High numbers of components and operations
Materials with some or all of the above traits are prime candidates to be considered for
production via production engineering and operations.
Figure 18.1 shows the key building blocks of production engineering and operations,
and Figure 18.2 focuses on the key business processes of production engineering and
operations that we’ll discuss in this chapter.

18.1 Key Terminology
Then, we discuss the system configuration, roles, and authorizations required for using
production engineering and operations. Then, we explain the core business processes of
production engineering and the master data setup requirements and version manage-
ment that support the production engineering and operations processes. We provide
comparisons to demonstrate key differences between the master data setup required for
production engineering and operations and the master data setup required for standard
discrete manufacturing. We then use an example to walk you through the process of
transitioning from engineering to manufacturing execution operations and using tools
to hand over processes from product lifecycle management systems to SAP S/4HANA
systems. Then, we discuss the business processes of production operations in a step-by-
step manner. Furthermore, we briefly touch upon integration of production engineering
and operations with other SAP S/4HANA areas, such as materials management, quality
management, the SAP Document Management service, and SAP Human Capital Manage-
ment for SAP S/4HANA. Lastly, as production engineering and operations processes
entail significant engineering changes to the products being manufactured, we also dis-
cuss change management and impact analysis.
Note
Because production engineering and operations processes significantly resemble dis-
crete manufacturing processes, we recommend that you read Chapter 3 and Chapter 6
before reading this chapter. We’ll only be discussing the processes briefly here so as to
stay focused on production engineering and operations.
18.1 Key Terminology
In Figure 18.2 you’ll see some of the following key terminologies used in production
engineering and operations:
 Production engineering
This bridges the gap between product engineering and production operations by
converting the product design into production process design, which is the basis for
production order management and shop floor execution.
 Manufacturing change management
This orchestrates the end-to-end production engineering process, as follows:
– The Monitor Engineering Snapshots app informs you of new sets of EBOM ver-
sions and triggers the production engineering process.
– The Manufacturing Change Record orchestrates the whole production engineer-
ing process and keeps track of all changes made to manufacturing objects when
you’re incorporating a new EBOM version.

18 Production Engineering and Operations
960
– The Change Impact Analysis app identifies all objects that are impacted by a prod-
uct engineering change.
– BOM and routing version management control the lifecycle and usage of differ-
ent revision levels.
 SAP 3D Visual Enterprise Manufacturing Planner
You use this to accomplish the handover from the EBOM to the MBOM by convert-
ing the design-driven EBOM structure into a manufacturing, built-sequence-driven
MBOM structure.
 Production process planning
This is used to create shop floor routings that include all information needed for
production planning, production costing, and shop floor execution, as follows:
– Operations are used for planning and costing purposes, as in classic SFC and dis-
crete manufacturing.
– Operation activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail
how an operation has to be performed: with work instructions, data collection
required, qualifications needed, production resources/tools (PRT), and buyoff
procedures.
Production process planning includes a set of “supporting” features:
– The BOMs and routings used for planning (MRP and PP/DS) can be distinguished
from those used for shop floor execution. The usage of BOMs and routings for dif-
ferent purposes is defined in the Manage Production Version app.
– Using templates and favorites significantly increases user efficiency when main-
taining routings.
– In the Manage Standard Texts app, a manufacturing engineer can maintain a
library of reusable text elements for efficient work instruction authoring.
– Buyoff templates can be defined and used during routing maintenance as an
approval process.
– Qualification requirements can be predefined for work centers and materials and
are inherited by related operations and activities.
The next section describes the basic configuration and setup activities you need to per-
form so you can use production engineering and operations.
18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and
Operations Setup
In the following sections, we’ll discuss the configuration settings and the basic produc-
tion engineering and operations setup that you need to put in place in the SAP
S/4HANA system.

18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup
18.2.4 Creating Business Partners and Manufacturing Users
In production engineering and operations, all SAP users need to be manufacturing
users too, regardless of their roles.
In SAP S/4HANA, a business user is defined as a natural person who is represented by a
business partner and is linked to a user in the SAP S/4HANA system. Business users
interact with the SAP S/4HANA system in the context of a business process—for exam-
ple, in the role of a production engineer, production supervisor, or production opera-
tor. For more information, see SAP Note 2570961.
You create a business partner by using the Maintain Employees app, and while doing
so, you assign the user ID of the user from the SAP S/4HANA system. Figure 18.5 shows
that the User is assigned Business Partner 9980000210, which you can confirm by
using Transaction SU3. We’ll use this business partner (9980000210) in examples
throughout this chapter. Because production engineering and operations entails work-
ing with various teams and business functions, you should create a few additional busi-
ness partners and assign their SAP user IDs accordingly.
Figure 18.5 SAP User ID Mapping with Business Partner IDs
To create manufacturing users, you create business partners as just discussed and then
use them as manufacturing users. Figure 18.6 1 shows that a manufacturing user is cre-
ated using the Manage User Settings app, in which not only previously created busi-
ness partner 9980000210 is assigned but so are Work Center (PEOWC) and Plant (1710).
You also use the Manage Teams and Responsibilities app to create teams that will be
managing the production engineering and operations business processes and to assign
team members accordingly 2. It’s also important for you to create appropriate teams
and responsibilities for several functions in production engineering and operations.
This is required for the following functions:
 When adding a buyoff to operation activities in the routing, it’s best practice to spec-
ify the appropriate team and provide information about its functions.
 When creating buyoff cycle templates, it’s required to specify the appropriate teams
and provide information about their functions.

18.2.10 Planning Scenarios
The next configuration step lets you activate or deactivate the planning scenarios for
creating a planning scope alternative. A planning scenario defines the parameters and
the associated scope based on which the system undertakes planning in production
engineering and operations. Here are some of the important planning scenarios that
you can create, activate or deactivate:
 Document-based nonconfigurable
This scenario allows a planner to create a planning scope alternative in which the
EBOM is derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a noncon-
figurable structure. We’ll use this planning scenario as an example to demonstrate
the end-to-end business process in this chapter.
 Document-based purchased part
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative for a purchased part.
The planning scope alternative of a purchased part is not available for planning.
 Product structure manager-based configurable
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is
derived from the product structure manager and the target is a configurable struc-
ture.
© 

969
18.2 Configuration Settings and Production Engineering and Operations Setup
 Document to nonconfiguration with network
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is
derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a nonconfigurable
structure. It also allows loading a project system network and assigning components
to the network activity. The reference points are copied from network activity to the
assigned MBOM components.
 Document to nonconfiguration group BOM
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is
derived from the document linked to an RH file and the target is a nonconfigurable
structure. This scenario is plant independent. The target structure is a nonconfigu-
rable group BOM.
 Product structure manager to configurable group BOM
This scenario allows creation of a planning scope alternative in which the EBOM is
derived from the product structure manager and the target is a configurable struc-
ture. This scenario is plant independent. The target structure is a configurable group
BOM.
To make changes to the relevant planning scenario, you follow the menu path SAP IMG 
Production  Visual Manufacturing Planner  Configure Planning Scenario.
On the screen shown in Figure 18.13, you assign the previously configured planning
BOM usage V to planning scenario 01 because this is the example we’ll be focusing on.
For any other planning scenario, you maintain the relevant BOM usage accordingly.
Figure 18.13 Planning Scenarios

neering.

18.3.3 Managing Shop Floor Routing
To create a version-controlled routing in production engineering and operations, you
use the Shop Floor Routing app or use Transaction MSFR4; the standard Transaction
CR01 for creating a routing will not offer the options required for a shop floor routing.
With a shop floor routing, a planner can create and manage version-controlled rout-
ings that are not dependent on time. A routing version determines the sequence of
individual operations that are necessary to produce a product. It also contains informa-
tion on where the work is performed and the work center’s resources that will be con-
sumed with all the necessary technical specifications, such as the standard times,
capacity requirements, and work instructions. The operation activities can be used to
describe the production process in more detail. Once released, a production version for
the routing version and BOM version can be used in production execution. Note that a
released routing version can no longer be changed.

 The target panel contains the MBOM structure and corresponding visual (the multi-

ufacturing Planner, the production engineer can decide to replace an EBOM material
with a manufacturing substitute. If required, the quantity can also be adjusted (e.g.,

ing over future EBOM changes and during EBOM/MBOM reconciliation.

The next section is on production operations.
18.5 Production Operations
Production operations are about initiating, preparing, executing, and documenting the
actual end-to-end production process. A shop floor production order is an extended

18 Production Engineering and Operations
990
version of a PP production order in discrete manufacturing. We’ll cover many additional
details of shop floor production orders in production engineering and operations here.
Figure 18.43 shows the end-to-end process flow that we’ll discuss in these sections,
along with the following additional details:
 Operations are used for planning and costing, as in classic SFC.
 Activities are used for shop floor execution, and they describe in detail how an oper-
ation has to be performed—that is, work instructions, data collection required, qual-
ifications needed, PRTs, buyoff procedures, and so on.
 Production monitoring provides a comprehensive overview of the expected and
actual status and progress of production orders and operations by automatically
detecting exceptions, providing decision support, and allowing for immediate
actions.
 Engineering change incorporates and updates production orders with the latest
engineering changes.
 Complex assembly execution is primarily designed for complex, low-volume pro-
duction processes with high change frequency and a focus on manual work—which
is typical for complex assembly and engineer-to-order businesses.
 Work assignment and work queues orchestrate the actual execution of production
order operations and activities. Operators can access their tasks in a work center’s
work queues, or supervisors can explicitly distribute work to their team members by
moving tasks to personal work queues.
 The Perform Work app supports production operators in executing their tasks by
guiding them through the production process with the help of interactive work
instructions, 3D visual support, data collection with real-time valuations, and so on.

of how a serialized part has been produced and how an order has been executed.

18.5.2 Assign Work App
The Assign Work app lists all operations to be performed at a given work center. It sup-
ports the operator in picking the next operation to be worked on based on order and
operation status, priority, scheduled start/end, start feasibility, and issues such as
holds, missing components, and delays (see Figure 18.45). Production supervisors can
see all their operators with their currently assigned workloads and labor statuses.
Supervisors can assign additional operation activities to operators while considering
their qualifications and work center assignments. Supervisors can also remove opera-
tion activities from the operator’s worklist and maintain target times for each opera-
tion activity assigned to an operator.
Some additional available functions are as follows:
 Select all unassigned operation activities or check existing assignments.
 Assign one or more production operators to individual operation activities to dis-
tribute the workload.
 Filter operators’ names by matching work centers and qualifications during an
assignment.
 View a number of current operators’ assignments.
 Add and edit the target duration for an operation activity during an assignment.
 Unassign operators from operation activities.
 Assign (and unassign) not only operators but also teams to specific operations.

at the operation activity level. Besides displaying the key data that’s needed, the app

18.5.5 Change Impact Analysis App
Before we delve into discussing the Change Impact Analysis app, let’s briefly discuss
another SAP Fiori app: Manage Change Records. You can create a change record at any
stage of the production engineering and operations business process by using the Man-
age Change Record app. However, we highly recommend that you to create a change
record as early as possible so that all objects affected by the changes are part of the
change record. The primary purpose of a change record is to contain references to all the
objects that are relevant to the change cycle. The following objects are supported:
 Master data: EBOM, MBOM, shop floor routing, and planning scope
 Transactional data: production order and purchase order
Change items can be configured depending on the change record type. You indicate the
context of the object in the change record by using the change relevance setting—for
example, Create, Change, New Version, Reference, and so on. The status network of the
change record is also configurable.
A manufacturing engineer can use the change record as a single point of entry to access
all the relevant objects for the change cycle. The change record enables an engineer to
navigate to the corresponding apps to view or manipulate the objects.
You access the screen shown in Figure 18.56 by opening the Manage Engineering
Records app or the Manage Change Records app, followed by selecting change record
200000020. This is the same change record that you created while creating the shop
floor routing. More objects, such as planning scopes, manufacturing material BOMs,
and even production order 80000043 were added to this change record later on to ana-
lyze the impact of changes made to any or all of these objects. Then, choose Analyze
Impact on the screen shown in Figure 18.56.
The change impact analysis can be triggered from different master data objects: EBOMs,
MBOMs, and shop floor routings. It determines the net change of the trigger objects
and identifies all manufacturing objects that are impacted by those changes: MBOMs,

shop floor routings, production orders, project networks, and directly related purchase
orders (direct procurement, purchase-to-order, and purchase-to-project). The change
impact analysis also determines which parts (BOM items, operations, or operation
activities) of an object are impacted by the change (see Figure 18.57).

Chapter 19
Production Planning Reporting
Timely, accurate, and comprehensive information helps a company
make better business decisions. A planner can immediately start to use a
large number of information systems and standard reports available in
SAP S/4HANA for production planning, as well as for logistics.
This chapter provides detailed guidance on how to leverage a large number of standard
reports that are available in all the logistics areas of SAP S/4HANA. Once you’re familiar
with the features and functions for reporting, including tools and icons, you’ll find it
easier to navigate to unexplored areas of reporting.
19.1 The Basics of Reporting
To fully take advantage of SAP S/4HANA, it’s essential to become familiar with the
icons and other features frequently available for use in reports. Because these icons
and functions are the same in all the information systems and analyses, a user can nav-
igate faster, get to desired information more quickly, and even find tips and tricks to
manage the display and availability of data as needed.
Note
It’s important to note that you may find similar icons in master data and transactions
such as the REM planning table (in Transaction MF50) or the stock/requirements list (in
Transaction MD04), but these icons may have different meanings and functions. For
example, in a standard business process, clicking the Details icon brings up the Item
details screen for the object. In the reporting function, however, clicking this icon
brings up a comprehensive list titled All Key Figures.
Information systems present a large amount of data and information in logically struc-
tured lists and sections. We provide you with the following menu paths and transac-
tion codes for both discrete manufacturing and process manufacturing to make the
information systems easier to use:
 Discrete manufacturing
Choose Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Information System  Order Infor-
mation System or use Transaction COOIS.

19 Production Planning Reporting
1012
 Process manufacturing
Choose Logistics  Production – Process  Process Order  Reporting  Order Informa-
tion System or use Transaction COOISPI.
19.2 Report Parameters
When we talk about the order information system, it can be either the production order
information system or the process order information system. The former is specific to
discrete manufacturing reporting, while the latter is for process manufacturing report-
ing.
An information system consists of the most up-to-date information about a specific
object, and it’s based on the transactions that the user has performed as part of the
business process. For example, when a planner executes the operations list in a produc-
tion order information system, the system displays the current status of various oper-
ations, including those that are completely or partially confirmed. If the same report is
run or executed again after six hours—for example, when the user has recorded more
confirmations for production orders—at that time, the system shows up-to-date (and
different) information. Another example is that when the user first uses the sales infor-
mation system, it shows the creation of 10 sales order for a specific material. After six
hours, when the user again runs the report (in the sales information system), the num-
ber of sales orders has increased to 18. This information helps to reflect the accurate
and up-to-date status of various activities and business processes.
In the order information system, we’ve divided the large number of available options
for parameters selection into several figures in the following sections for ease of under-
standing and comprehension.

ters. If you select Components from the dropdown list in Figure 19.2, then you will need
to give selection parameters on the screen shown in Figure 19.3 (in the next section)
under Selection at Component Level. If you select Operations/Phases from the drop-
down list, then you can provide greater details in the Select. at Operation Level area on
the screen shown in Figure 19.3.
This all-in-one report caters to the reporting requirements of all major business pro-
cesses of production. Notice that a user can also provide the sales order number with a
sales order item number, if a company has MTO process order (or production order)

19.2.6 Maintain Variant
Maintaining a variant in SAP S/4HANA is similar to saving a layout that is then avail-
able for repeated use when needed. A user can also set a variant as a default by manag-
ing the layout options. For example, say that when using the order information sys-
tem, a user always uses plant 1710, order type PI01, and the date range of 30 days from
today. Instead of entering all of those parameters every time to run the order informa-
tion system, a user can simply save these details as a variant and subsequently select it.
There are two areas in which you can maintain variants. The first one is on the Initial
Parameters Selection screen of any report, information system, or analysis that you
use when defining the selection parameters. You can simply enter the information
once in the parameter selection screen and then save the variant by choosing Goto 
Variants  Save as Variant. On the Variant Attributes screen that appears, you can give
the variant a name and a short description and then choose whether to make each field
required, hidden, or protected from data entry. Then, you can save the variant, and
next time, you can simply choose Goto  Variants  Get to get the variant. You can also
delete incorrect or unwanted variants.
You can use the second option for saving a variant when an analysis report is already
executed in the order information system. The system displays all list fields, and a user
has made the required changes—for example, setting any filters, sorting any columns
in ascending or descending order, or performing aggregation. In this case, you choose
Select Layout and select the Save Layout option from the dropdown. In the ensuing
popup, you enter the name of the layout and a short description. Here again, a user can
change or delete the saved layouts, if needed.

When executing a report or conducting a standard analysis, a user can also download

19.2.10 Copy Selective Data to Microsoft Excel
A user can copy selected data if they don’t want to download all the data from SAP
S/4HANA into Excel or a different format. To do this, they press (Ctrl)+(Y) to select the
fields or content within the report. This enables block selection, in which they select the
block of fields to copy. Then, they press (Ctrl)+(C) to copy the data. In Excel or another
program (Notepad or Microsoft Word), they press (Ctrl)+(P) to paste the copied data.
19.2.11 Print
Users have options to create a printout of the report. They access the print option by

19.3 Order Information System Reports
The following sections cover some of the actual order reports, such as automatic goods
movement, capacities, document links, and execution steps.
19.3.1 Automatic Goods Movement
In automatic goods movement, the system performs one or both of the following func-
tions:
 If the Backflush functionality is activated, the component’s consumption or GI is auto-
matically recorded at the time of confirmation against the process order. This is
denoted by movement type 261.
 If the Auto-GR functionality is activated, the system automatically performs the GR
for the process order of the confirmed production quantity when the user performs
the confirmation for the process order. This is denoted by movement type 101.
Next, from the List dropdown option shown earlier in Figure 19.1 (we’ll be referring to
this List dropdown in each of the report options discussed next), you should select
Automatic Goods Movements. Figure 19.12 shows the Movmt type (movement type)
column for backflush and auto-GR.
Figure 19.12 Automatic Goods Movements in Order Information System

19 Production Planning Reporting
1024
19.3.2 Capacities
Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Capacities. Based on the information
from the routing of the material, the system automatically calculates the required
capacities when the user creates a production order. When the user confirms an opera-
tion of the production order, the system correspondingly reduces the capacity require-
ments of the work center.
This will display the work centers, operation quantities, and remaining processing time
(in hours) for each of the production orders.
19.3.3 Production Resources/Tools
Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Production Resource/Tool (PRT).
Although it’s not directly involved in the production process, PRT plays a pivotal role in
the manufacturing process.
The available PRT categories are material (M), equipment (E), document (D), and miscel-
laneous (O). The details in Usage Value denote the duration for which a PRT is needed
during the production process for a production order.
19.3.4 Items
Next, from the List dropdown menu, you select Items. This will display the items of the
production order quantities, the actual recorded scrap, the GR, and the variance, if any.
This screen also contains the overdelivery or underdelivery for the material, plus col-
umns containing fields such as Quantity, Scrap, and ExpVarRcpt (expected variance in
receipt).
19.3.5 Document Links
Next, from the List dropdown menu, select Document Links. These documents were
created in the SAP Document Management service and then attached to an object, such
as the material master or BOM. When a production order for a material containing an
SAP Document Management service document is created or when a BOM containing
such a document is used in the production order, the system automatically creates the
document links. An SAP Document Management service document consists of a docu-
ment number, document type, document part, and document version.
19.3.6 Execution Steps
In the production order information system, a user will see the same information in
the Control Recipe list as is shown in the process order information system. Execution
steps (XSteps) or control recipes are used in process manufacturing, depending on the

19.4 Missing Parts Information System
Having access to a standard SAP S/4HANA report that contains information about
missing parts of components in the production or process order is highly valuable to a
company’s production planner. Production or process orders with missing compo-
nents are shown with the system status MSPT. When a user executes the report, the sys-
tem not only shows the requirements quantity but also the committed or available
quantity, as well as the shortage quantity.
The missing parts information system can be accessed for all of the different produc-
tion types—that is, discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and REM. The
menu path/transaction for each is listed here:
 Discrete manufacturing
Choose Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Missing Parts Info System or use
Transaction CO24.
 Process manufacturing
Choose Logistics  Production—Process  Process Order  Reporting  Missing Parts
Info System or use Transaction CO24.

 Repetitive manufacturing

Chapter 20
Standard Planning Analysis
Standard analyses in SAP S/4HANA provide great insights into many
important logistics and supply chain management key performance
indicators, which can help companies make informed decisions. Stan-
dard analyses aren’t just available in production planning but also in all
logistics and supply chain management areas of SAP S/4HANA.
Standard analyses enable users to draw information from the available standard info
structures in SAP S/4HANA. The difference between shop floor information system
(reports) and standard analyses is that in information systems, the information is
dynamic and changes when the user performs the relevant business functions. In stan-
dard analyses, the system instead draws from historical information to enable the user
to make better business decisions. In other words, the data and information from the
information systems flow into the analyses.
This chapter will cover some of the standard analyses for discrete manufacturing. We
also review a few features and functionalities that are available only in analyses and not
in information systems, such as time series analysis and ABC analysis. The concepts
underlying standard analyses for discrete manufacturing that we discuss in this chap-
ter will enable users to explore standard analysis of process manufacturing and REM,
for which we’ve provided the necessary menu paths as well as the associated transac-
tion codes.
Note
Refer to Table 20.1 through Table 20.3 for the transaction codes of the various available
standard analyses in discrete manufacturing, process manufacturing, and REM, respec-
tively. We’ll also provide the relevant SAP S/4HANA menu paths you can use to access
the standard analyses of each production type.
20.1 Discrete Manufacturing/Production Order
To access the standard analyses of discrete manufacturing, you follow menu path
Logistics  Production  Shop Floor Control  Information System  Shop Floor Informa-
tion System  Standard Analyses. Table 20.1 lists the transaction codes of various stan-

Figure 20.5 shows the standard analysis production orders (Transaction MCP3). Here,

Figure 20.5 Production Order Analysis
The options in ABC analysis are as follows:
 Total GR Quantity (%)
The GR quantity is divided by the defined percentage.
 Number Material (%)
The number of materials is divided by the defined percentage.
 GR Quantity (Absolute)
The actual GR quantity is divided into A, B, and C segments.

20 Standard Planning Analysis
1032
 Number Material
The number of materials is divided into A, B, and C segments.
ABC analysis enables a user to select the right strategy and then divide the key figures
into the top three groupings. Furthermore, there’s flexibility to define what percentages
of a key figure should be listed in the A, B, and C segments.
You can overwrite the system-suggested segment sizes for A, B, and C. For this example,
enter the A Segment as “70,” the B Segment as “20,” and the C Segment as “10.” These
three entries cause the system to divide and display the Total GR Quantity (%) in such a
way that the top 70% will be categorized in segment A, 20% will be categorized in seg-
ment B, and the bottom 10% will be categorized in segment C, in a sequential order.
Next, click Calculate to open SAP Business Graphics as a graphical representation of the
ABC analysis. Then, click Graphics in the menu bar to make the screen shown in Figure
20.6 appear.

Chapter 21
SAP Fiori Apps for Production Planning
Timely, accurate, and comprehensive information helps companies
make better and faster business decisions. A large number of SAP Fiori
apps are available not just for production planning but also for logistics
and supply chain management, and they can be used immediately.
SAP Fiori is the next-generation web-based user experience for working with the SAP
S/4HANA system, and each app is accessed via a tile in the SAP Fiori launchpad. A large
number of SAP Fiori apps are available in SAP S/4HANA, and the SAP Fiori apps refer-
ence library is constantly expanding. Apps for master data, transactional data, report-
ing, and analytics in all SAP areas are available in the SAP Fiori apps reference library.
We’ll cover some of them in this chapter, after taking a quick look at the SAP Fiori
launchpad and the library for all SAP Fiori apps.
21.1 SAP Fiori Launchpad and SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library
Figure 21.1 shows the initial screen of the SAP Fiori launchpad, wherein individual apps
are represented as tiles. The apps can be grouped together based on different roles,
such as materials planner, production planner, and inventory controller. They can also
be grouped together based on functional areas such as production planning (PP), mate-
rials management (MM), and quality management (QM), as shown in the different tabs
in Figure 21.1. Due to their highly intuitive nature, the apps make it easy to quickly get
up to speed. Furthermore, most master data and transactional data apps and even sev-
eral reporting apps in SAP Fiori still give the look and feel of the SAP GUI by showing the
same menu options, menu bars, buttons, and icons. The top-right-hand corner of the
SAP Fiori launchpad provides an option to search for an app by its name or by transac-
tion code, such as COR2 (change process order).
The library of SAP Fiori apps is constantly expanding at a rapid pace for both SAP
S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud. To search for and explore the latest or most rele-
vant SAP Fiori apps, go to http://s-prs.co/v216700 and click the Home icon. On the initial
screen of the SAP Fiori apps reference library, you can search for a relevant app on the
left-hand side of the screen and view app details on the right-hand side. To make rele-
vant apps available for use, reach out to your SAP Fiori consultant with an app’s details
and the specific role or group to which it needs to be assigned.

21 SAP Fiori Apps for Production Planning

Figure 21.1 SAP Fiori Launchpad Homepage
21.2 SAP Fiori Applications
In the following sections, we’ll cover some of the SAP Fiori apps of PP, such as Monitor
Material Coverage, Monitor External and Internal Requirements, and Manage Work
Center Capacity.
21.2.1 Monitor Material Coverage App
This app enables a production planner to monitor all the materials in a selected area of
responsibility. This includes MTS and collective requirements materials, MTO and ETO
materials, and materials for direct production and direct procurement. The app also
provides a list of all materials with a net requirement segment or individual segments.
These materials might have coverage issues according to a specified shortage defini-
tion, and the shortage definition a planner chooses contains a set of rules that the sys-
tem uses for calculating the material shortages. These rules define the scope of the
shortage calculation—that is, the supply and demand elements to be considered and
the conditions that have to be met for a shortage to be relevant.
Figure 21.2 shows the Monitor Material Coverage app. In it the green bars represent
materials for which there is a comfortable stock availability situation. The red bars rep-
resent a situation that the inventory or materials planner needs to pay greater atten-
tion to in order to avoid stock-outs or unavailability of required material on the desired
date. The displayed columns show the shortage quantity and when the first shortage is
likely to happen, and more columns can be displayed too. Multiple dropdowns on the
left-hand side of the screen provide options for the materials or production planner to
filter materials based on different criteria. After applying a new filter, the planner can
© 

1041
21.2 SAP Fiori Applications
click Go and then use the default Time till Shortage, Material, and Individual Segment
filters to narrow down the scope of the list.

screen shown in Figure 21.2—for example, 2269 —and the screen shown in Figure 21.3

sales orders and stock transport orders in a selected area of responsibility. A planner
can use the default Time till Requirement Date filter to determine the period in which
they wants to check for uncovered external requirements. Only one shortage defini-
tion is available for calculating uncovered requirements, and it is displayed for infor-
mation purposes. This app provides necessary information that helps materials plan-
ners react to shortage situations and fulfill customers’ demands on time.

from production orders, process orders, maintenance orders, and network orders. To
help you determine which receipts and requirements are included in the coverage cal-
culation, a shortage definition is available and displayed in the app. The app provides
all the necessary information to help you react quickly to critical situations. From this
app, you can directly navigate to the Manage Internal Requirements app to view details
of the selected materials and check different solutions for shortages. Choose any mate-
rial on the screen shown in Figure 21.6 to view the screen shown in Figure 21.7.
Figure 21.6 Monitor Internal Requirements App
The screen shown in Figure 21.7 has the look and feel of the stock/requirements list
(Transaction MD04). The bottom-right-hand corner of the screen offers the option to
trigger the Start MRP Run app (not shown), which will then plan the material again. You
can also use the Create Order option to create procurement elements, such as a
planned order for in-house production or a purchase requisition for external procure-
ment, as long as a source of supply is defined for the material. You can also choose to
edit an order, covert a planned order into a production or process order, or even delete
a planned order (PlnOrd).

21.2.4 Manage Production Orders App
With the Manage Production Orders app, you can manage those production orders that
have issues based on the settings made in the Monitor Production Orders app. A plan-
ner can view details regarding delayed materials and missing components, as well as
the status of operations and milestones. This app provides all the necessary informa-
tion for planners to react quickly to critical situations and avoid delays in production.
The Issues column shown in Figure 21.8 includes icons in each row for four important
categories: material shortage, scheduling delay, quality issues, and material delay.
For each category, if there’s an issue, the related icon turns red. In this situation, you
select an item and then choose Check Availability—for example, to view the number of
missing components required to produce a product. Choosing a production order and
then clicking the Details button (the forward arrow > located on the far-right-hand side
of the screen) will bring up a detailed screen that you can used not only to evaluate the
current status of an order but also to perform several important business functions.
Some of the functions are changing an order, confirming an order, confirming an oper-
ation, and setting an order to the technically complete status. The mini charts at the
top of the screen provide a summary of the status of various process orders, and you
can click them to get greater insights into the issues. This is shown in Figure 21.9.
Figure 21.9 shows the detailed screen of the Manage Production Order app. Notice the
same issues that were previously shown in the overview screen. Here again, a user can
scroll down and not just evaluate the details of a production order (such as compo-
nents, operations, and order schedule) but also perform business functions such as
confirming a production order. (Various tabs in Figure 21.9 facilitate this.)

21.2.5 Display MRP Master Data Issues App
The Display MRP Master Data Issues app provides details of the materials in a specific
plant for which an issue occurred in the last MRP run. You can display both types of
information—on materials for which messages were created in the MRP run and on
those that may not have been planned at all. Information about the responsible MRP
controller and the date and time that the last planning run was carried out is also
displayed. After you address the issues faced during materials planning, you can plan

materials correctly in future planning runs. Figure 21.10 shows the overview of the
issues faced in planning for several materials.
Figure 21.10 Display MRP Master Data Issues App
21.2.6 Scrap Reason App
The Scrap Reason app alerts users based on KPIs and enables a step-by-step data analy-
sis from different perspectives to identify the root causes of scrap in the production
process. The app shows information on the distribution of actual scrap recorded in pro-
duction confirmations in the dimensions of time, work center, material, plant, and rea-
son for variance. As a production planner, your ability to analyze the confirmed scrap
and rework makes it possible for you to work on the reasons or root causes of the great-
est loss of production, which will help you prevent problems in the future (see Figure
21.11).
You should be sure to check out more display options from the Yield, Scrap, and
Rework % by Entry Time (Line Chart) dropdown list. You can also change the chart
types—for example, from the horizontal bars shown to a pie chart—by selecting one of
the chart options available in the top right-hand corner of the screen. You can also dis-
play the data in table form.

21.2.7 Operation Scrap App
Figure 21.12 shows the Operation Scrap app, which enables a planner to compare actual
scrap figures recorded in the production confirmations with the expected scrap that
was defined in the order operation. It allows the planner to implement a feedback loop
for updating the expected scrap percentage in the order operation. The app shows the
work centers where the expected scrap percentage most differs from the actual scrap
percentage.
Figure 21.12 Operations Scrap App

21.2.8 Reprocess Failed Material Movements App
The Reprocess Failed Material Movements app enables you to view and process unsuc-
cessful materials movements, and it also provides the reasons why these goods move-
ments failed. From this app, you can navigate to the root cause of the problem, such as a
missing storage location or an incorrect posting period, and then correct the problem.
To correct a failed material movement issue, you select a material and choose Reprocess.
Figure 21.13 shows the SAP Fiori version of Transaction COGI (Reprocess Failed Material
Movements), which we covered in Chapter 6, Section 6.9.1. Here, it shows that a few
goods movements failed due to one issue or another.
Figure 21.13 Reprocess Failed Material Movements App
21.2.9 Manage Work Center Capacity App
With the Manage Work Center Capacity app (see Figure 21.14), a planner can visualize
the capacities of work centers and the load on work centers. The app allows a planner to
detect overload situations, along with the cumulative overload situation across the
selected evaluation horizons based on bucket (or grouping) types, such as days or
weeks. For a work center, several factors have to be balanced to ensure an optimal utili-
zation of capacity such that there are no overloads across the evaluation horizon, as
well as no backlog in terms of orders or operations. With the information provided in
the app, a planner can make informed decisions to achieve this balance.
It’s also possible to evaluate critical and overload situations based on priority, using
KPIs such as first critical load, first overload, and maximum load. These critical and
©

normal load levels can be personalized as per the requirement that anything above
critical will be considered an overload.
Figure 21.14 Manage Work Center Capacity App
With the app, a planner can view the capacity required to fulfill all the open orders, such
as planned, production, and process orders, in a set evaluation horizon. The planner
chooses a work center and clicks the forward arrow (>) located on far right-hand side of
the screen, and the screen shown in Figure 21.15 appears.
Figure 21.15 Work Center Capacity

With the Manage Work Center Capacity app, a planner can get details of the capacity sit-
uation a of work center and the loads on the work center. The app offers the ability to
detect overload situations across select evaluation horizons based on bucket (or group-
ing) types, such as days or weeks. For a work center, a planner attempts to balance sev-
eral factors to ensure an optimal utilization of capacity so that there are no overloads
across the evaluation horizon, as well as no backlog in terms of orders or operations.
With the information provided in the app, a planner can make informed decisions to
achieve this balance (see Figure 21.15).

21.2.11 Manage Production Version App
A production version is the link between the BOM of a product and its routing, master
recipe, or rate routing. It determines which BOM relates to the relevant routing or mas-
ter recipe to produce a material or plan a material. There may be different production
versions based on the lot sizes and validity dates. With the Manage Production Version
app, a user can create, edit, copy, and delete various production versions. Figure 21.18
shows the SAP Fiori version of the Transaction C223 (Manage Production Version)
screen covered in Chapter 6, Section 6.2.5.
Figure 21.18 Manage Production Version App

21 SAP Fiori Apps for Production Planning

21.2.12 Capacity Scheduling Board App
With the Capacity Scheduling Board app, a production planner can preschedule the
orders to be dispatched from a work center based on their priority. A planner can select
the source of supply (in the production version) and the work centers to dispatch the
orders on the chosen dates. For ease of selection, the app highlights a work center with
a capacity duration that meets the capacity required to complete the operation. The
app also uses midpoint scheduling to adjust the operational time (of dispatch, resched-
ule, etc.), according to the strategy that has been set. The production versions can be
switched when the work center capacity is insufficient, and orders can be rescheduled
if they overlap in a time slot (see Figure 21.19).
Figure 21.19 Capacity Scheduling Board
21.2.13 Kanban Board App
The Kanban Board app that we discussed in detail in Chapter 9 is a tool that a planner
uses to monitor circulating kanban containers and evaluations, and it provides an
overview of work progress and material consumption. The Kanban board presents a
clear picture of missing part situations and bottlenecks, for example, for every supply
area. For this purpose, the statuses of the kanban containers and the error situations
are represented by different colors. The Kanban board can also be used to trigger the
Kanban signal (see Figure 21.20).

21.2.14 Stock—Single and Multiple Materials App
With the Stock—Single Material app, a planner and an inventory controller can get an
overview of the material stock. Stock can be reviewed by the plants and storage loca-
tions for which a planner is responsible, and the app can display the stock information
as a table or a diagram.
The app also features a comparison of the stock information with the safety stock and
minimum safety stock values defined for the material. Furthermore, the detailed stock
history for each plant or storage location for the last 11 periods (at period-end closing)
and the stock balance at the reporting date can be viewed simply by clicking the graph
located in the lower-right-hand corner of the screen (see Figure 21.21).
Figure 21.21 Stock—Single Material App

21 SAP Fiori Apps for Production Planning
1054
You can switch to the graphical representation (see Figure 21.22) by clicking the Chart
View icon located in the top-right-hand corner of the screen. This lets you not only view
various stock types but also perform the desired transactions from the options located
at the bottom of the screen.
Figure 21.22 Graphical View of Stock—Single Material App
Note
You must meet the prerequisites listed in SAP Notes 2404856 and 2694741 before you
can use either of these apps.
Planners can use the Stock—Multiple Materials app to get an overview of material
stocks. Planners can review stock for the plants and storage locations for which they or
an inventory controller may be responsible. The app can also display the stock infor-
mation as a table.
The app calculates the stock values based on the stock quantity of the selected report-
ing date multiplied by the current material price. This means that the app always
makes its calculations using the current material price, irrespective of the reporting
date that was selected.
The result of a calculation method can be displayed based on the historical prices at the
time of the related material postings. SAP recommends using the main calculation
method based on the current material price, because this method leads to faster results
and is optimized for stock analysis (see Figure 21.23).

monthly periods in the past.

21.2.21 Capacity Scheduling Table App
With the Capacity Scheduling Table app shown in Figure 21.32, you as a planner can pre-

You can also select the source of supply (in the production version) and the work cen-
ters to dispatch the orders on the dates that you choose. For ease of selection, the app
highlights a work center with a capacity duration that meets the capacity required to
complete the operation. The app also uses midpoint scheduling to adjust the opera-
tional time (of dispatch, reschedule, and so on), according to the strategy that you have
set. You can switch production versions when the work center capacity is insufficient
or reschedule orders that coincide in a time slot.

egy (finite or infinite scheduling) and direction of orders.

work center capacity will suffice.
 Set the maximum number of days (up to 30 days) possible to schedule orders in the
past.
 Execute mass dispatch or deallocation of order operations.
 Filter and resolve late or overdue orders.
 Realign scheduled orders to a past or a future date and time.

ticular order is delayed, you can navigate to the details to see at a glance why the order
is delayed—perhaps there are missing components or there is a quality deviation. You

between phases, and other operations linked to the process order. If a particular opera-

delayed operation. For example, there may be missing components, the resource may

There, you can see details related to confirmation of the processing status of process

you can do with this app are as follows:
 View the details of all the operations involved in a process order.

21.2.24 Production Execution Duration App
With the Production Execution Duration app shown in Figure 21.36, you as a planner
can compare the planned and actual operation durations required for execution.
Figure 21.36 Production Execution Duration App

21 SAP Fiori Apps for Production Planning
1064
The planned operation durations are defined in the manufacturing order and are calcu-
lated using the durations defined in the routing or master recipe. The time that was
actually required for execution is recorded in the production confirmations. Using this
app, you can easily compare these values. You can perform a step-by-step analysis for a
specific time period and continuously fine-tune the production facility.
21.3 Summary
This chapter covered how to leverage some of the next-generation SAP Fiori apps for
PP. The information covered in this chapter can certainly help a planner use the same
reporting tools and features in other logistics and supply chain components of an SAP
S/4HANA system.
To conclude, PP in the SAP S/4HANA system is quite comprehensive and offers in-
depth and practical solutions for a large number of business processes and scenarios in
a diverse range of industries. It also offers options to implement and integrate PP in all
important business processes. However, your company can only ensure an actual
return on its investment in SAP S/4HANA system implementation by striving to
understand the integration of various SAP S/4HANA system areas with each other. You
can achieve flawless integration during an SAP S/4HANA system implementation by
maintaining extensive collaboration and intense coordination with the various stake-
holders of your company. In addition, those who work in supply chain management
roles have seen how the PP function is vital to many processes in the logistics and sup-
ply chain.
